genuine_levers: Na/RX (Williamson) = SN2 on primary R'X; conc. H2SO4/heat = intermolecular dehydration (SN2) for symmetrical ethers; acid + tertiary alcohol gives alkene (E1), NOT ether
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the SN2 step occurs at a stereocenter (inversion of configuration); if the alkyl halide (R'X) is chiral, the ether product will have a new stereocenter
common_traps: Using tertiary alkyl halide in Williamson synthesis leads to elimination (E2), not substitution
Applying acid-catalyzed conditions to a tertiary alcohol yields alkene, not ether
Confusing Na/RX (ether synthesis) with NaBH4 (reduction)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (condition discrimination: Williamson SN2 vs acid-catalyzed dehydration vs elimination)

edges.conditions: RT; anhydrous CH₂Cl₂ solvent; PCC or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ (Jones reagent, aqueous). Secondary alcohol → ketone. PCC in CH₂Cl₂: mild, anhydrous, stops at ketone. Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone): secondary alcohol → ketone. Swern oxidation (–78°C): secondary → ketone. Oppenauer oxidation (Al-alkoxide/ketone, mild): secondary → ketone.

reagents: or PDC, Collins, Corey, Swern, Moffatt, Jones
reagent_1.conditions: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) in CH₂Cl₂; RT or mild Swern (oxalyl chloride/DMSO/Et₃N, –78°C); or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ (Jones, in acetone). 2° alcohol → ketone. PCC: mild, anhydrous, selective. Jones: aqueous, orange CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone. Oppenauer (Al-t-BuO/cyclohexanone, RT): 2° alcohol → ketone. All stop
reagent_1.note: Secondary alcohol → ketone: propan-2-ol (isopropanol, 2° alcohol) + PCC (or PDC, Collins, Corey, Swern, Moffatt, Jones, X₂, NBS, Cu/300°C) → propan-2-one (acetone, ketone); GENERAL: any 2° alcohol + any oxidant from the selective list → ketone; ALL oxidants (both selective and strong) oxidize 2° alcohol → ketone; KETONE CANNOT BE FURTHER OXIDIZED under normal conditions (no α-H? then completely resistant; even strong KMnO₄/H+ gives no rxn with 2° ketone product under normal conditions); under VERY HIGH TEMPERATURE and STRONG OA (hot KMnO₄, HNO₃, K₂Cr₂O₇/Δ): ketone undergoes Popoff's Rule cleavage → two carboxylic acids (C-C bonds alpha to C=O are cleaved; RCOOH formed from each fragment); TERTIARY (3°) → always NO REACTION under all standard selective oxidants

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Secondary alcohol → ketone: propan-2-ol (isopropanol, 2° alcohol) + PCC (or PDC, Collins, Corey, Swern, Moffatt, Jones, X₂, NBS, Cu/300°C) → propan-2-one (acetone, ketone); GENERAL: any 2° alcohol + any oxidant from the selective list → ketone; ALL oxidants (both selective and strong) oxidize 2° alcohol → ketone; KETONE CANNOT BE FURTHER OXIDIZED under normal conditions (no α-H? then completely resistant; even strong KMnO₄/H+ gives no rxn with 2° ketone product under normal conditions); under VERY HIGH TEMPERATURE and STRONG OA (hot KMnO₄, HNO₃, K₂Cr₂O₇/Δ): ketone undergoes Popoff's Rule cleavage → two carboxylic acids (C-C bonds alpha to C=O are cleaved; RCOOH formed from each fragment); TERTIARY (3°) → always NO REACTION under all standard selective oxidants

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice for secondary alcohol: PCC, Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄), Swern, or Oppenauer all give ketone; primary alcohol with PCC gives aldehyde, not ketone — substrate identity is a genuine lever
common_traps: PCC does NOT oxidise tertiary alcohols — if starting material is tertiary, this step gives no reaction
PCC does NOT oxidise primary alcohols to ketones (stops at aldehyde) — misidentifying primary alcohol as secondary is a trap
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: PCC vs KMnO4 vs Jones for primary vs secondary alcohols)

edges.exception_description: Aldehydes oxidised by KMnO₄ → PCC oxidises 1° alcohol to aldehyde and stops there; KMnO₄ goes all the way to carboxylic acid | Corey_House_gives_only_cross_product_clean → Exception to the standard rule: Corey house gives only cross product clean. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | PCC_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_carboxylic_acid_like_Jones → Exception to the standard rule: Pcc oxidizes primary alcohol to carboxylic acid like jones. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | MnO2_oxidizes_all_alcohols_like_PCC → Exception to the standard rule: Mno2 oxidizes all alcohols like pcc. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Choosing wrong oxidant for target oxidation level

edges.conditions: 0°C to room temperature
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: Choice of sulfonyl chloride: TsCl gives tosylate (OTs), MsCl gives mesylate (OMs), Tf2O gives triflate (OTf) — different leaving group abilities
Pyridine acts as base to scavenge HCl; other bases (e.g., Et3N) can be used but pyridine is standard
Reaction with a chiral alcohol proceeds with retention of configuration at the carbon — stereochemistry is preserved
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alcohol is chiral; the reaction proceeds with retention of configuration, so it does not create new stereocenters but preserves existing ones
common_traps: Confusing this with an EAS reaction — the note about 'EAS' and 'regiochemistry' is a trap; this is a nucleophilic substitution on sulfur, not electrophilic aromatic substitution on the benzene ring
Thinking the reaction inverts stereochemistry — it proceeds with retention via a two-step mechanism (formation of a sulfonate ester)
Using TsCl without a base leads to poor yield due to HCl buildup
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the reaction as a functional group transformation to create a good leaving group, not an EAS); Structural (if the alcohol is chiral, stereochemistry is a key point)

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; <110°C (<383K); direct esterification of OH by H₂SO₄. Product: alkyl hydrogen sulphate (monoester of H₂SO₄, R-OSO₃H). No dehydration at this low temperature. Reversible on warming. Used industrially (e.g., ethyl sulphate from ethanol + H₂SO₄ cold). At higher T: ether (110–170°C) → alkene (>170°C).

reagent_1.conditions: 0°C to room temperature, non-aqueous solvent (DCM)
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: -78°C to 0°C, anhydrous conditions
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Temperature is a genuine lever: <110°C gives sulfonate; 110–170°C gives ether; >170°C gives alkene (dehydration)
Reagent identity: conc. H₂SO₄ directly esterifies OH; other sulfonyl chlorides (TsCl, MsCl) with base give different sulfonate esters (ROTs, ROMs)
common_traps: Confusing this low-T sulfonate formation with high-T dehydration to alkene
Assuming the product is a symmetrical sulfate ester (R-O-SO₂-O-R) instead of the alkyl hydrogen sulfate (R-OSO₃H)
Forgetting the reaction is reversible on warming
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature control of product: sulfonate vs ether vs alkene)

edges.conditions: RT; dry HCl or p-TsOH (acid catalyst); excess dry alcohol (2 equiv ROH); anhydrous conditions (remove H₂O to drive equilibrium). Mechanism: H⁺ protonates C=O → oxocarbenium → first ROH attack → hemiacetal → protonation of OH → loss of H₂O → oxocarbenium → 2nd ROH → acetal. Reverse (hydrolysis) occurs in aqueous acid. Acetals are stable to base; used as protecting groups.
edges.notes: Protecting group strategy: acetal stable to base/LiAlH₄; removed by dilute acid. Ensure the protecting group is compatible with all subsequent steps.

genuine_levers: Choice of diol (ROH) determines acetal structure; ethylene glycol gives cyclic acetal, methanol gives dimethyl acetal
Anhydrous conditions are essential for formation; water presence drives equilibrium toward hydrolysis back to aldehyde
common_traps: Students forget the acid catalyst (dry HCl/p-TsOH) is required for both formation AND hydrolysis
Mistaking acetal for stable under acidic conditions — it is stable to base but hydrolyzes in aqueous acid
Assuming the reaction is irreversible; it is an equilibrium driven by removal of water
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (protecting group logic: acetal stable to base, labile to acid; mechanism reversibility)

edges.conditions: RT; protic solvent (EtOH or EtOH/H₂O); NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride) — mild, safe, selective. Alternative: LiAlH₄ (dry ether, anhydrous, more powerful). NaBH₄: reduces aldehydes and ketones but NOT esters, acids, amides. LiAlH₄: reduces all C=O including esters, acids, amides. Mechanism: H⁻ delivered from B or Al to electrophilic carbonyl C → alkoxide → protonation during workup.

reagent_1.conditions: 0–5°C; dilute NaOH (5–10% aq.); cold conditions (aldol condensation: cold=aldol addition product; hot=dehydrated enone). Mechanism: OH⁻ deprotonates α-H → enolate → attacks C=O of 2nd aldehyde → β-hydroxy aldehyde (aldol). Cold conditions stop at addition; heat → dehydration → α,β-unsaturated aldehy
reagent_1.note: Aldol addition (cold) vs condensation (warm): specify temperature. Crossed aldol requires careful substrate choice to avoid four-product mixture.

reagent_2.conditions: NaBH₄; EtOH or EtOH/H₂O; RT. Mild selective reduction: aldehyde → primary alcohol. NaBH₄ selective (reduces C=O not C=C). H⁻ delivers to aldehyde C → primary alcohol. Very mild: NaBH₄ + EtOH, RT, 30 min → full conversion. Workup: dilute acid or water. Alternatively: LiAlH₄/dry ether (more reactive).
reagent_2.note: LAH (or NaBH₄) reduction of aldehyde → primary alcohol: propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) + LiAlH₄ → propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH); GENERAL: R-CHO + LiAlH₄ → R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol, 1°); ALSO WORKS WITH NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride), H₂/Pt, H₂/Pd, H₂/Ni; MECHANISM: H⁻ from AlH₄⁻ attacks the electrophilic C of C=O (aldehyde carbon is more electrophilic than ketone due to less steric/electronic donation from R) → alkoxide intermediate R-CH₂-O⁻ (coordinated to Al) → H₂O workup → R-CH₂-OH; ALDEHYDE REDUCTION PRODUCTS: always primary alcohol (1°); KETONE REDUCTION: always secondary alcohol (2°); SELECTIVITY: LiAlH₄ reduces ALL carbonyls (aldehyde, ketone, ester, COOH, CONH₂, C≡N, C=N); NaBH₄ is SELECTIVE (reduces aldehyde and ketone ONLY; does NOT reduce ester, COOH, amide, nitrile — too mild for these)

reagent_3.conditions: anhydrous ether solvent; addition of R⁻ (carbanion character) to C=O of HCHO → R-CH₂-OMgX → H₃O⁺ → R-CH₂-OH; adds exactly 1 carbon
reagent_3.note: Grignard + different carbonyls: HCHO → 1° alcohol (+1C); RCHO → 2° alcohol (+1C at α); R₂C=O → 3° alcohol (+1C at α); RMgX + epoxide → 1° alcohol (+2C via ring-opening at less hindered C. RMgX + O₂ → R-OOMgX → H₂O → R-OH (peroxide intermediate → alcohol). Use Grignard to homologate: each addition increases carbon count.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Aldol addition (cold) vs condensation (warm): specify temperature. Crossed aldol requires careful substrate choice to avoid four-product mixture. | LAH (or NaBH₄) reduction of aldehyde → primary alcohol: propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) + LiAlH₄ → propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH); GENERAL: R-CHO + LiAlH₄ → R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol, 1°); ALSO WORKS WITH NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride), H₂/Pt, H₂/Pd, H₂/Ni; MECHANISM: H⁻ from AlH₄⁻ attacks the electrophilic C of C=O (aldehyde carbon is more electrophilic than ketone due to less steric/electronic donation from R) → alkoxide intermediate R-CH₂-O⁻ (coordinated to Al) → H₂O workup → R-CH₂-OH; ALDEHYDE REDUCTION PRODUCTS: always primary alcohol (1°); KETONE REDUCTION: always secondary alcohol (2°); SELECTIVITY: LiAlH₄ reduces ALL carbonyls (aldehyde, ketone, ester, COOH, CONH₂, C≡N, C=N); NaBH₄ is SELECTIVE (reduces aldehyde and ketone ONLY; does NOT reduce ester, COOH, amide, nitrile — too mild for these) | Grignard + different carbonyls: HCHO → 1° alcohol (+1C); RCHO → 2° alcohol (+1C at α); R₂C=O → 3° alcohol (+1C at α); RMgX + epoxide → 1° alcohol (+2C via ring-opening at less hindered C. RMgX + O₂ → R-OOMgX → H₂O → R-OH (peroxide intermediate → alcohol). Use Grignard to homologate: each addition increases carbon count.

genuine_levers: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄ selectivity: NaBH₄ reduces aldehydes/ketones only; LiAlH₄ reduces all C=O (esters, acids, amides) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the aldehyde is prochiral (RCHO where R ≠ H) and the reduction creates a chiral alcohol; achiral aldehydes (e.g., CH₃CHO) give achiral alcohols
common_traps: NaBH₄ does NOT reduce esters, carboxylic acids, or amides — a common JEE trap is to assume it reduces all carbonyls like LiAlH₄
NaBH₄ is used in protic solvent (EtOH/H₂O); LiAlH₄ requires anhydrous conditions (dry ether) — confusing the conditions leads to wrong product or no reaction
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄); Structural only if the product is genuinely chiral

edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | Aldol condensation requires base → Acid-catalysed aldol is also possible; intramolecular aldol (Dieckmann equivalent) can cyclise to form rings under both acid and base conditions | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_requires_only_1_alpha_H → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation requires only 1 alpha h. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_E2_mechanism → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation e2 mechanism. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Robinson_annulation_aldol_first_then_Michael → Exception to the standard rule: Robinson annulation aldol first then michael. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Dilute NaOH (5–10%); heat (Δ, 70–80°C); aqueous ethanol. Aldol condensation: ethanal (acetaldehyde) is both enolisable donor and electrophile acceptor. Step 1: OH⁻ + CH₃CHO → ⁻CH₂CHO (enolate) → + CH₃CHO → β-hydroxyaldehyde (aldol). Step 2: heat → dehydration → (E)-but-2-enal (crotonaldehyde, trans). Cold NaOH → aldol addition (β-hydroxy aldehyde); heat → condensation (dehydrated, conjugated).

reagent_1.conditions: NaOH (aq); RT. The Me₂N group (strong EDG) makes the ring strongly electron-rich; carbonyl is push-pull activated. NaOH treatment: OH⁻ adds to C=O → tetrahedral intermediate (zwitterion with N⁺ and O⁻ or negative charge delocalised). Quinoid zwitterion form: Me₂N⁺=Ar=CH-O⁻. This is why Meisenheimer-
reagent_1.note: 4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde (p-Me₂N-C₆H₄-CHO) + NaOH → does NOT give normal Cannizzaro products; instead forms a ZWITTERION (betaine); mechanism: OH⁻ attacks C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → the dimethylamino nitrogen becomes quaternary (N⁺) and the alkoxide (O⁻) remains → ZWITTERION (Me₂N⁺=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ quinoid/betaine form); this zwitterion is stable and does not undergo the normal Cannizzaro hydride transfer; RESULT: NO normal Cannizzaro products (no alcohol + acid); instead: zwitterionic intermediate is the final/stable product under basic conditions; similar behavior: p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (OH-Ph-CHO) + NaOH → quinoid zwitterion (⁻O=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ type); ALL aryl aldehydes with strong ELECTRON-DONATING groups in para position (p-NR₂, p-OH) → zwitterion exception to Cannizzaro; compare to EWG aryl aldehydes → deformylation exception

edges.notes: Self-aldol condensation of ethanal (acetaldehyde, CH3CHO): (1) OH- removes α-H from CH3CHO → enolate (-CH2CHO); (2) Enolate attacks C=O of another CH3CHO → β-hydroxy aldehyde (3-hydroxybutanal, CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO = aldol product); (3) E1CB: OH- removes α-H from the β-hydroxy intermediate → carbanion → β-OH leaves → forms C=C; product = (E)-but-2-enal (trans-crotonaldehyde, CH3-CH=CH-CHO); E-geometry preferred (trans more stable); CONDITION: ≥2 α-H needed for aldol condensation (1 for enolate, 1 for E1CB); RATE ∝ [CH3CHO][OH-]^1 (first order in each) | 4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde (p-Me₂N-C₆H₄-CHO) + NaOH → does NOT give normal Cannizzaro products; instead forms a ZWITTERION (betaine); mechanism: OH⁻ attacks C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → the dimethylamino nitrogen becomes quaternary (N⁺) and the alkoxide (O⁻) remains → ZWITTERION (Me₂N⁺=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ quinoid/betaine form); this zwitterion is stable and does not undergo the normal Cannizzaro hydride transfer; RESULT: NO normal Cannizzaro products (no alcohol + acid); instead: zwitterionic intermediate is the final/stable product under basic conditions; similar behavior: p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (OH-Ph-CHO) + NaOH → quinoid zwitterion (⁻O=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ type); ALL aryl aldehydes with strong ELECTRON-DONATING groups in para position (p-NR₂, p-OH) → zwitterion exception to Cannizzaro; compare to EWG aryl aldehydes → deformylation exception

genuine_levers: Dilute NaOH + heat → aldol condensation (dehydrated α,β-unsaturated aldehyde); Cold dilute NaOH → aldol addition (β-hydroxy aldehyde) — temperature is a genuine lever
Self-condensation of acetaldehyde vs. cross-aldol with a different carbonyl partner — substrate choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Forgetting that heat is required for the dehydration step to get the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde
Writing the product as the β-hydroxy aldehyde when the conditions specify heat
Mistaking this for Cannizzaro reaction (acetaldehyde has α-H, so it undergoes aldol, not Cannizzaro)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature control: addition vs. condensation); Structural (recognizing self-aldol product)

edges.exception_description: Aldol condensation requires base → Acid-catalysed aldol is also possible; intramolecular aldol (Dieckmann equivalent) can cyclise to form rings under both acid and base conditions | aldol_condensation_requires_only_1_alpha_H → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation requires only 1 alpha h. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_E2_mechanism → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation e2 mechanism. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | chloral_NaOH_gives_Cannizzaro_products → Exception to the standard rule: Chloral naoh gives cannizzaro products. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | EWG_on_benzaldehyde_ring_increases_Cannizzaro_rate → Exception to the standard rule: Ewg on benzaldehyde ring increases cannizzaro rate. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Cannizzaro_always_second_order → Exception to the standard rule: Cannizzaro always second order. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Tischenko_and_Cannizzaro_give_same_products → Exception to the standard rule: Tischenko and cannizzaro give same products. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Robinson_annulation_aldol_first_then_Michael → Exception to the standard rule: Robinson annulation aldol first then michael. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | all_no_alpha_H_aldehydes_give_Cannizzaro → Exception to the standard rule: All no alpha h aldehydes give cannizzaro. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_aldehyde_with_EWG_gives_faster_Cannizzaro → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl aldehyde with ewg gives faster cannizzaro. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkynyl_aldehyde_gives_Cannizzaro_no_alpha_H → Exception to the standard rule: Alkynyl aldehyde gives cannizzaro no alpha h. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | p_aminobenzaldehyde_gives_Cannizzaro_like_benzaldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: P aminobenzaldehyde gives cannizzaro like benzaldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Step 1: Reflux in acetic acid/HCl; Zn-Hg amalgam (Clemmensen reduction). Strongly acidic conditions (conc HCl). Mechanism uncertain but involves carbenoid/zinc-carbenoid intermediate on metal surface; not via alcohol intermediate. Aldehyde or ketone directly → CH₂ (methylene). Alternative: Wolff-Kishner (NH₂NH₂/KOH/EG, reflux 200°C) — for base-sensitive substrates avoid; acid-sensitive → use Clemmensen. Both go C=O → CH₂.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Clemmensen (acidic) vs Wolff-Kishner (basic) — both give alkane but choice depends on substrate acid/base sensitivity
Clemmensen reduces only C=O to CH2; Wolff-Kishner reduces both C=O and C=C (exception case)
common_traps: Assuming Clemmensen proceeds via alcohol intermediate (it does not; it's a direct reduction via carbenoid)
Confusing Wolff-Kishner as only reducing C=O (it reduces C=C as well, which is an exception)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent choice based on substrate sensitivity; exception case for Wolff-Kishner)

edges.exception_description: Wolff_Kishner_reduces_both_C=O_and_C=C → Exception to the standard rule: Wolff kishner reduces both c=o and c=c. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Reductive amination: Step 1 (imine formation): pH 4–5, RT or mild heat, NH₄OAc or NH₃/EtOH, –H₂O. Step 2 (reduction of imine): H₂/Pd (RT, 1 atm, EtOH) or NaBH₃CN (RT, MeOH, pH 6–7). NaBH₃CN is selective: reduces iminium (not carbonyls, unlike NaBH₄). Product: primary amine from aldehyde + NH₃; secondary amine from ketone + NH₃.

reagent_1.conditions: Reductive amination: Step 1 (imine): pH 4–5, RT, RNH₂ (primary amine) + RCHO/R₂CO, –H₂O. Step 2: NaBH₃CN (pH 6, MeOH) or H₂/Pd (1 atm, EtOH). Product: secondary amine (one new N-C bond). Catalytic hydrogenation at RT; NaBH₃CN is more selective at RT. Iminium ion (protonated imine) is the actual elec
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: Step 1: Dean-Stark, reflux toluene or benzene; 2° amine (R₂NH) + R₃CO/R₃CHO → enamine (R₃NH + ketone → iminium → deprotonation at α-C → enamine). Step 2: reduce enamine (H₂/Pd or NaBH₃CN, pH 6). Product: tertiary amine. Enamines are also useful as nucleophilic enol equivalents (alkylation then hydro
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: NaBH3CN reduces imine/iminium selectively at pH 6-7, leaving other carbonyls intact; NaBH4 would reduce the original aldehyde.
Ammonia source: NH3 yields a primary amine; using a primary amine (RNH2) yields a secondary amine.
pH control: Imine formation requires mildly acidic conditions (pH 4-5); NaBH3CN reduction works at pH 6-7 to avoid decomposition.
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting aldehyde is chiral (alpha-chiral aldehyde) or if the imine intermediate is prochiral; reduction of the imine creates a new stereocenter at the former carbonyl carbon.
common_traps: Using NaBH4 instead of NaBH3CN for reduction — NaBH4 would reduce the original aldehyde to an alcohol, not the imine to an amine.
Assuming the reaction gives a tertiary amine — reductive amination with NH3 stops at primary amine; a secondary amine source is needed for a secondary amine product.
Forgetting the two-step sequence (imine formation then reduction) and drawing a direct addition product.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH3CN vs NaBH4; mechanism/step sequence); Structural (if a chiral amine product is formed)

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; Tollens' reagent ([Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺, alkaline) → silver mirror (RT); Fehling's (Cu²⁺/tartrate, alkaline, heat) → Cu₂O brick-red precipitate; KMnO₄/H⁺ or K₂Cr₂O₇/H⁺ (acidic, RT to reflux). Benedict's: similar to Fehling's but citrate buffer (milder). Aldehydes oxidised; ketones NOT oxidised by Tollens'/Fehling's (except α-hydroxy ketones). Formic acid gives positive Tollens' (can be oxidised to CO₂).

reagent_1.conditions: Perkin condensation: acetic anhydride (Ac₂O, 3 equiv); potassium acetate (CH₃COOK) or sodium acetate (base/catalyst); heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Mechanism: CH₃COO⁻ deprotonates Ac₂O → enolate → nucleophilic addition to PhCHO → aldol-like → acylated β-hydroxy → elimination → PhCH=CH-COOH (cinnamic acid, tr
reagent_1.note: Perkin reaction: benzaldehyde + acetic anhydride + sodium acetate/Δ → (E)-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid (cinnamic acid, PhCH=CH-COOH); PERKIN REACTION ONLY FOR AROMATIC ALDEHYDES AND KETONES (not aliphatic); mechanism: (1) CH3COO- (sodium acetate, base) removes α-H from acetic anhydride → enolate -CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3; (2) Enolate attacks PhCHO → β-hydroxy adduct Ph-CH(OH)-CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3; (3) OH is acetylated by the second acetyl group → Ph-CH(OAc)-CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3 type; (4) E1CB or heat: loss of AcOH → Ph-CH=CH-CO-O-CO-CH3 (unsaturated anhydride); (5) H3O+/Δ: hydrolysis → PhCH=CH-COOH (cinnamic acid, E geometry); with propionic anhydride (CH3CH2CO)2O + AcONa → Ph-CH=C(CH3)-COOH (alpha-methylcinnamic acid)

reagent_2.conditions: Perkin condensation: Ac₂O + CH₃COONa; heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Same as benzaldehyde case but EWG (NO₂) on ring makes aldehyde more electrophilic → faster condensation. Product: (E)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)prop-2-enoic acid (4-nitrocinnamic acid). Perkin works best for aldehydes lacking α-H (ArCHO) because self
reagent_2.note: Perkin reaction Q2: 4-nitrobenzaldehyde (O2N-Ph-CHO) + acetic anhydride (Ac2O) + sodium acetate (AcO-Na+) → H3O+/Δ → (E)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)prop-2-enoic acid (4-nitrocinnamic acid); same Perkin mechanism as benzaldehyde; note that the EWG (NO2) activates the aromatic aldehyde C=O toward nucleophilic attack by the anhydride enolate → may give SLIGHTLY FASTER rate for the addition step but overall Cannizzaro rate is DECREASED by EWG (opposite for Perkin: EWG activates aldehyde toward enolate addition); Perkin works for all aromatic aldehydes (EWG or EDG on ring); product = always E (trans) geometry (more stable, thermodynamic)

reagent_3.conditions: Perkin with propionic anhydride: (CH₃CH₂CO)₂O + CH₃CH₂COONa (sodium propanoate); heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Propionic anhydride enolate attacks PhCHO → β-hydroxy acyl → elimination → α-methylcinnamic acid. α-Methyl substituent from propionic anhydride. E-isomer (trans) thermodynamically preferred.
reagent_3.note: Perkin reaction with propionic anhydride (propanoic anhydride): PhCHO + (CH3CH2CO)2O + CH3CH2COO-Na+ → H3O+/Δ → (E)-2-methyl-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid (alpha-methylcinnamic acid, Ph-CH=C(CH3)-COOH); the alpha-carbon of the anhydride (CH3-CH2 in propionic anhydride → the CH2 is the alpha-carbon) gets incorporated into the product; the alpha-substituent (CH3 from propionyl) ends up at the alpha-carbon of the product acid; GENERAL RULE: Perkin with (RCO)2O → Ar-CH=C(R)-COOH (R = rest of acyl group, one carbon shorter); for Ac2O (R=CH3) → one α-carbon, no substituent (R=H effectively) → Ar-CH=CH-COOH; for Pr2O (R=CH2CH3) → Ar-CH=C(CH3)-COOH

reagents: or K2Cr2O7/H+, HNO3, Jones reagent, H2CrO4, Tollens, Fehling (for aliphatic only), m-CPBA, H2O2, O3
reagent_4.conditions: KMnO₄/H⁺ or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ (Jones) or Tollens'/Fehling's + oxidation. Aldehyde → carboxylic acid (2e⁻ oxidation). Mild oxidants: Tollens' gives silver mirror + carboxylate. Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone): RCHO → RCOOH cleanly. KMnO₄/acidic: RCHO → RCOOH. PCC does NOT oxidise aldehydes to acids (stops at
reagent_4.note: Aldehyde oxidation to carboxylic acid: propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) + KMnO₄/H+ → propanoic acid (CH₃CH₂COOH); COMPLETE LIST of reagents that oxidize R-CHO → RCOOH: (1) KMnO₄/H+ (acidic, hot); (2) K₂Cr₂O₇/H+; (3) HNO₃; (4) Jones reagent (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/H₂O); (5) H₂CrO₄; (6) Mn⁷+ neutral; (7) Cr⁶+ neutral; (8) O₃ (ozonolysis); (9) H₂O₂; (10) m-CPBA (meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid); (11) Tollens (ALL aldehydes); (12) Fehling's (ALIPHATIC aldehydes only; Ar-CHO = benzaldehyde does NOT give Fehling positive); SELECTIVE REAGENTS for aldehyde (do NOT oxidize primary alcohol, only aldehyde): {O₃, H₂O₂, m-CPBA, Tollens, Fehling's} — these reagents oxidize R-CHO → RCOOH but do NOT oxidize R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol); NON-SELECTIVE: KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, HNO₃, Jones — these oxidize BOTH primary alcohol AND aldehyde to RCOOH
