RESONANCE > SAYTZEFF — SPECIFIC EXAMPLE WITH ALLYLIC Br (Q1, RM lec8):

SUBSTRATE: Cyclohexene ring (one existing C=C in ring) with Br at the allylic position (C adjacent to the ring C=C). + alc. KOH/Δ.

TWO POSSIBLE E2 PRODUCTS:
(A) Eliminate toward the ring C=C side → new C=C conjugates with exist
reagent_3.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_4.conditions:
E1 (UNIMOLECULAR ELIMINATION) — FORMAL INTRODUCTION (RM lec9, page 6):

NAME: E1 = Elimination, Unimolecular. Teacher label: 'E1 (Unimolecular elimination rxn) ⟹' (circled #, underlined, in green = important).

CONDITIONS:
- Polar & protic solvent (water, aqueous alcohol, ROH)
- Heat (Δ) = provides
reagent_4.note: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product. | Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

genuine_levers: alc. KOH = E2 elimination → alkene (Zaitsev product unless bulky base)
aq. KOH = SN2 substitution → alcohol
common_traps: alc. KOH gives alkene, NOT carbocation — carbocation is only a transient intermediate in E1
E2 is anti-periplanar: if substrate is cyclic, product geometry is constrained
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (alc. vs aq. KOH discrimination is a classic JEE lever)

edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.

edges.conditions: Reflux; dry acetone solvent; NaI (sodium iodide). Equilibrium driven right because NaCl or NaBr is insoluble in acetone and precipitates. SN2 mechanism (inversion at C). Acetone must be anhydrous. Used to convert R-Cl or R-Br to R-I for subsequent reactions requiring better leaving group. Rate: primary > secondary >> tertiary.

reagent_1.conditions: Finkelstein reaction: NaI; dry acetone; reflux. NaCl or NaBr (products) insoluble in acetone → precipitate → drives equilibrium toward R-I. SN2 mechanism (inversion). Primary > secondary >> tertiary. Must use DRY acetone (NaI slightly soluble in wet acetone → equilibrium not driven). Used to convert
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagents: AgF or Hg2F2 or CaF2 or CoF3 or SbF3 / heat
reagent_2.conditions: Halide exchange with fluoride source: AgF, Hg₂F₂, CaF₂ (industrial) or SbF₃ (antimony trifluoride, Swarts reaction); heat (Δ). Swarts reaction: RCCl₃ + SbF₃ → RCCF₃ (trifluoromethyl). AgF: SN2 on primary. SbF₃/SbCl₅: industrial halogen exchange. Ionic: F⁻ poor nucleophile in polar protic; use dry po
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagents: Cl2/hv (progressive chlorination of CH4), or 2[H] (reductive path)
reagent_3.conditions: Progressive chlorination of CH₄: Cl₂/hν, stepwise radical chlorination CH₄→CH₃Cl→CH₂Cl₂→CHCl₃→CCl₄ — stopping at CHCl₃ stage by controlling Cl₂:CH₄ ratio. OR: 2[H] reductive path: CCl₄ + Fe/H₂O → CHCl₃ + HCl. Industrial: chlorination of methane at 400°C with excess methane → CHCl₃. Lab: reductive me
reagent_3.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_4.conditions: CHCl₃ + conc HNO₃; heat. HNO₃ oxidises CHCl₃ → CCl₃NO₂ (trichloronitromethane, chloropicrin). Lachrymatory, used as pesticide. Mechanism: electrophilic nitration of C-H followed by oxidation, or direct reaction with N₂O₅/NO₂. Also: CCl₃CHO + conc HNO₃ → CCl₃NO₂ + CO₂ + H₂O.
reagent_4.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_5.conditions: Acetone (SPECIFICALLY — not DMF, DMSO, or other polar aprotic). DRIVING FORCE: NaCl and NaBr are INSOLUBLE in acetone (precipitate out) while NaI is SOLUBLE in acetone. Precipitation of NaCl/NaBr by Le Chatelier drives equilibrium toward R-I. Room temperature to mild heating.
reagent_5.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_6.conditions: NORBORNYL (BICYCLO[2.2.1]HEPTYL) SIGMA-NGP: The exo-norbornyl tosylate reacts 140,000 times FASTER than endo-norbornyl tosylate in AcOH. REASON: The C1-C6 SIGMA BOND (the C-C bond of the norbornane bridge) is ANTI to the exo-OTs leaving group (backside geometry) → C1-C6 bond does sigma-NGP (intramol
reagent_6.note: Archetype ['III', 'IV'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_7.conditions:
GAMMA-ELIMINATION (1,3-ELIMINATION) WITH Zn-Cu → CYCLOPROPANE (RM lec 11, page 2):

NAME: gamma-elimination = 1,3-elimination = elimination of two groups on C1 and C3 to form a 3-membered ring.
Contrast: beta-elimination = 1,2-elimination = groups on C1 and C2 → alkene.

TEACHER HEADER (page 2): 'β-
reagent_7.note: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

reagent_8.conditions: SN2 reaction conditions: nucleophile (e.g., NaOH/H₂O → alcohol intermediate → then re-bromination? Or: directly via SN2 with Br⁻ after inversion). Actually: inversion of configuration at SN2 centre. (R)-2-bromobutane + Br⁻ (Finkelstein-type: NaBr/acetone or label exchange) → (S)-2-bromobutane (net i
reagent_8.note: SN2 at chiral centre → Walden inversion → configuration flips R→S (or S→R); GENERAL: SN2 at (R)-substrate → (S)-product (inversion); CIP R/S ASSIGNMENT PROCEDURE: (1) assign priorities a>b>c>d to 4 substituents using CIP Sequence Rules; (2) orient molecule so group d (LOWEST priority) points AWAY from viewer (into page, dash bond); (3) count a→b→c: CLOCKWISE = R; ANTI-CLOCKWISE = S; (4) if d is pointing TOWARD viewer (wedge), do step 3 then REVERSE (R↔S); CIP SEQUENCE RULE 1 (most used): higher atomic number (Z) = higher priority; tie → go to next attached atoms (outward exploration); tie broken at FIRST point of difference; SEQUENCE RULE 2 (tie-breaker for isotopes): higher mass number = higher priority (²H > ¹H); SEQUENCE RULE 3-5: for multiple bonds and stereocentres, use phantom atom rules (see phantom_atom concept)

genuine_levers: NaI in dry acetone is an SN2 reaction — inversion of configuration at chiral centers
Primary halides react fastest; tertiary halides may not react (E2 with base or SN1 with nucleophile can compete)
The reaction is driven by precipitation of NaCl/NaBr — if solvent is not dry acetone, equilibrium may not shift
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alkyl halide is chiral; SN2 inverts configuration, so a new stereocenter is not created, but the existing one is inverted
common_traps: Assuming this is an SN1 reaction — it is not; NaI is a good nucleophile in aprotic solvent, favoring SN2
Forgetting that tertiary halides give elimination (E2) with NaI in acetone, not substitution
Mistaking this for Finkelstein reaction with KI in acetone — NaI is used similarly but note the sodium salt precipitation
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN2 vs SN1/E2; stereochemistry inversion)

edges.conditions:
ALKYNE SYNTHESIS BY DOUBLE ZN DEHALOGENATION (RM lec 11, page 6):

TEACHER Q18 (page 6): Br-chain-Br (dibromo or more) + Zn → 'n≡n' (= alkyne, H≡H type shown)

ROUTE:
  Option 1: 1,1,2,2-Tetrahalide (gem-gem dihalide on two adjacent C's) + 2Zn → alkyne
    R-CBr₂-CBr₂-R' + 2Zn → R-C≡C-R' + 2ZnBr₂
    Each Zn removes one pair of geminal Br's → generates one triple-bond component
    OR: removes both vicinal pairs in two steps
  Option 2: Sequential treatment
    Step 1: R-CBr₂-CBr₂-R' + Zn → R-CB
edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

genuine_levers: Requires a 1,1,2,2-tetrahalide (geminal dihalides on two adjacent carbons) as the substrate; vicinal dihalides (1,2-dihalides) do NOT give alkynes with Zn
Zn must be in excess (2 equivalents) and heated to drive the double dehalogenation
common_traps: Confusing this with the elimination of a simple vicinal dihalide (which gives an alkene, not an alkyne)
Assuming any dihalide with halogens on adjacent carbons will work (only the tetrahalide works)
Forgetting the heat requirement for the reaction to proceed
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate recognition: geminal vs. vicinal dihalides)

edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.

edges.conditions: Excess NH₃ (sealed vessel or pressure); heat; SN2. Primary alkyl halides preferred. Product mixture: R-X + NH₃ → R-NH₂ → R-NH₃⁺X⁻ (salt, then free with base). But R-NH₂ also attacks R-X → 2°, 3°, and quaternary ammonium salt. Use excess NH₃ to favour primary amine. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis gives pure primary amine.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Excess NH₃ favours primary amine; limited NH₃ leads to mixture of 1°, 2°, 3° amines and quaternary ammonium salt
Gabriel phthalimide synthesis gives pure primary amine, bypassing polyalkylation
SN2 mechanism: primary alkyl halides preferred; secondary gives elimination, tertiary fails
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide is chiral and the reaction proceeds via SN2 (inversion of configuration); if the halide is achiral, no stereocenters are created
common_traps: Forgetting that R-NH₂ is nucleophilic and can react with another R-X, leading to polyalkylation unless NH₃ is in excess
Applying this SN2 route to aryl/vinyl halides (which do not undergo SN2)
Confusing this with Gabriel synthesis, which is a distinct route for pure 1° amines
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent excess vs. stoichiometry; mechanism choice SN2 vs. elimination; alternative Gabriel synthesis)

edges.exception_description: Gabriel synthesis can make any amine → Gabriel synthesis cannot use ArX (no SN2 on aryl halides) and only gives 1° amines
edges.exception_trap: Students assume it is universal like reductive amination

edges.conditions:
1,7-ELIMINATION → POLYMERIZATION (NOT 7-MEMBERED RING):

FOR ALL 1,n-ELIMINATIONS:
n=3: 1,3-dihalo → cyclopropane (3-ring)
n=4: 1,4-dihalo → cyclobutane (4-ring)
n=5: 1,5-dihalo → cyclopentane (5-ring) [most favorable — cyclopentane is very stable]
n=6: 1,6-dihalo → cyclohexane (6-ring) [very favorable]
n=7: 1,7-dihalo → POLYMERIZATION [NOT 7-membered ring]

WHY 1,7 → POLYMERIZATION:
As n increases, the probability of the two reactive ends of the SAME molecule finding each other (intramolecular

reagent_1.conditions:
E1CB (UNIMOLECULAR ELIMINATION w.r.t. CONJUGATE BASE) — COMPLETE FORMAL INTRODUCTION (RM lec9, page 10):

TEACHER LABEL: '# E1CB (Unimolecular elimination rxn w.r.t - conjugate base)' (underlined)

NAME EXPLANATION:
  E1CB = Elimination (1) Conjugate Base
  'Unimolecular' refers to the RDS = Step 2
reagent_1.note: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice (Mg vs Zn) determines mechanism: Mg → Grignard (carbanion equivalent); Zn → Simmons-Smith carbenoid (cyclopropanation)
Chain length (n) in 1,n-dihaloalkane determines product: n=3-6 → cycloalkane; n=7+ → polymerization (intramolecular cyclization disfavored)
common_traps: Assuming 1,7-dihaloalkane gives a 7-membered ring — it polymerizes instead
Confusing Grignard formation (carbanion) with carbene formation (cyclopropanation) — reagent is key
Overlooking that 'any carbanion conditions' is a placeholder; specific reagent dictates product
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent mechanism discrimination: carbanion vs carbenoid); Structural (chain-length effect on intramolecular vs intermolecular reaction)

edges.conditions: Alcoholic KOH, heat (E2 elimination). Anti-periplanar geometry required. Follows Saytzeff rule (more substituted alkene preferred). Primary substrates may give some SN2. Distinguished from aq. KOH (SN2 → alcohol).

reagent_1.conditions: aq. KOH favours SN1 (dilute, polar protic, stabilises carbocation); conc. NaOH favours SN2 (concentrated nucleophile, primary substrate, less steric hinderance)
reagent_1.note: Primary R-X + conc. NaOH → SN2 → inversion at α-carbon. Tertiary R-X + aq. KOH → SN1 → carbocation → OH⁻ attack → racemisation. SN2: Walden inversion; SN1: racemic mixture. Key: aq/dilute = SN1; conc. = SN2.

edges.notes: alc. KOH → E2 → alkene (Saytzeff). aq. KOH → SN2 → alcohol. Key JEE distinction. Carbocation is the SN1/E1 intermediate for tertiary substrates in polar solvent without strong base — product is still alkene (E1) or alcohol (SN1), not isolated carbocation.

genuine_levers: Base size (small base like alc. KOH → Saytzeff; bulky base like t-BuOK → Hofmann)
Leaving group ability (good LG like I⁻ → Saytzeff; poor LG like F⁻ or NR₃⁺ → Hofmann)
Substrate geometry (acyclic → Saytzeff; rigid cyclic → anti-periplanar geometry overrides Saytzeff)
Resonance stabilization of a possible product (conjugated alkene → major, overriding substitution level)
common_traps: Assuming E2 always gives the more substituted (Saytzeff) alkene, ignoring anti-periplanar constraints in cyclic systems
Confusing alc. KOH (E2) with aq. KOH (SN2)
Thinking E1 gives a mixture of regioisomers (it gives only Saytzeff)
Mistaking t-BuOH (solvent, E1) for t-BuO⁻ (base, E2)
Believing fluoride is a good leaving group in E2 (it is poor, favoring Hofmann)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (base/leaving group selectivity; anti-periplanar vs. thermodynamic control)

Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.

edges.conditions: Mg; dry ether; RT. 1,3-Dibromopropane + Mg → BrMg-CH₂CH₂CH₂-MgBr? No: intramolecular: one Grignard end attacks the other C-Br → cyclopropane + MgBr₂ (intramolecular SN2). Mg inserts into one C-Br → Grignard → intramolecular displacement of other Br → cyclopropane. Alternatively: Zn/ether gives same result. 1,3-dihalide with Mg → intramolecular coupling → cyclopropane.
edges.notes: 1,3-dibromopropane (Br-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-Br, n=3, α,ω-dihaloalkane) + Mg/ether → cyclopropane; MECHANISM: Mg inserts into one C-Br → Br-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-MgBr (monoGrignard) → the carbanion at C1 does intramolecular SN2 on C3-Br (3-exo-tet ring closure) → cyclopropane + MgBr₂; RING SIZE RULE for X-(CH₂)n-X + Mg: n=3 → 3-membered ring (cyclopropane); n=4 → 4-membered ring (cyclobutane); n=5 → 5-membered ring (cyclopentane); GENERAL: number of carbons in ring = n (the number of CH₂ groups between the two halogens); LONGER CHAINS: 1,5-dihalo + Mg(1eq)/Δ → cyclopentane (heat required for longer chains to overcome kinetic barrier; ring closure is thermally assisted for 5- and 6-membered rings)

genuine_levers: Ring size (n) in α,ω-dihaloalkane X-(CH₂)n-X determines cycloalkane product (n=3 → cyclopropane, n=4 → cyclobutane, etc.)
Choice of metal (Mg vs Zn) — both give intramolecular coupling to cycloalkane, but Zn is less common
common_traps: Assuming the product is a linear Grignard dimer (BrMg-(CH₂)₃-MgBr) instead of the intramolecular cyclization product
Forgetting the mechanism: Mg inserts into one C-Br to form a Grignard, which then performs an intramolecular SN2 on the other C-Br
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing intramolecular cyclization vs. standard Grignard formation)

edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: NaOEt (sodium ethoxide); heat (reflux in EtOH). CHCl₃ + 3 NaOEt → HC(OEt)₃ (triethyl orthoformate) + 3 NaCl. Mechanism: NaOEt displaces Cl (SN2) three times on CHCl₃ → HC(OEt)₃. Anhydrous conditions. Orthoformate used as formyl-equivalent, protecting group, and in synthesis.

reagent_1.conditions: AMBIDENT NUCLEOPHILE — NO2- FROM KNO2 ATTACKS VIA OXYGEN: Nitrite ion (NO2- = O-N=O-) is ambident: it can attack from either O or N. With KNO2 (free nitrite anion), the O-end attacks R-X preferentially → gives ALKYL NITRITE R-ONO (also written R-O-N=O). The product is an ester of nitrous acid (HO-N=
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: NaOEt (strong base/nucleophile) in anhydrous ethanol with heat drives SN2 substitution, not elimination; using a bulky base (e.g., t-BuOK) would favor E2 elimination to alkene
CHCl₃ is the specific substrate; using a different alkyl halide (e.g., R-CH₂-Cl) would give a simple ether (R-OEt) via SN2, not an orthoester
common_traps: Mistaking NaOEt/heat for E2 elimination conditions (which would give alkene) — here it's an SN2 substitution on a polyhalogenated substrate
Forgetting the triple substitution on CHCl₃ to form the orthoester HC(OEt)₃, not a mono-substituted product
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role discrimination: NaOEt as nucleophile for SN2 vs. base for E2; substrate-specific triple substitution)

edges.conditions: RT to reflux; dry aprotic solvent (DMF, DMSO, or ether); SN2. Sodium/potassium alkoxide (R'ONa/R'OK) prepared first (alcohol + Na). R'O⁻ + R-X → R-O-R' + X⁻. MUST use primary R-X (secondary gives E2; tertiary gives only E2). Best: R'ONa + CH₃I or primary alkyl halide. Dry conditions (alkoxides react with H₂O).

reagent_1.conditions: Dry Ag₂O (silver oxide): RT; SN2 on primary or secondary R-X; dry solvent (Et₂O or acetone). Ag₂O acts as base (accepts X⁻ as AgX precipitate) and O-nucleophile source. 2 R-X + Ag₂O → R-O-R + 2 AgX↓. Mild, anhydrous. Good for simple symmetrical ethers from 1° alkyl halides. Compare: Williamson (alko
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Primary alkyl halide → SN2 ether; secondary/tertiary halide → E2 alkene — substrate structure is a genuine lever
R'ONa (strong base/nucleophile) → SN2 ether; R'OH/H⁺ (acidic conditions) → SN1 ether (with possible carbocation rearrangement) — reagent identity is a genuine lever
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the SN2 reaction occurs at a chiral sp3 carbon (inversion of configuration); if the starting alkyl halide is achiral, no new stereocenters are created
common_traps: Using secondary/tertiary alkyl halide leads to E2 elimination (alkene) instead of SN2 ether — a classic mistake
Using aqueous conditions or protic solvent leads to side reactions (hydrolysis to alcohol) — dry aprotic solvent is essential for alkoxide nucleophile
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate vs. reagent selectivity: SN2 vs. E2; primary vs. secondary/tertiary halide)

edges.conditions: Anhydrous, inert atmosphere (N₂ or Ar); dry ether (diethyl ether) or dry THF solvent; Mg metal turnings; RT or gentle warming. Must exclude moisture strictly (Grignard reacts immediately with H₂O). Mg inserts into C-X bond: R-X + Mg(0) → R-Mg-X. Reactivity order: R-I > R-Br > R-Cl >> R-F. Vinyl and aryl halides require THF (higher bp) and sometimes I₂ activation of Mg surface.
edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

genuine_levers: Anhydrous/inert conditions are mandatory (Grignard reacts with water/O2)
tertiary Grignards suffer Wurtz coupling and elimination side reactions — not a clean textbook step for tertiary halides
common_traps: Grignard carbon bears formal carbanion character — do not confuse with SN2 product
Tertiary Grignards are experimentally problematic; prefer primary/secondary for clean chain synthesis
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (nomenclature of RMgX) + reagent-condition knowledge
