edges.notes: 1,2-addition of HBr to buta-1,3-diene; KCR (Kinetically Controlled Rxn); -40°C (low temperature); H+ to C1 → delocalized allylic cation (C2 and C4); Br- attacks C2 (less hindered, lower TS barrier) → 3-bromobut-1-ene (CH3-CHBr-CH=CH2); 1,2-product = kinetic product; less stable (terminal alkene CH=CH2 remains); at LOW TEMP, kinetic product (1,2) is major; same allylic intermediate also gives 1,4-product at higher temperature | 1,4-addition of HBr to buta-1,3-diene; TCR (Thermodynamically Controlled Rxn); 25°C or room temperature (high temperature relative to -40°C); H+ to C1 → allylic cation → Br- attacks C4 (1,4 product); product: (E)-1-bromobut-2-ene (CH3-CH=CH-CH2Br); 1,4-product = thermodynamic product = more stable (internal alkene CH=CH retained); at HIGH TEMP, thermodynamic product (1,4) is major; rule: 1,2-addition = KCR (-40°C); 1,4-addition = TCR (25°C); SAME allylic cation intermediate for both | 1,4-addition of Br2 (1eq) to buta-1,3-diene; Br+ to C1 → OPEN allylic cation (NOT closed bromonium; allylic resonance breaks the tight bromonium bridge) → Br- attacks C4 ANTI to C1-Br → (E)-1,4-dibromobut-2-ene (TRANS) = ONLY ONE PRODUCT (no cis isomer); key: even in 1,4-addition, the anti-addition geometry is maintained → trans product exclusively; Peter Sykes reference; TS = extended s-trans allylic cation; Br- comes from outside (anti) → trans diaxial addition across C1 and C4; Br2 1,4-addition to 1,3-diene always gives TRANS (E) product | IIT Adv 2016 type; HBr addition to 2-methylbuta-1,3-diene (isoprene, CH2=C(CH3)-CH=CH2); H+ adds to C1 (NOT C4); reason: H+ to C1 → 3° allylic cation [CH3-C+(CH3)-CH=CH2 ↔ CH3-C(CH3)=CH-CH2+] = 3°+allylic (more stable ✓); H+ to C4 would give 2°+allylic (less stable ✗); TCP at 25°C: Br- attacks C4 of the more stable 3°-allylic cation → product (E)-1-bromo-2-methylbut-2-ene (CH2Br-C(CH3)=CH-CH3, 1,4-addition); key: always add H+ to give MOST STABLE allylic carbocation (3° > 2° > 1°)

genuine_levers: Low temperature (-40°C) is a genuine lever — it gives the kinetic 1,2-addition product; at higher temperature (40°C), the thermodynamic 1,4-addition product becomes major
1 equivalent of HBr is a genuine lever — excess HBr can lead to a second addition across the remaining alkene
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the product 3-bromo-1-butene (CH3-CHBr-CH=CH2) is chiral only if the starting diene is unsymmetrical, creating a stereocenter at C-2 upon Br addition; for symmetrical dienes like buta-1,3-diene, the product is achiral
common_traps: Students often write the 1,4-addition product (1-bromo-2-butene) as the major product, forgetting the kinetic control at low temperature
Students may incorrectly draw a simple Markovnikov addition to one alkene, ignoring the conjugated system and allylic cation resonance
Students might assume the product is always chiral, forgetting that symmetrical dienes yield achiral products
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (kinetic vs thermodynamic control); Structural (regiochemistry of addition to conjugated system)

edges.conditions: Cu catalyst; 300°C; air/O₂; catalytic oxidative dehydrogenation. HOCH₂CH₂OH → OHC-CHO (glyoxal) + 2H₂O. Industrial. Alternatively: KMnO₄ (mild, partial oxidation stops at aldehyde before carboxylic acid if carefully controlled). Also: HIO₄ cleaves 1,2-diols → 2 HCHO (not glyoxal, since vicinal diol cleaved).

reagent_1.conditions: Conc H₃PO₄; Δ (200–300°C); dehydration. HOCH₂CH₂OH → H₂C=CHOH (vinyl alcohol, enol) + H₂O → tautomerism → CH₃CHO (acetaldehyde). High-temperature dehydration; acid-catalysed. Enol formed first (thermodynamically unfavourable) → rapid keto-enol tautomerism → acetaldehyde.
reagent_1.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_2.conditions: KHSO₄ or conc H₂SO₄; Δ (strong heat, >200°C); double dehydration (loss of 2 H₂O). Step 1: dehydration at C-2 → enol → propenediol intermediate. Step 2: 2nd dehydration → acrolein (CH₂=CH-CHO, prop-2-enal). Pungent, lachrymatory aldehyde. Characteristic smell of burning fat (triglyceride pyrolysis →
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers. | Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Cu/300°C is a catalytic oxidative dehydrogenation (industrial) — different from oxidative cleavage with HIO4 or KMnO4
HIO4 cleaves 1,2-diols to carbonyls (2 HCHO for ethane-1,2-diol), NOT to the same dialdehyde as Cu/300°C
KMnO4 (mild, controlled) can partially oxidize a diol to aldehyde, but over-oxidation to carboxylic acid is a competing risk
common_traps: Confusing Cu/300°C oxidative dehydrogenation with HIO4 oxidative cleavage — they give different products
Assuming any diol oxidation with KMnO4 stops cleanly at the aldehyde stage without careful control
Forgetting that vicinal diol cleavage (HIO4) of HOCH2CH2OH gives two formaldehyde molecules, not glyoxal
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: oxidative dehydrogenation vs. oxidative cleavage vs. partial oxidation)

edges.conditions: Red phosphorus + excess HI; heat (Δ, reflux); exhaustive dehalogenation. Step 1: HI (or PI₃) replaces OH → 1,2,3-triiodopropane. Step 2: excess HI → reduces C-I bonds → propane. Proof of glycerol structure (historically). HI: red P + I₂ → PI₃ → SN2 on primary OH. Complete reduction.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Excess HI with heat drives exhaustive reduction to alkane; limited HI would stop at alkyl iodide (SN2 product)
Red P + I2 generates PI3 in situ, which is the active electrophile for OH substitution; other reagents (e.g., HBr) give different mechanisms/products
common_traps: Mistaking this for a simple dehydration (diol → alkene) — this is a reductive deoxygenation, not an elimination
Assuming the intermediate triiodide undergoes elimination (E2) instead of reductive cleavage by excess HI
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-driven mechanism shift: PI3 for SN2 substitution followed by HI for reductive cleavage)

edges.conditions: PI₃ (excess, from P + I₂); heat; SN2 on primary OH. Glycerol + PI₃ → 1,2,3-triiodopropane → elimination (HI + alkali or Zn) → allyl iodide (or propene with excess HI). Phosphorus halides substitute OH with I via phosphite ester intermediate → SN2. Primary OH reacts faster than secondary.

reagent_1.conditions: I₂ + NaOH (excess, aq); RT. 2,3-Butanediol: each CHOH is secondary; NaOI oxidises each CHOH → CO group → CH₃COCH₃ (acetone) fragments or pinacol→pinacolone-like? Actually: 2,3-BDO → oxidation to 2,3-butanedione (diacetyl) under I₂/NaOH → each CH₃CO undergoes haloform → 2 CHI₃ + oxalate (C₂O₄²⁻ from
reagent_1.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

genuine_levers: PI3 substitutes OH with I via SN2 on primary OH; PCl5 or SOCl2 would give Cl instead — halide choice is a genuine lever
Excess PI3 leads to complete substitution of all OH groups; stoichiometric PI3 could lead to partial substitution
Primary OH reacts faster than secondary OH — regioselectivity is a genuine lever if diol is unsymmetrical
common_traps: Assuming PI3 gives the same product as PCl5 or SOCl2 (wrong halide)
Forgetting that excess PI3 on glycerol leads to triiodopropane, which can undergo elimination to allyl iodide or propene — not stopping at the alkyl halide
Applying this to tertiary alcohols (tertiary OH does not undergo SN2 with PI3)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: PI3 vs other P-halides; stoichiometry and subsequent elimination)

edges.conditions: KMnO₄ (hot, acidic, excess); reflux. Hot acidic KMnO₄ cleaves vicinal diol → 2 HCOOH (formic acid from primary OH → HCOOH by overoxidation). Contrast: periodic acid (HIO₄) also cleaves 1,2-diols to aldehydes/ketones/HCHO (milder, no overoxidation). KMnO₄ overoxidises HCHO → HCOOH → CO₂ at extreme conditions.

reagent_1.conditions: Conc HNO₃ (oxidising acid); heat; vigorous conditions. HNO₃ oxidises ethylene glycol completely → oxalic acid (ethanedioic acid, HOOC-COOH). HNO₃ is strong oxidant in this context. Compare: glycerol + conc HNO₃/H₂SO₄ → nitroglycerin (esterification, not oxidation — different reaction).
reagent_1.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_2.conditions: I₂ + NaOH (excess); RT. PhCH(OH)CH₂OH → PhCHO-CH₂OH via oxidation? The PhCHOH is secondary → NaOI → PhCO-CHO (phenylglyoxal) → haloform on PhCO-CH= ? Alternatively: NaOH/I₂ oxidises CH₂OH → then the adjacent PhCHOH → PhCO → iodoform from CH₃ group? The product benzoate + CHI₃ means: PhCO-CHI₃ interm
reagent_2.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

edges.notes: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice: Hot acidic KMnO4 overoxidises primary OH groups to carboxylic acids (HCOOH), while HIO4 stops at aldehydes/ketones — a genuine lever
Temperature/conditions: Hot acidic KMnO4 cleaves vicinal diol to acids; cold dilute KMnO4 gives syn-dihydroxylation without cleavage
common_traps: Confusing HIO4 (milder, stops at aldehydes/ketones) with KMnO4 (overoxidises to acids) — a classic JEE trap
Forgetting that hot acidic KMnO4 can further oxidise formic acid (HCOOH) to CO2 under extreme conditions
Mistaking the stereochemistry of the starting diol (syn vs anti) as relevant for the cleavage product — stereochemistry is erased in this oxidative cleavage step
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: KMnO4 vs HIO4; condition selectivity: hot acidic vs cold dilute)

edges.conditions: Acetic acid (2 equiv) or Ac₂O (2 equiv carefully); RT to 60°C; no special catalyst. 1° OH groups (C-1, C-3) are less hindered than 2° OH (C-2) → preferential esterification at positions 1 and 3. Selective under mild conditions. Can also use enzymatic acylation for strict regioselectivity.
edges.notes: Archetype ['III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Regioselectivity: 1° OH groups (C-1, C-3) are preferentially esterified over the 2° OH (C-2) under mild conditions
Reagent choice: Ac₂O (anhydride) vs CH₃COOH (acid) changes the mechanism and reaction rate, potentially affecting selectivity
common_traps: Assuming all three OH groups react equally — the product is a partially esterified diol, not a fully acetylated triacetate
Confusing this mild, selective acylation with exhaustive esterification under forcing conditions (e.g., excess Ac₂O, strong acid catalyst)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regioselectivity based on steric hindrance and reagent mechanism)

edges.conditions: Conc HNO₃ + conc H₂SO₄ (mixed acid, 1:2 ratio by vol); 0°C (CRITICAL — below 5°C, above → explosive decomposition); O-nitration of all 3 OH groups → glyceryl trinitrate (nitroglycerin). H₂SO₄ acts as dehydrant/proton source for NO₂⁺ generation. Temperature strictly <5°C. Product: oily liquid, highly sensitive to shock and heat. Alfred Nobel stabilised with kieselguhr → dynamite.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'II'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Temperature is a critical lever: >5°C leads to explosive decomposition, not controlled esterification
H₂SO₄ acts as a dehydrating agent to generate NO₂⁺; changing the acid (e.g., to HCl) would not yield the nitronium ion
Substrate is a 1,2,3-triol (glycerol); a diol with fewer OH groups yields a partially nitrated ester
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the central carbon of glycerol becomes a stereocenter only if the three nitrate groups are considered different; in practice, the molecule is achiral (C2 symmetry axis)
common_traps: Confusing this with aromatic nitration (electrophilic substitution on benzene) — this is esterification of alcohol OH groups
Forgetting the critical temperature control and writing the reaction at room temperature
Writing the product as a nitro compound (R-NO₂) instead of a nitrate ester (R-O-NO₂)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role: H₂SO₄ as dehydrant, not catalyst; temperature as safety/product lever)

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; neat amine or amine in EtOH or THF; primary or secondary amine (RNH₂ or R₂NH) opens epoxide. Without acid catalyst: amine (better nucleophile than H₂O) attacks at less hindered C (SN2, anti addition) → β-amino alcohol. With acid: more substituted C attacked (SN1-like). Product: trans-β-amino alcohol. Used in synthesis of amino alcohols for asymmetric synthesis.
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry is a genuine lever: basic conditions (RNH₂ alone) → attack at less hindered epoxide carbon (SN2); acidic conditions (RNH₂ with H⁺) → attack at more substituted carbon (SN1-like)
Stereochemistry is a genuine lever: anti addition of amine to epoxide ring; overall stereochemistry depends on the stereochemistry of the starting epoxide
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide carbon being attacked is already a stereocenter; the new alcohol and amine groups create a new stereocenter at the attacked carbon, resulting in a trans-β-amino alcohol relative to the existing substituents
common_traps: Assuming the amine attacks the more substituted carbon under all conditions — this is only true under acidic catalysis
Forgetting the stereochemical outcome: the ring-opening is an anti addition, which is critical for determining the final stereochemistry of the β-amino alcohol
Confusing this with the reaction of a tertiary amine, which does not open epoxides via nucleophilic attack but can form amine oxides with oxidants
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry switch based on acid/base conditions); Interpretive (stereochemistry tracking from epoxide formation to ring-opening)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: RT; aqueous acid (dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl, pH 2–4) or aqueous base (NaOH). Acid opening: H⁺ protonates O → ring strain drives SN1-like opening at more substituted C (Markovnikov) → H₂O attacks from back → anti-diol (trans). Base opening: HO⁻ attacks at less hindered C (SN2, anti addition) → anti-diol. Either way: anti addition of two OH groups across former double bond.
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Acid-catalyzed opening: protonation leads to SN1-like attack at the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov regiochemistry)
Base-catalyzed opening: HO⁻ attacks the less substituted carbon via SN2 (anti-Markovnikov regiochemistry)
Stereochemistry: anti-addition of OH groups is mandatory for both acid and base pathways — compare with syn-addition routes (e.g., OsO4)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide carbons are already chiral or become chiral upon opening; the anti-addition creates two new stereocenters with defined relative stereochemistry (racemic or meso)
common_traps: Assuming syn-addition (common confusion with dihydroxylation via OsO4)
Forgetting that acid and base give opposite regiochemistry but the same anti stereochemistry
Mistaking the epoxide opening for a carbocation rearrangement step (rearrangements are rare unless a tertiary carbocation can form)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry switch: acid vs base); Interpretive (stereochemistry: anti-addition is fixed)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: 18O ISOTOPE LABELING IN ACIDIC EPOXIDE OPENING (symmetrical substrate): H3O+ protonates the labeled 18O → bridged protonated intermediate [C-18O+H-C (epoxide with labeled O protonated)]. H2O (unlabeled, 16O) attacks from ANTI face (backside of the 18O bridge) → forms new 16C-O bond from water, releases 18C-O as 18OH group on the other C. Since the substrate cyclohexene oxide is SYMMETRIC (C2 symmetry), H2O can attack EITHER C with equal probability: attack on C1 → 18O ends up as 18OH on C2 (labe

reagent_1.conditions: BASIC/NEUTRAL EPOXIDE OPENING — 2 KEY RULES: (1) SN2 MECHANISM: Nu:- attacks directly (no protonation step). (2) Nu ATTACKS LESS SUBSTITUTED (LESS CROWDED) CARBON: SN2 prefers less steric hindrance → attacks at 1° C (primary C next to O). The 3° or more substituted C gets the O- (alkoxide). Reason:
reagent_1.note: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Acidic opening (H3O+) leads to anti-addition of water via SN2-like attack on the MORE substituted carbon (Markovnikov-like regioselectivity)
Basic opening (OH-) leads to anti-addition of hydroxide via SN2-like attack on the LESS substituted carbon (anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity)
18O isotope labeling in the acid-catalyzed mechanism: the labeled oxygen (18O) of the epoxide ends up in the OH group on the carbon NOT attacked by water
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide is unsymmetrical and the ring-opening creates a carbon with four different substituents; symmetrical epoxides (like cyclohexene oxide) yield achiral diols
common_traps: Confusing acidic (H3O+) and basic (OH-) opening regioselectivity rules
Forgetting that the overall stereochemistry is anti-addition (trans-diol) from a single enantiomer of an epoxide
Incorrectly applying the 18O isotope result: in acidic opening, the 18O from the epoxide is NOT lost; it becomes an 18OH group
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regioselectivity lever: acid vs base); Structural (stereochemistry of anti-addition)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: RT; anhydrous (or slightly acidic for HX); HX (HCl, HBr, HI). Acid opens epoxide at more substituted C (SN1-like via oxocarbenium) → halide attacks → anti-addition. Trans diaxial opening preferred for cyclohexene epoxides. Product: β-halohydrin (trans if from symmetrical alkene). HBr/HCl: X attacks at more substituted C under acidic conditions.
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Acidic conditions (HX) open epoxide at more substituted carbon (SN1-like via oxocarbenium); basic/nucleophilic conditions open at less substituted carbon (SN2-like)
Stereochemistry: anti-addition of X and OH; trans diaxial opening for cyclohexene epoxides
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates stereocenters if the epoxide is chiral or if the halohydrin product has a chiral center; anti-addition dictates relative stereochemistry
common_traps: Assuming syn addition overall — the correct sequence is syn epoxidation (mCPBA) followed by anti ring-opening (HX)
Forgetting regioselectivity: under acidic conditions, the halide adds to the more substituted carbon, opposite to basic ring-opening
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry under acidic vs basic conditions; stereochemical outcome of anti-addition)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: HX (1 equiv); aqueous or anhydrous; acid opens epoxide at more substituted C (Markovnikov for SN1-like) → anti addition of H and X. Alternatively: X₂ opens epoxide via halonium. Product: trans (anti) vicinal dihalide from cyclic epoxide. Compare: diol from H₂O opening, amino alcohol from amine opening.
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: HX opens via protonation and SN1-like attack at more substituted carbon; X2 opens via halonium ion formation, leading to anti addition
Regiochemistry: Acidic (HX) opening follows Markovnikov-like rule; basic or nucleophilic opening attacks less substituted carbon
Stereochemistry: Cyclic epoxide opening gives trans (anti) vicinal dihalide; stereochemistry is inverted at the attacked carbon
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates stereocenters at both carbons if the epoxide is unsymmetrical or chiral; product stereochemistry is defined by anti addition
common_traps: Assuming syn addition for HX opening (it is anti addition overall)
Forgetting that epoxide opening with HX is regioselective (more substituted carbon attacked by X-)
Confusing this with diol formation (using H2O) or amino alcohol formation (using amine)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry and stereochemistry control); Structural (reagent discrimination: HX vs X2 vs other nucleophiles)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: PCl₅; anhydrous; RT to mild heat. RCOOR' + PCl₅ → RCOCl (acyl chloride) + R'Cl + POCl₃. Both O-C bonds cleaved: acyl-O gives RCOCl, alkyl-O gives R'Cl. Useful for converting esters to acyl chlorides without going through the free acid. Clean for primary R'.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: PCl5 cleaves both O-C bonds of the ester: acyl-O bond gives acid chloride, alkyl-O bond gives alkyl chloride — reagent choice is a genuine lever (e.g., SOCl2 typically converts acids to acid chlorides, not esters)
common_traps: Assuming the ester's alkoxy group (R'O) is lost as an alcohol (R'OH) — it is actually lost as an alkyl chloride (R'Cl)
Confusing this with acid chloride formation from a carboxylic acid (which uses SOCl2, PCl3, or PCl5 but yields HCl/SO2 as byproducts, not R'Cl)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-specific bond cleavage: PCl5 vs other chlorinating agents)

edges.conditions: DIBALH (diisobutylaluminium hydride, DIBAL-H); dry toluene or hexane; –78°C (very low T critical!); anhydrous. Selective reduction: ester → aldehyde (stops at aldehyde at –78°C because product aldehyde-Al complex is stable; warming → alcohol). Mechanism: H⁻ from DIBALH attacks ester C=O → tetrahedral intermediate (stable Al-O chelate at –78°C) → aqueous workup → aldehyde. At RT: ester → alcohol (2 equiv DIBALH or full reduction).
edges.notes: DIBALH (diisobutylaluminium hydride) reduces ester to ALDEHYDE (stops at aldehyde, does NOT over-reduce to alcohol); ethyl ethanoate + DIBALH → ethanal (acetaldehyde); DIBALH is the MOST PREFERRED reagent for ester → aldehyde conversion; also: nitrile + DIBALH → aldehyde (after hydrolysis); amide + DIBALH → aldehyde (good yield); acyl halide + DIBALH → aldehyde; key: DIBALH stops at aldehyde stage because the aluminum alkoxide product is stable at -78°C and does not allow further reduction until workup

genuine_levers: Temperature control: -78°C stops at aldehyde; warming to room temperature leads to over-reduction to alcohol
Reagent stoichiometry: 1 equivalent DIBALH at -78°C gives aldehyde; 2 equivalents or excess at RT gives alcohol
Solvent choice: dry, aprotic solvent (toluene/hexane) is critical; protic solvents can quench the reagent or alter the outcome
common_traps: Assuming DIBALH always gives aldehyde regardless of temperature (it does not; temperature is a critical lever)
Confusing DIBALH with LiAlH4 (LiAlH4 reduces ester directly to primary alcohol, not aldehyde)
Forgetting the aqueous workup step is required to liberate the aldehyde from the aluminium complex
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition selectivity: DIBALH vs LiAlH4; temperature control as a critical lever)

edges.exception_description: DIBALH_over_reduces_ester_to_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Dibalh over reduces ester to alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: H₃O⁺ (dil H₂SO₄ or HCl); H₂O (excess); reflux. Acid hydrolysis (AAc2 mechanism, most common): H⁺ protonates C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → H₂O → hemiacetal-like → EtOH leaves → acetic acid. Reversible (Fischer esterification reverse). Drive forward by excess H₂O. Base: NaOH (irreversible saponification) → sodium acetate → acidify → acetic acid.
edges.notes: Ester acid hydrolysis: ethyl ethanoate (CH3COOC2H5, ethyl acetate) + H3O+/Δ (reversible equilibrium) → ethanoic acid (CH3COOH) + ethanol (C2H5OH); MECHANISM: acid-catalyzed nucleophilic acyl substitution (AAC2): H+ protonates C=O of ester → nucleophilic attack of H2O → tetrahedral intermediate → proton transfers → -OR (alcohol) leaves → carboxylic acid; REVERSIBILITY: acid hydrolysis of esters is REVERSIBLE (equilibrium); the reverse reaction = Fischer esterification (RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H2O); use excess H2O to drive toward hydrolysis; GENERAL: any ester (RCOOR') + H3O+/Δ → RCOOH + R'OH; acid hydrolysis: RCOOH produced (free acid); compare basic: R-COO- produced (carboxylate, requires acidification to get RCOOH); for ALL acid derivatives (anhydride, acyl halide, ester, amide) + H3O+ → RCOOH + corresponding leaving group (R'COOH, HX, R'OH, NH3)

genuine_levers: Acid hydrolysis is reversible; base hydrolysis (saponification) is irreversible and yields carboxylate salt
Excess water drives equilibrium forward; excess alcohol drives reverse (esterification)
common_traps: Confusing acid hydrolysis (reversible, AAc2) with base hydrolysis (irreversible, BAc2)
Forgetting to acidify the product after base hydrolysis to get the free carboxylic acid
Writing SN2 instead of nucleophilic acyl substitution (AAC2) mechanism
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: AAc2 vs BAc2; reversibility: acid vs base hydrolysis)

reagents: BAc2, most common basic hydrolysis, IRREVERSIBLE
edges.conditions: NaOH (aqueous, 1–2 mol/L); reflux (80–100°C); BAc2 mechanism (base, acyl, bimolecular). Mechanism: OH⁻ attacks ester C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → breakdown → carboxylate + R'OH. Irreversible (carboxylate is resonance-stabilised, does not re-esterify under basic conditions). Used for soap manufacture (triglyceride + NaOH → soap + glycerol). ¹⁸O in NaOH ends up in carboxylate product (acyl-O cleavage).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: aq NaOH → BAc2 mechanism, irreversible hydrolysis to carboxylate; alc. NaOH → alkoxide, can lead to transesterification (different product)
¹⁸O labeling: OH⁻ attacks acyl carbon, so ¹⁸O from NaOH ends up in carboxylate product (acyl-O cleavage) — a key mechanistic tracer
common_traps: Confusing BAc2 (base-catalyzed, irreversible) with AAc2 (acid-catalyzed, reversible) — acid hydrolysis uses H3O+ and is reversible
Writing 'ester + NaOH → alcohol + aldehyde' — NaOH hydrolyzes to carboxylate, not aldehyde
Forgetting that the product under basic conditions is the carboxylate salt (RCOO⁻Na⁺), not the free acid (RCOOH) until acidified
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: BAc2 vs AAc2; ¹⁸O tracer analysis)

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄ + MeOH; RT. AAc1 (acid, acyl, unimolecular acyl-O cleavage): H⁺ protonates C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → acylium R-CO⁺ → loses CO → t-Bu⁺ (very stable 3°) → MeOH → t-BuOMe + CO. Only works for pivalate (2,2-dimethylpropanoate) and other very hindered esters where acyl C is inaccessible to nucleophile. CO evolution is diagnostic. Normal esters go AAc2.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Mechanism switch (AAc1 vs AAc2): conc. H₂SO₄/MeOH on a pivalate ester (R = t-Bu) gives AAc1 → ether; normal esters (e.g., MeOAc) under same conditions give AAc2 → transesterification (different ester).
Substrate sterics: Only highly hindered esters (e.g., pivalate) undergo AAc1; less hindered esters follow AAc2.
CO evolution is a diagnostic clue for the AAc1 mechanism.
common_traps: Applying AAc1 mechanism to all esters — students often miss the steric requirement and predict ether for any ester with conc. H₂SO₄/MeOH.
Predicting transesterification (the AAc2 product) for pivalate esters, missing the unique AAc1 pathway.
Confusing this with Fischer esterification (carboxylic acid + alcohol → ester) or its reverse.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: AAc1 vs AAc2 based on substrate sterics)

edges.conditions: Fries rearrangement: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid, >1 equiv); anhydrous; heat (Δ, 100–200°C); CS₂, PhNO₂ or nitrobenzene solvent; or neat. Low T (room to 100°C) → ortho product (thermodynamic control of intramolecular delivery). High T (>150°C) → para product. Photo-Fries (UV, RT) → predominantly ortho. AlCl₃ cleaves ester O-CO → acylium → intramolecular FC acylation of phenol ring.

reagent_1.conditions:
GRIGNARD + TERTIARY AMIDE → KETONE (UNIQUE SNth PRODUCT OF AMIDES):

AMIDE REACTIVITY: Tertiary amide (R-CO-NR2) is the LEAST REACTIVE acid derivative in SNth. NaCl, carboxylate, alkoxide, 2° amine, etc. CANNOT displace NR2 from the amide under normal conditions (NR2⁻/NHR⁻ is a very poor LG, pKa of
reagent_1.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

edges.notes: Rearrangement step: product carbon skeleton differs from substrate. Always verify whether migration occurs before assigning product structure. | Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

genuine_levers: Temperature control: Low T (<100°C) → ortho product (kinetic/thermodynamic intramolecular delivery). High T (>150°C) → para product (thermodynamic control).
Photo-Fries (UV light, RT) → predominantly ortho product.
Substrate limitation: Only phenyl esters (phenol-derived esters) undergo this rearrangement.
common_traps: Assuming the product is a simple ester hydrolysis product (phenol + carboxylic acid) instead of a ketone.
Forgetting that AlCl₃ is required in >1 equivalent (catalytic amount insufficient) and that anhydrous conditions are critical.
Applying standard Friedel-Crafts acylation rules directly (e.g., expecting meta-substitution if ring is deactivated).
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature-dependent regioselectivity; distinguishing Fries rearrangement from simple hydrolysis or intermolecular FC acylation)

edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Fries_AlCl3_gives_C_acylation_not_O_acylation → Fries rearrangement (phenyl ester + AlCl₃/heat) gives C-acylation (ketone at ring carbon) NOT further O-acylation (which would give another ester). AlCl₃ causes the acyl group to migrate from O to C. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | Fries_high_T_gives_ortho_not_para → Exception to the standard rule: Fries high t gives ortho not para. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Fries_rearrangement_high_temperature_gives_para_product → Exception to the standard rule: Fries rearrangement high temperature gives para product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

edges.conditions: Fries rearrangement: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid, excess); heat (Δ, >150°C); anhydrous nitrobenzene or neat. High temperature → para product (4'-hydroxyacetophenone, 4-hydroxyphenyl ethanone). Low temperature → ortho product. AlCl₃ cleaves ester O-CO → acetyl group migrates to ring at para (high T). Used in synthesis of hydroxyphenyl ketones (sunscreen components).

reagent_1.conditions: Fries rearrangement at low T (ortho product): AlCl₃; anhydrous; CS₂ or PhNO₂ solvent; RT to 100°C → ortho product (1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one = 2'-hydroxyacetophenone). High T (>150°C) → para product (4'-hydroxyacetophenone). Acyl group migrates from O to ring C-ortho via intramolecular Friedel-
reagent_1.note: Fries rearrangement: aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R) + AlCl3/Delta → hydroxy aryl ketone (acyl group migrates from O-ester to the aromatic ring; OH is exposed on ring); phenyl ethanoate (Ph-O-CO-CH3) + AlCl3/Delta → 1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one (2-hydroxyacetophenone, ortho) as MAJOR at HIGH TEMP; 4-hydroxyacetophenone (para) MAJOR at LOW TEMP; KEY TEMPERATURE RULE: LOW temp → PARA product (kinetic); HIGH temp → ORTHO product (thermodynamic, chelation stabilization); products: ortho = cyclic chelate favored thermodynamically; para = less steric, kinetically favored; RELATED: Gattermann-Koch also introduces CHO to ring (but without rearrangement)

edges.notes: Fries rearrangement; phenyl acetate (O-acyl) + AlCl3 → 4-hydroxyacetophenone (C-acyl, para major); O-to-C acyl migration; low T favors para product; contrast: without AlCl3, phenol+CH3COCl gives O-acylation (ester) only | Fries rearrangement: aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R) + AlCl3/Delta → hydroxy aryl ketone (acyl group migrates from O-ester to the aromatic ring; OH is exposed on ring); phenyl ethanoate (Ph-O-CO-CH3) + AlCl3/Delta → 1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one (2-hydroxyacetophenone, ortho) as MAJOR at HIGH TEMP; 4-hydroxyacetophenone (para) MAJOR at LOW TEMP; KEY TEMPERATURE RULE: LOW temp → PARA product (kinetic); HIGH temp → ORTHO product (thermodynamic, chelation stabilization); products: ortho = cyclic chelate favored thermodynamically; para = less steric, kinetically favored; RELATED: Gattermann-Koch also introduces CHO to ring (but without rearrangement)

genuine_levers: Temperature controls regiochemistry: high temperature (>150°C) favors para product; low temperature favors ortho product
Substrate requirement: must be an aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R); alkyl esters do not undergo Fries rearrangement
common_traps: Confusing Fries with Friedel-Crafts acylation — Fries uses an aryl ester as the acyl source, not an acyl halide
Assuming the acyl group migrates to the same carbon as the OH group — it migrates to an adjacent ring carbon (ortho or para)
Forgetting that AlCl₃ cleaves the ester O-CO bond, not the Ar-O bond
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature-dependent regioselectivity; mechanism involving O-to-C acyl migration)

edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Fries_AlCl3_gives_C_acylation_not_O_acylation → Fries rearrangement (phenyl ester + AlCl₃/heat) gives C-acylation (ketone at ring carbon) NOT further O-acylation (which would give another ester). AlCl₃ causes the acyl group to migrate from O to C. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | Fries_high_T_gives_ortho_not_para → Exception to the standard rule: Fries high t gives ortho not para. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gattermann_Koch_with_AlCl3_alone_sufficient → Exception to the standard rule: Gattermann koch with alcl3 alone sufficient. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Fries_rearrangement_high_temperature_gives_para_product → Exception to the standard rule: Fries rearrangement high temperature gives para product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

reagents: conc HX (HI > HBr, acidic conditions, SN1 or SN2 depending on R)
edges.conditions: Conc HX (HI > HBr >> HCl) or excess H₂SO₄; reflux. Mechanism: H⁺ protonates ether O → oxonium ion → SN1 (3° or 2° with HI/HBr) or SN2 (1° ethers, X⁻ attacks) → alkyl halide + alcohol → further halogenation of alcohol. HI cleaves fastest (I⁻ best nucleophile). Aryl ethers (PhOR): Ar-O cleaved → PhOH + R-X (aryl C-O too strong for SN on ring). Excess HI: PhOH → PhI ultimately.

reagent_1.conditions: PCl₅; RT to mild heat; anhydrous. R-O-R' + PCl₅ → RCl + R'Cl + POCl₃. Both C-O bonds cleaved. Compare with HX cleavage (gives one alcohol + one halide initially). PCl₅ is more reactive than HX; both C-O bonds broken. Less commonly used; HX is the standard ether cleavage.
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: PCl₅; anhydrous; RT to gentle heat. R-O-R' + PCl₅ → R-Cl + R'-Cl + POCl₃. Both C-O bonds cleaved. Contrast: HI cleavage of ethers (one C-O at a time, gives R-OH then R-I with excess HI). PCl₅ gives both halides directly. Less selective; used mainly for specific halide syntheses.
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions:
ETHER CLEAVAGE WITH HX:

STEP 1: Protonation of ether O → oxonium ion R-O⁺H-R' (fast)
STEP 2: C-O bond cleavage — MECHANISM DEPENDS ON SUBSTITUTION:

IF any C is 3° (tertiary): → SN1: the 3° C-O bond breaks → 3° C⁺ → X⁻ captures → 3° R-X. Other fragment = alcohol.
   - 3°/3°: SN1 at either 3° C (rac
reagent_3.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_4.conditions: No 3° groups in ether → SN2 pathway. Oxonium formed: (CH₃)₂CH-O⁺H-CH₃. Br⁻ attacks at CH₃ (1°, less hindered than isopropyl 2°) from BACKSIDE → CH₃-Br (bromomethane) + (CH₃)₂CH-OH (propan-2-ol). Selectivity: SN2 at 1° vs 2° → strongly favors 1° (less steric hindrance). Product: CH₃Br (IUPAC: bromome
reagent_4.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: HI vs HBr vs HCl reactivity: HI cleaves fastest (I⁻ best nucleophile) and can cleave aryl ethers to PhI with excess
Mechanism switch: SN1 for 3°/2° alkyl ethers (HI/HBr, acidic) vs SN2 for 1° alkyl ethers
Aryl ethers (PhOR) cleave at alkyl-oxygen bond → PhOH + R-X (aryl C-O bond too strong for SN on ring)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide product carbon is chiral and the mechanism (SN1 vs SN2) affects stereochemistry; SN1 leads to racemization, SN2 leads to inversion
common_traps: Assuming HCl cleaves ethers as readily as HI/HBr (HCl is much slower/ineffective)
Forgetting that the alcohol initially formed can be further halogenated to a second alkyl halide with excess HX
Applying SN1 mechanism to 1° alkyl ethers (should be SN2)
Assuming aryl ethers (PhOR) cleave to give Ph-X (instead of PhOH + R-X)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN1 vs SN2 based on substrate; reagent selectivity: HI > HBr >> HCl)

edges.conditions: BF₃·Et₂O (Lewis acid catalyst); CO (1–3 atm); RT to mild heat; anhydrous. Mechanism: BF₃ activates ether O → oxocarbenium (R⁺-O-R') → CO insertion → acylium → ester product. Koch reaction variant. Uncommon in lab; industrial interest for carbonylation of ethers. BF₃ is essential Lewis acid.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: BF₃ is essential Lewis acid catalyst — without it, no reaction; other Lewis acids may give different reactivity or no reaction
CO insertion is the key step — changing to other electrophiles (e.g., alkyl halides) would give different products (e.g., alkylated ethers)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the ether oxygen is attached to a chiral carbon; the CO insertion step does not inherently create new stereocenters, but if the oxocarbenium intermediate is chiral and the reaction proceeds with retention, chirality may be preserved
common_traps: Confusing with ester hydrolysis (ester → ether) — this is the reverse transformation
Assuming a simple substitution — this is a carbonylation (CO insertion), not a typical nucleophilic substitution
Mistaking BF₃ for a Brønsted acid — it's a Lewis acid that activates the ether, not a proton source
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: Lewis acid activation followed by CO insertion, not a common reaction)

edges.conditions: O₂ (air); RT, slow process; radical chain, alpha-C selectivity. Radical initiator (trace): ether stored in air slowly forms alpha-hydroperoxide. Alpha C-H abstraction by RO₂• → alpha radical → O₂ → hydroperoxide. HAZARD: ether peroxides accumulate in stored diethyl ether/THF → explosive on evaporation or heating. Always check peroxide level before distilling aged ether. Remove peroxides: ferrous sulphate, copper mesh, alumina.

reagent_1.conditions: Cl₂/hν or Cl₂/NBS; RT; radical alpha-C-H substitution. Similar selectivity to alkane halogenation but alpha-C (adjacent to O) is most reactive (O lone pair stabilises radical → alpha selectivity). Product: alpha-halo ether (R-O-CHX-R'). Used as alkylating agents (alpha-halo ethers are reactive elect
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Radical mechanism: initiation required (hν or ROOR). Chain process — omitting any propagation step loses marks in mechanism questions. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Radical initiator (e.g., light, trace peroxide) is required for the chain process; without it, the reaction does not proceed at a significant rate.
Alpha-C selectivity: the radical abstracts the hydrogen from the carbon alpha to the oxygen, not from other positions.
The presence of oxygen (O₂) is essential; in its absence, the radical would dimerize or disproportionate instead of forming a hydroperoxide.
common_traps: Assuming this is a standard ionic substitution or addition reaction; it is a radical chain process.
Forgetting that the product is a hydroperoxide (ether-OOH), not a simple oxidized ether like an aldehyde or ketone.
Overlooking the explosive hazard of accumulated ether peroxides upon concentration or heating.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the radical chain mechanism and its specific selectivity)

edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Thermal Claisen rearrangement: heat (Δ, 200–250°C); no catalyst required; neat or high-boiling solvent (diethylaniline). Mechanism: [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement (pericyclic, chair-like TS); allyl group migrates from O to C-2 (ortho) with inversion at γ-carbon (γ,γ'-connectivity). If ortho blocked → further Cope → para product (Claisen-Cope). Product: 2-allylphenol (ortho). Stereospecific (E-allyl ether → Z-allylic C at product).

reagent_1.conditions: ARYL ETHER CLEAVAGE RULE [IIT JEE 2010]: The Ar-O bond (Ph-O) is NEVER broken by HX because the phenyl ring's lone pair on O donates into the ring (resonance) → partial C=O double bond character → Ar-O bond is much stronger than ordinary C-O. ONLY the alkyl C-O bond is cleaved. Products: ALWAYS phen
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: 18O ISOTOPE LABELING MECHANISTIC PROOF for aryl ether cleavage rule: Ph-[18O]-cyclopentyl + H3O+ → SN1 at cyclopentyl (secondary, benzylic-adjacent). Mechanism: H+ protonates [18O] → Ph-18O+(H)-cyclopentyl → cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (NOT Ph-O bond per aryl ether rule) → cyclopentyl+ C+ + Ph-[18O]
reagent_2.note: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.notes: Rearrangement step: product carbon skeleton differs from substrate. Always verify whether migration occurs before assigning product structure. | Archetype ['I'] transformation.
