Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: Thermal conditions (Δ) are essential for the [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement; acid or base catalysts would lead to different reactions (e.g., acid-catalyzed cleavage of ethers)
If the ortho position on the aromatic ring is blocked, the reaction proceeds via a second Cope rearrangement to give the para-allylphenol (Claisen-Cope cascade)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a stereocenter is created at the γ-carbon of the migrating allyl group; the configuration (E/Z) of the starting allyl ether determines the stereochemistry of the new allylic carbon in the product (stereospecific with inversion)
common_traps: Assuming this is a simple substitution or acid-catalyzed reaction — the thermal, pericyclic mechanism is non-ionic and distinct
Forgetting that the product carbon skeleton changes (the allyl group migrates from O to C, altering connectivity) and drawing the original ether structure incorrectly
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism recognition: pericyclic vs ionic); Structural (regiochemistry: ortho vs para migration based on substitution)

edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para). | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

edges.conditions: Step 1: –78°C to 0°C; dry ether (Et₂O) or THF; anhydrous; HCHO (formaldehyde, gas or paraformaldehyde) added to RMgX → addition. Step 2: Aqueous workup H₃O⁺ (NH₄Cl/H₂O); RT. Product: primary alcohol with one more C than Grignard (RMgX + HCHO → R-CH₂-OH). Strictly anhydrous throughout; moisture destroys Grignard (R-MgX + H₂O → R-H + Mg(OH)X).

reagent_1.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; anhydrous; RCHO (aldehyde) + R'MgX → addition to carbonyl. Step 2: Aqueous workup (NH₄Cl/H₂O or dilute H₂SO₄). Product: secondary alcohol (new C-C bond formed, new stereocentre = racemic mixture). Carbonyl carbon hybridisation changes sp²→sp³. Grignard is organome
reagent_1.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

reagent_2.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; anhydrous; R₂CO (ketone) + R''MgX → addition. Step 2: Aqueous workup. Product: tertiary alcohol (three different or same R groups). Steric hindrance of ketone can slow reaction (especially with aryl Grignard + hindered ketone). Use THF (higher bp, better solvation
reagent_2.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

genuine_levers: HCHO (formaldehyde) gives a primary alcohol with one extra carbon; any other aldehyde (RCHO) gives a secondary alcohol; ketone (R2CO) gives a tertiary alcohol
Anhydrous conditions are mandatory — moisture destroys Grignard reagent before reaction
Ester + RMgX gives tertiary alcohol (two additions); amide + RMgX gives ketone (one addition after workup)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbonyl carbon of the aldehyde/ketone (other than HCHO) is prochiral and the Grignard R group is different from the existing substituents
common_traps: Forgetting that HCHO is the only carbonyl giving a primary alcohol
Assuming ester + RMgX stops at ketone (it proceeds to tertiary alcohol)
Using aqueous conditions destroys Grignard (RMgX + H2O → RH)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (carbonyl substrate selectivity: HCHO vs RCHO vs ketone vs ester vs amide); Structural (product carbon count and alcohol class)

edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; dry CO₂ gas (passed through Grignard solution or solid dry ice added) + RMgX → addition → MgX-carboxylate. Step 2: Aqueous workup (H₃O⁺). Product: RCOOH (one carbon more than Grignard). Mechanism: nucleophilic addition of R⁻ to electrophilic C of CO₂. Double addition prevented (carboxylate is much less reactive than CO₂ toward RMgX).
edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

genuine_levers: CO₂ is a unique electrophile for Grignard reagents, giving a carboxylic acid after workup; other carbonyls (aldehydes, ketones, esters) give alcohols
The reaction adds ONE carbon; product RCOOH has one more carbon than the alkyl group R in RMgX
common_traps: Confusing this with ester + Grignard (which gives tertiary alcohol via double addition); CO₂ gives acid, ester gives alcohol
Forgetting the aqueous workup (H₃O⁺) step to protonate the carboxylate salt to the free acid
Thinking the reaction requires two equivalents of Grignard (only one is needed; carboxylate is not electrophilic)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing CO₂ as a distinct, one-carbon electrophile for Grignard addition)

edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: RT; protic solvent (EtOH or EtOH/H₂O); NaBH₄ (mild, selective). Alternative: LiAlH₄ (dry ether, 0°C, anhydrous). Mechanism: H⁻ from BH₄⁻ attacks electrophilic carbonyl C of ketone → alkoxide → protonation → secondary alcohol. NaBH₄: selective (aldehydes and ketones only, NOT esters). New stereocentre formed — racemic mixture (unless chiral catalyst). Facial selectivity governed by steric approach.

reagent_1.conditions: NaBH₄; EtOH or H₂O/EtOH; RT. Ketone → secondary alcohol. H⁻ from NaBH₄ attacks carbonyl C (less hindered face if chiral: gives mixture or facial selectivity with catalyst). Product: propan-2-ol (isopropanol). New stereocentre: racemic unless asymmetric catalyst (CBS, BINAL-H). Alternatively: LiAlH₄/
reagent_1.note: LAH (or NaBH₄) reduction of ketone → secondary alcohol: propan-2-one (acetone, CH₃COCH₃) + LiAlH₄ → propan-2-ol (isopropanol, (CH₃)₂CHOH, secondary alcohol 2°); GENERAL: R-C(=O)-R' + LiAlH₄ → R-CH(OH)-R' (secondary alcohol); MECHANISM: H⁻ attacks C of ketone C=O from less hindered face → alkoxide R-CH(O⁻)-R' → H₂O → secondary alcohol; FACIAL SELECTIVITY in cyclic ketones: H⁻ (small reagent) attacks from axial direction (equatorial attack possible but depends on substrate) → can give cis or trans product; COMPARISON: LiAlH₄ (bulky) and L-Selectride (hindered LAH) give DIFFERENT facial selectivity in cyclic ketones — use to control stereochemistry; NaBH₄ also works for ketones (selective, mild); SUMMARY TABLE: aldehyde → primary alcohol; ketone → secondary alcohol; ester → two primary alcohols; RCOOH → primary alcohol; amide → amine; acyl halide → primary alcohol

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | LAH (or NaBH₄) reduction of ketone → secondary alcohol: propan-2-one (acetone, CH₃COCH₃) + LiAlH₄ → propan-2-ol (isopropanol, (CH₃)₂CHOH, secondary alcohol 2°); GENERAL: R-C(=O)-R' + LiAlH₄ → R-CH(OH)-R' (secondary alcohol); MECHANISM: H⁻ attacks C of ketone C=O from less hindered face → alkoxide R-CH(O⁻)-R' → H₂O → secondary alcohol; FACIAL SELECTIVITY in cyclic ketones: H⁻ (small reagent) attacks from axial direction (equatorial attack possible but depends on substrate) → can give cis or trans product; COMPARISON: LiAlH₄ (bulky) and L-Selectride (hindered LAH) give DIFFERENT facial selectivity in cyclic ketones — use to control stereochemistry; NaBH₄ also works for ketones (selective, mild); SUMMARY TABLE: aldehyde → primary alcohol; ketone → secondary alcohol; ester → two primary alcohols; RCOOH → primary alcohol; amide → amine; acyl halide → primary alcohol

genuine_levers: NaBH₄ is selective for aldehydes/ketones; LiAlH₄ reduces a broader range (esters, acids, amides) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Protic solvent (EtOH) vs anhydrous ether for LiAlH₄ — condition choice dictates reagent stability and mechanism
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the carbonyl carbon, resulting in a racemic mixture unless the substrate is prochiral and the reducing agent is chiral or the substrate has a pre-existing chiral center inducing diastereoselectivity
common_traps: NaBH₄ does NOT reduce esters, carboxylic acids, or amides — a common overreach
Assuming LiAlH₄ can be used in protic solvents (it reacts violently with H₂O/ROH)
Forgetting that the product is a racemic secondary alcohol unless stereoselective conditions are specified
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄); Structural (stereochemistry if prochiral ketone specified)

edges.conditions: SeO₂ (selenium dioxide); dioxane or AcOH-H₂O; reflux. Riley oxidation: SeO₂ oxidises methyl ketone at α-carbon → α-diketone (2-oxopropanal = methylglyoxal = pyruvaldehyde). Mechanism: SeO₂ ene reaction with enol → [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement → → α-hydroxyl → further oxidation → diketo. Product: SeO₂ + CH₃COCH₃ → CH₃CO-CHO (methylglyoxal). SeO₂ also oxidises α-C of C=C (allylic oxidation) and secondary C=C.
edges.notes: SeO₂ oxidation of α-CH₃ to C=O: propan-2-one (acetone, CH₃COCH₃) + SeO₂ → 2-oxopropanal (methylglyoxal, CH₃-CO-CHO); GENERAL: SeO₂ oxidizes the -CH₂- or -CH₃ group ALPHA to an existing C=O (minimum 2 α-H required); the result is introduction of a NEW C=O at the α-carbon → α-diketone or α-ketoaldehyde; RULE 1: needs minimum 2 α-H on the carbon being oxidized; SELECTIVITY: -CH₃ > -CH₂- (methyl group is preferentially oxidized over methylene in mixed systems; boxed rule); EXAMPLES: CH₃CHO (acetaldehyde) + SeO₂ → glyoxal (OHCCHO); acetophenone (Ph-COCH₃) + SeO₂ → phenylglyoxal (Ph-CO-CHO); cyclohexanone + SeO₂ → cyclohexane-1,2-dione (1,2-diketone from ring); Ph-CH₂-Ph (diphenylmethane) + SeO₂ → Ph-CO-Ph (benzophenone; CH₂ alpha to two Ph rings → C=O); N-methylmorpholine + SeO₂ → N-formylmorpholine (N-CH₃ → N-CHO; works for N-adjacent CH₃ too); MECHANISM: SeO₂ forms an ene-type cyclic TS with the α-C-H → selenious acid intermediate → SH₂O-Se-O forms on α-C → O transferred → α-carbonyl; ALSO: for methyl ketones R-CO-CH₃ with both α-CH₃ (1C, 3H) and α-CH₂ (2C if present): CH₃ is preferentially oxidized (-CH₃ > -CH₂-)

genuine_levers: SeO2 oxidises the alpha-methylene or alpha-methyl group of a ketone to a carbonyl, producing an α-diketone or α-ketoaldehyde
Minimum of two alpha-hydrogens required on the carbon being oxidised; -CH3 > -CH2- in selectivity
common_traps: SeO2 does NOT reduce a ketone to an aldehyde; it oxidises the alpha-carbon, so the original carbonyl remains
SeO2 oxidation is NOT a general method for converting any ketone to an aldehyde; it is specific for alpha-carbon oxidation
SeO2 is also used for allylic oxidation of alkenes, which is a different transformation
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent specificity: SeO2 for alpha-oxidation vs other reagents for carbonyl reduction or oxidation)

edges.conditions: Wolff-Kishner: hydrazone formation first (NH₂NH₂, EtOH, RT) then base/heat (KOH, ethylene glycol, reflux 200°C). Mechanism: C=O + NH₂NH₂ → hydrazone (C=N-NH₂) → base removes N-H → anion → loss of N₂ → carbanion → protonation → CH₂. Base-sensitive substrates: Clemmensen (Zn-Hg/conc HCl) instead. Bamford-Stevens: tosylhydrazone + base → diazo intermediate → carbene/alkene.

reagent_1.conditions: Hot concentrated HNO₃; Δ; or hot KMnO₄/H₂SO₄. Oxidative ring opening: cyclohexanone + HNO₃ (conc, fuming, 60–70°C) → ring-cleaved adipic acid (hexanedioic acid). Industrial: cyclohexanone/cyclohexanol mixture + HNO₃ (Baeyer catalyst V₂O₅/HNO₃) → adipic acid for Nylon-6,6. Vigorous acidic oxidative c
reagent_1.note: Popoff's Rule — cyclic ketone oxidative ring opening: cyclohexanone (6-membered ring ketone) + HNO₃/Δ → hexanedioic acid (adipic acid, HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH); POPOFF'S RULE: ketone cleavage by hot strong oxidants (KMnO₄/H+ at high T, K₂Cr₂O₇/H+/Δ, HNO₃/Δ); RULE: the C=O carbon goes with the chain containing FEWER carbons; for CYCLIC ketones: ring is opened and BOTH fragments get COOH → diacid; MECHANISM: ketone enolizes → enol (C=C adjacent to C=O); KMnO₄ cleaves the C=C of the enol; both carbons of the former C=C become COOH; for cyclohexanone: enol of cyclohexanone has C=C in the ring → cleavage of ring → linear 6-carbon chain with COOH on each end → adipic acid; ACYCLIC EXAMPLE: pentan-2-one (CH₃-CO-CH₂CH₂CH₃) + hot KMnO₄ → CH₃COOH (acetic acid, 2C, the 'smaller' fragment containing C=O) + CH₃CH₂COOH (propanoic acid, 3C); Note: -C(=O)- goes with the chain containing fewer C atoms; DETERMINATION: count Cs on each side of ketone C=O (not counting C=O itself); fewer C side gets the C=O in the product; this is Popoff's Rule

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Popoff's Rule — cyclic ketone oxidative ring opening: cyclohexanone (6-membered ring ketone) + HNO₃/Δ → hexanedioic acid (adipic acid, HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH); POPOFF'S RULE: ketone cleavage by hot strong oxidants (KMnO₄/H+ at high T, K₂Cr₂O₇/H+/Δ, HNO₃/Δ); RULE: the C=O carbon goes with the chain containing FEWER carbons; for CYCLIC ketones: ring is opened and BOTH fragments get COOH → diacid; MECHANISM: ketone enolizes → enol (C=C adjacent to C=O); KMnO₄ cleaves the C=C of the enol; both carbons of the former C=C become COOH; for cyclohexanone: enol of cyclohexanone has C=C in the ring → cleavage of ring → linear 6-carbon chain with COOH on each end → adipic acid; ACYCLIC EXAMPLE: pentan-2-one (CH₃-CO-CH₂CH₂CH₃) + hot KMnO₄ → CH₃COOH (acetic acid, 2C, the 'smaller' fragment containing C=O) + CH₃CH₂COOH (propanoic acid, 3C); Note: -C(=O)- goes with the chain containing fewer C atoms; DETERMINATION: count Cs on each side of ketone C=O (not counting C=O itself); fewer C side gets the C=O in the product; this is Popoff's Rule

genuine_levers: Wolff-Kishner (NH₂NH₂/KOH) reduces C=O to CH₂; Clemmensen (Zn-Hg/HCl) is the alternative for acid-sensitive substrates
Wolff-Kishner reduces both C=O and C=C (exception case) — standard carbonyl reductions (NaBH₄, LiAlH₄) do NOT reduce isolated C=C
common_traps: Confusing Wolff-Kishner with Clemmensen — both give alkane from ketone, but conditions differ (basic vs acidic)
Forgetting the high-temperature step (KOH/ethylene glycol, reflux) — hydrazone formation alone does not give the product
Applying Wolff-Kishner to substrates with other reducible groups (e.g., nitro, nitrile) without considering side reactions
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: Wolff-Kishner vs Clemmensen vs standard carbonyl reductions); Exception handling (C=C reduction)

edges.exception_description: Wolff_Kishner_reduces_both_C=O_and_C=C → Exception to the standard rule: Wolff kishner reduces both c=o and c=c. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: I2/NaOH (aq): iodoform reaction. Only methyl ketones (CH3COR) react. Product: RCOO⁻ (carboxylate/carboxylic acid after acidification) + CHI3 (iodoform, yellow precipitate). Iodoform test is qualitative test for CH3CO- group.

reagent_1.conditions: I₂ + NaOH (excess); RT. Both terminal groups (PhCH₂- and CH₃CO-) participate: (1) PhCH₂CO- → PhCOO⁻ + CHI₃; and (2) remaining fragment → CHI₃ + acetate? Wait: PhCH₂-CO-CH₃ has only one CH₃CO- group. The CH₂ group adjacent to phenyl is benzylic — NaOH/I₂ gives oxidative cleavage? Actually: 2 mol CHI₃
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['II', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Iodoform reaction: CH3-CO-R + 3I2 + 4NaOH → R-COO⁻Na⁺ + CHI3↓ + 3NaI + 3H2O. Also positive for secondary alcohols with -CHOH-CH3 group (oxidised to methyl ketone in situ). CHI3 is yellow solid with antiseptic smell.

genuine_levers: Only methyl ketones (CH3COR) react; other ketones (e.g., RCH2COR') are negative
Secondary alcohols with -CHOH-CH3 group give positive test (oxidized in situ to methyl ketone)
Acid derivatives (esters, amides, etc.) with CH3CO- group are negative despite having the group
common_traps: Assuming all carbonyls with an alpha-CH3 give iodoform (esters, acids, amides do NOT)
Forgetting that cyclohexanone gives a positive test via ring cleavage to sodium adipate
Mistaking EtOAc as directly positive (it's positive only after saponification to ethanol)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate scope: methyl ketone vs. other carbonyls; in situ oxidation of alcohols)

edges.exception_description: Positive iodoform test for all CH3CO- containing compounds → CH3COOH and ALL acid derivatives (ester, anhydride, acid chloride, amide) are NEGATIVE for iodoform despite having CH3CO group | Cyclohexanone is iodoform negative (no CH3CO group) → Cyclohexanone DOES give CHI3 via ring-opening; triiodination at alpha-C then base cleaves ring → CHI3 + sodium adipate (dicarboxylate) | EtOAc (ethyl acetate) is haloform negative (it's an ester) → EtOAc gives INDIRECT positive iodoform: OH⁻ saponifies EtOAc → EtOH → EtOH gives iodoform (HCOO⁻ + CHI3) | Methanol gives iodoform (it has C-H bond alpha to OH) → MeOH gives NEGATIVE iodoform test; it oxidizes to HCHO (formaldehyde) which has NO alpha-CH3 group → formate only
edges.exception_trap: Students confuse functional group presence with reactivity; acid derivatives cannot form enolate under basic iodoform conditions

edges.conditions: Mannich reaction: aqueous EtOH or MeOH; slight acidity (pH 4–5, AcOH); RT to mild heat (50°C). Three components: ketone (with α-H) + HCHO + NH₃ (or primary/secondary amine). Mechanism: HCHO + NH₃ → iminium (H₂C=NH₂⁺) → enol attacks iminium → Mannich base (β-amino carbonyl). Product: β-aminoketone. Quaternary if 2° amine used (Mannich base with no N-H).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Ketone must have at least one α-H to enolise — no reaction if α-carbon is quaternary
Primary amine (RNH2) gives secondary Mannich base (N-H); secondary amine (R2NH) gives tertiary Mannich base (no N-H) — amine nucleophile identity is a lever
Formaldehyde (HCHO) is the typical electrophile; other aldehydes give substituted β-aminocarbonyls (crossed Mannich)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter forms at the β-carbon (the carbon bearing the amine) if the ketone enolate attacks the iminium from one face; product is often racemic under standard conditions
common_traps: Using a ketone without α-H (e.g., di‑tert‑butyl ketone) gives no reaction — a common trap
Confusing with reductive amination (ketone + amine + reducing agent → amine) — Mannich uses HCHO and an enol
Forgetting the three‑component nature (ketone + HCHO + amine) and writing a two‑component condensation
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (three‑component coupling; enolate vs imine chemistry); Structural (new C–C bond formation at α‑position)

edges.conditions: Wittig reaction: methylenetriphenylphosphorane (H₂C=PPh₃, ylide); anhydrous THF or benzene; RT to mild heat. Mechanism: nucleophilic addition of ylide C to ketone C=O → [2+2] cycloaddition → oxaphosphetane → retro-[2+2] → alkene (C=CH₂, exo-methylene) + Ph₃P=O. Wittig gives C=C where C=O was. Phosphorus ylide: Ph₃P + CH₂X₂ → Ph₃P-CH₂X⁺ → base → ylide.
edges.notes: Wittig reaction; cyclohexanone + H2C=PPh3 (ylide/methylenephosphorane) → methylenecyclohexane (exocyclic alkene) + Ph3P=O (triphenylphosphine oxide); yield ~95%; mechanism: [2+2] cycloaddition of C=O and C=PPh3 → oxaphosphetane (4-membered ring: C-O-P-C betaine) → retro-[2+2] → alkene + Ph3P=O; trick: replace the C=O with C=CH2 (whatever is on ylide carbon replaces O); can also make di/trisubstituted alkenes; key advantage: forms alkene at EXACT position of C=O, not mixture; 95% yield (high selectivity unlike E1/E2 which give Zaitsev mixture)

genuine_levers: Ylide carbon substituents determine alkene substituents — change from H2C=PPh3 to RCH=PPh3 gives substituted alkene (RHC=CHR')
Ketone vs aldehyde: both work, but aldehyde gives terminal alkene (RHC=CH2) unless ylide is substituted
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the ylide is non-symmetrical (R1R2C=PPh3) AND the ketone is prochiral, creating a new chiral alkene (E/Z isomerism, not a stereocenter); Wittig does NOT create new tetrahedral stereocenters
common_traps: Wittig gives alkene, NOT alcohol — do not confuse with Grignard addition (RMgX → alcohol)
Ylide carbon is nucleophilic, NOT electrophilic — it attacks the carbonyl carbon
Product is exocyclic alkene (C=CH2) for methylenetriphenylphosphorane, NOT endocyclic unless ylide is part of a ring
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ylide structure determines alkene substitution pattern); Structural (C=O to C=C transformation)

edges.conditions: Haloform reaction: X₂ (Cl₂, Br₂, or I₂) + NaOH (aq, excess); RT to mild heat. Step 1: base deprotonates α-H → enolate → halogenation → repeat ×3 → CX₃ group on methyl. Step 2: NaOH attacks CX₃-CO-R → CX₃⁻ + RCOO⁻ (tetrahedral intermediate → collapse). CX₃⁻ → CHX₃ (haloform). Positive haloform test (yellow CHI₃ precipitate with I₂/NaOH): methyl ketone, acetaldehyde, ethanol, secondary methyl alcohol (CH₃CHOH-R).

reagent_1.conditions: NaOH (aq); heat (Δ). Favorskii rearrangement: α-haloketone + base → ring contraction. 2-Chlorocyclohexan-1-one + NaOH → cyclopentane carboxylic acid (ring contracts by 1 carbon via cyclopropanone intermediate). Mechanism: α-H removed by base → carbanion → ring closes on C-Cl (displacement of Cl⁻) →
reagent_1.note: Favorskii rearrangement: 2-chlorocyclohexan-1-one (alpha-chlorocyclohexanone, 6-membered ring) + (1)NaOH → (2)H+ → cyclopentane-1-carboxylic acid (COOH on 5-membered ring); GENERAL: alpha-halocyclic ketone + base (OH-) → ring-contracted carboxylic acid (ring shrinks by 1 C; the COOH replaces the ring junction); MECHANISM: OH- abstracts alpha-H → carbanion → intramolecular SN2 on C-X (the carbanion attacks the C-Cl carbon → forms cyclopropanone intermediate) → cyclopropanone ring opens under OH- (nucleophilic addition of OH-) → ring-opened carboxylate → H+ → carboxylic acid; RING CONTRACTION RULE: n-membered ring alpha-haloketone + NaOH → (n-1)-membered ring COOH; so cyclohexanone derivative → cyclopentane-COOH; cyclopentanone derivative → cyclobutane-COOH; ALTERNATIVE: with OR- (alkoxide) instead of OH-: gives ester (methyl or ethyl ester) instead of carboxylic acid; NICHE JEE TOPIC: a ring-contracted COOH from a cyclic ketone is the signature of Favorskii

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Favorskii rearrangement: 2-chlorocyclohexan-1-one (alpha-chlorocyclohexanone, 6-membered ring) + (1)NaOH → (2)H+ → cyclopentane-1-carboxylic acid (COOH on 5-membered ring); GENERAL: alpha-halocyclic ketone + base (OH-) → ring-contracted carboxylic acid (ring shrinks by 1 C; the COOH replaces the ring junction); MECHANISM: OH- abstracts alpha-H → carbanion → intramolecular SN2 on C-X (the carbanion attacks the C-Cl carbon → forms cyclopropanone intermediate) → cyclopropanone ring opens under OH- (nucleophilic addition of OH-) → ring-opened carboxylate → H+ → carboxylic acid; RING CONTRACTION RULE: n-membered ring alpha-haloketone + NaOH → (n-1)-membered ring COOH; so cyclohexanone derivative → cyclopentane-COOH; cyclopentanone derivative → cyclobutane-COOH; ALTERNATIVE: with OR- (alkoxide) instead of OH-: gives ester (methyl or ethyl ester) instead of carboxylic acid; NICHE JEE TOPIC: a ring-contracted COOH from a cyclic ketone is the signature of Favorskii

genuine_levers: Haloform/iodoform reaction is SELECTIVE for methyl ketones (CH3COR) — the alpha-carbon must be a methyl group
Product is RCOOH + CHX3 (trihalomethane precipitate), NOT an alkyl halide
common_traps: The organic product is the CARBOXYLIC ACID (RCOOH), not the haloform (CHX3) — students often name the wrong product
Iodoform (CHI3) is the precipitate used for the qualitative test; the useful synthetic product is the carboxylate
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate scope: methyl ketone requirement; product identification)

edges.conditions: 0°C to RT; neutral to slightly acidic; HCN or KCN/H₂SO₄. CN⁻ attacks ketone carbonyl (less reactive than aldehyde due to steric + electronic factors). Equilibrium lies to left for sterically hindered ketones. Acetone cyanohydrin → methyl methacrylate (industrial). Product: α-hydroxy nitrile (racemic).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: HCN (acidic) vs. KCN (basic) dictates mechanism — HCN gives acid-catalyzed addition (slower), KCN gives direct nucleophilic attack by CN⁻
Substrate reactivity: Aldehydes react faster than ketones; sterically hindered ketones give poor yields due to unfavorable equilibrium
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the carbonyl carbon, but the product is racemic unless a chiral catalyst or enzyme is used
common_traps: Forgetting that the product is an α-hydroxy nitrile (cyanohydrin), not a nitrile or an imine
Assuming the reaction is irreversible — equilibrium lies to the left for ketones, requiring careful conditions
Confusing with other carbonyl additions (e.g., Grignard gives tertiary alcohol, not cyanohydrin)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and equilibrium control); Structural (recognizing cyanohydrin functional group)

edges.conditions: Mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of peroxyacid O-OH to C=O → tetrahedral intermediate (Criegee intermediate). Then 1,2-migration of R group anti-periplanar to the O-O bond → loss of carboxylate → ester. The group that migrates is retained with its configuration (suprafacial migration). For cyclic ketones → lactone (ring expansion). O is inserted between C=O carbon and the migrating group.
edges.notes: Full Baeyer-Villiger migration order: H > 3° > 2° > cyclohexyl > Ph > 1° > cyclopropyl > CH₃. Key exceptions: (1) H migrates FASTER than 3° alkyl — opposite to carbocation stability; (2) cyclopropyl migrates SLOWER than 1° alkyl (unlike SN1 where cyclopropylcarbinyl is exceptionally fast); (3) Ph (aryl) migrates less readily than secondary alkyl. Regioselectivity rule: more substituted/electron-rich C migrates → O goes between carbonyl C and the migrating group → ester C-O forms on side of migrating group.

genuine_levers: Migratory aptitude order (H > 3° > 2° > Ph > 1° > CH3) determines which group migrates to form the ester — a classic JEE selectivity lever
Cyclic ketones undergo ring expansion to lactones — ring size changes
Configuration of the migrating chiral center is retained (suprafacial migration)
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the migrating group is chiral; stereochemistry is retained, not created de novo
common_traps: Assuming migratory aptitude follows carbocation stability (it does not; H migrates fastest)
Forgetting that cyclopropyl migrates slower than primary alkyl (opposite to SN1 reactivity)
Applying the reaction to tertiary amines (they form amine oxides with peracids, not esters)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (migratory aptitude is counterintuitive and a major JEE trap); Structural (regiochemistry for unsymmetrical ketones)

edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | The rate of intramolecular SN2 cyclization increases as ring size increases (larger rings form more slowly due to entropy loss, so smaller rings form faster), giving order: 3 > 4 > 5 > 6 > 7 → Rate order is: 6 > 5 > 3 > 7 > 4. The 4-membered ring is the SLOWEST (not the largest ring). The 6-membered ring is the FASTEST (not the 3-membered). The 3-membered ring (aziridine) forms FASTER than the 4-membered ring (azetidine) despite being more strained. Teacher's Fun Fact (page 3, yellow data): n=4 (6-membered ring) = 100, n=1 (3-membered) = 7, n=2 (4-membered) = 1, n=5 (7-membered) = 2. The 4-membered ring is exceptionally slow because the cyclobutane-like TS has extreme angle strain (Baeyer strain ~ 90° bond angles). The 6-membered ring is fastest because the chair-like TS has no ring strain AND near-ideal SN2 geometry (N···C-Br angle ~170°). The teacher's teacher 'Rate of intramolecular cyclisation ↓' (decreasing arrow) refers to the rate going from 6 → lower as you go to other ring sizes, not a monotonic decrease with ring size. | In 1,2-migration reactions, more substituted (tertiary) alkyl groups migrate FASTER than less substituted (hydrogen), following carbocation stability → In Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, HYDROGEN migrates FASTER than tertiary alkyl: the full order is H > 3° > 2° > cyclohexyl > phenyl > 1° > cyclopropyl > methyl. This is the ONLY major rearrangement where H has the highest migratory aptitude and where cyclopropyl (highly reactive in SN1) migrates SLOWER than primary alkyl. Reason: BV migration is NOT a carbocation process — it is a concerted migration toward the electrophilic O-O bond (Criegee mechanism). The C-H bond donates electrons directly into the O-O σ* orbital; C-H hyperconjugation is exceptionally effective in this geometry.
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: RT to reflux; acid catalyst (H⁺, p-TsOH, or BF₃); excess alcohol; Dean-Stark or molecular sieves (remove H₂O). Mechanism: acid protonates ketone → oxocarbenium → alcohol attacks → β-alkoxy alcohol → loss of H₂O → enol ether. Enol ethers stable to base but hydrolysed easily by aqueous acid → ketone. Used as protecting groups for ketones.
edges.notes: Archetype ['III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Acid-catalyzed acetal formation (ketone + ROH + H⁺) yields ether (acetal); base-catalyzed aldol or enolate alkylation yields a different product — catalyst choice is a genuine lever
Using a diol (e.g., ethylene glycol) yields a cyclic acetal; using a mono-alcohol yields an acyclic acetal — alcohol structure is a genuine lever
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the acetal carbon (C(OR)₂) only if the two alkoxy groups are different; with identical R groups or a symmetric diol, the product is achiral
common_traps: Mistaking this for Fischer esterification (carboxylic acid + ROH + H⁺) which yields ester, not acetal
Forgetting that acetals are protecting groups and are hydrolyzed back to ketone with aqueous acid — writing them as irreversible final products is a trap
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst role: acid for acetal formation vs base for enolate chemistry); Structural (if a chiral acetal is formed)

edges.conditions: X₂ + sodium acetate (CH₃COONa) buffer; acidic conditions (AcOH); RT. Mono-halogenation stops at monosubstitution under acidic catalysis (unlike base: base-catalysed → trihalomethyl). Mechanism: enol formation (acid-catalysed tautomerism) → halogen attacks enol → mono-halo ketone + HX (neutralised by NaOAc). Product: single α-halo ketone.

reagent_1.conditions: NaOH (dilute) + heat; RT first then Δ; aldol condensation. Acetone self-aldol: OH⁻ → enolate CH₂⁻COCH₃ → attacks another acetone C=O → 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one → heat → dehydration → 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one (mesityl oxide). Alternatively: mixed aldol with formaldehyde → other products. Self-co
reagent_1.note: Self-aldol condensation of propan-2-one (acetone): (1) OH- removes α-H → enolate CH3CO-CH2- ; (2) Enolate attacks C=O of another acetone → 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one (diacetone alcohol, β-hydroxy ketone = ALDOL PRODUCT); (3) E1CB: -H2O → 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one (mesityl oxide, CH3-CO-CH=C(CH3)2 = α,β-unsaturated ketone = ALDOL CONDENSATION product); condition: slow Δ with dilute NaOH; diacetone alcohol is the isolated product at low temp (no condensation); mesityl oxide is the product at higher temp; RATE ∝ [acetone][OH-]^1

reagent_2.conditions: NaOH (dilute) + acetaldehyde (ethanal); RT then heat; mixed (crossed) aldol condensation. Acetone (enolisable, acceptor and donor) + ethanal (better electrophile) → OH⁻ → acetone enolate attacks ethanal C=O → β-hydroxy ketone → heat → dehydration → (E)-pent-3-en-2-one (trans-methyl vinyl ketone anal
reagent_2.note: Cross-aldol condensation of propan-2-one (acetone) + ethanal (acetaldehyde) + OH-/Δ → MAJOR product = (E)-pent-3-en-2-one (CH3-CH=CH-CO-CH3, methyl vinyl ketone type); REASON MAJOR: (a) acetone has 6 α-H (generates most enolate) = nucleophile; (b) acetaldehyde C=O is more electrophilic (less hindered, more reactive) = electrophile; so cross product (acetone enolate + acetaldehyde electrophile) is major; MINOR products: crotonaldehyde (CH3CHO self-aldol) and mesityl oxide (acetone self-aldol); cross-aldol with 2 enolizable C=O compounds gives 4 possible products (A+A, A+B, B+A, B+B) = 4 condensation products theoretically; major = cross product using more reactive electrophile + more enolizable nucleophile

reagent_3.conditions: NaOH (dilute, 5–10%); low temperature (0–5°C or RT); aqueous. Aldol addition (not condensation): OH⁻ → acetone enolate → attacks 2nd acetone C=O → β-hydroxy ketone product (diacetone alcohol = 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one). Stop at addition product (no heat → no dehydration). Ba(OH)₂ is selective
reagent_3.note: Aldol REACTION (no condensation) of acetone: (1) OH- removes α-H → enolate; (2) Enolate attacks another acetone → tetrahedral intermediate → protonation → 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one (DIACETONE ALCOHOL); NO condensation (-H2O) occurs at low temperature; DISTINCTION: Aldol RXN = β-hydroxy carbonyl product (diacetone alcohol = aldol product); Aldol CONDENSATION = α,β-unsaturated product (mesityl oxide); condition for aldol rxn only (no condensation): dilute NaOH, low temp; condition for condensation: heating/stronger base; REQUIREMENT: at least 1 α-H for aldol rxn; at least 2 α-H for aldol condensation (one for enolate, one for E1CB elimination)

reagent_4.conditions: NH₂OH·HCl (hydroxylamine hydrochloride); NaOAc (sodium acetate, buffer); aqueous-ethanolic; pH 4–5; RT. Condensation: C=O + H₂NOH → carbinolamine (C(OH)(NHOH)) → –H₂O → oxime (C=NOH). Syn and anti oximes (E/Z about C=N). Acid buffers (pH 4–5): protonates OH → facilitates dehydration step. Too acidic
reagent_4.note: Carbonyl + NH2-G reaction: >C=O + NH2-G (pH 5-7, H+) → >C=N-G + H2O; propan-2-one (acetone) + NH2OH (hydroxylamine) → propan-2-one oxime ((CH3)2C=N-OH); MECHANISM: (1) N: of NH2-G attacks C=O → tetrahedral aminol intermediate (C with OH and NH-G); (2) protonation of OH → loss of H2O; (3) >C=N-G product; pH 5-7 is OPTIMAL: too acidic (pH<3) = amine protonated (RNH3+) → no nucleophile; too basic (pH>9) = C=O not activated → slow reaction; ALSO: G table: G=-H → NH3 → imine (>C=NH); G=-R → RNH2 (primary amine) → Schiff base (>C=NR); G=-OH → NH2OH → oxime; G=-NH2 → N2H4 → hydrazone; G=-NHPh → PhNHNH2 → phenylhydrazone; G=-NH-2,4-DNP → 2,4-DNP-hydrazone (orange precipitate, used for identification); G=-NHCONH2 → semicarbazide → semicarbazone

reagent_5.conditions: N₂H₄ (hydrazine, anhydrous or aq); H⁺ (mildly acidic, pH 5–7); EtOH-H₂O; RT. Condensation: C=O + N₂H₄ → hydrazone (C=N-NH₂). Same mechanism as oxime. Hydrazones: Wolff-Kishner precursor (KOH/heat → alkane); Bamford-Stevens (tosylhydrazone + base → diazo → alkene). Unsubstituted hydrazine: C=N-NH₂; p
reagent_5.note: Hydrazone formation: C=O + N2H4 (hydrazine, NH2-NH2) + H+/pH 5-7 → hydrazone (>C=N-NH2); propan-2-one (CH3COCH3) + N2H4 → propan-2-one hydrazone ((CH3)2C=N-NH2); KEY USE: hydrazone is the INTERMEDIATE in Wolff-Kishner reduction (forms at step 1); ALSO: o-phthalaldehyde + N2H4 → cyclic di-imine product (both CHO react with both NH2 of N2H4 → bicyclic ring containing N); BASICITY/REACTIVITY order for NH2-G reagents toward C=O: N2H4 (hydrazine) > PhNHNH2 (phenylhydrazine) > NH2NHCONH2 (semicarbazide) [order 1>2>3]; semicarbazide is least reactive because the CONH2 group withdraws electrons from the NH2 making it less nucleophilic; phenylhydrazone: Ph ring withdraws electrons → less nucleophilic than hydrazine; 2,4-DNP reagent (2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine) gives bright orange/red precipitate (characteristic test for C=O compounds = 2,4-DNP TEST)

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Self-aldol condensation of propan-2-one (acetone): (1) OH- removes α-H → enolate CH3CO-CH2- ; (2) Enolate attacks C=O of another acetone → 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one (diacetone alcohol, β-hydroxy ketone = ALDOL PRODUCT); (3) E1CB: -H2O → 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one (mesityl oxide, CH3-CO-CH=C(CH3)2 = α,β-unsaturated ketone = ALDOL CONDENSATION product); condition: slow Δ with dilute NaOH; diacetone alcohol is the isolated product at low temp (no condensation); mesityl oxide is the product at higher temp; RATE ∝ [acetone][OH-]^1 | Cross-aldol condensation of propan-2-one (acetone) + ethanal (acetaldehyde) + OH-/Δ → MAJOR product = (E)-pent-3-en-2-one (CH3-CH=CH-CO-CH3, methyl vinyl ketone type); REASON MAJOR: (a) acetone has 6 α-H (generates most enolate) = nucleophile; (b) acetaldehyde C=O is more electrophilic (less hindered, more reactive) = electrophile; so cross product (acetone enolate + acetaldehyde electrophile) is major; MINOR products: crotonaldehyde (CH3CHO self-aldol) and mesityl oxide (acetone self-aldol); cross-aldol with 2 enolizable C=O compounds gives 4 possible products (A+A, A+B, B+A, B+B) = 4 condensation products theoretically; major = cross product using more reactive electrophile + more enolizable nucleophile | Aldol REACTION (no condensation) of acetone: (1) OH- removes α-H → enolate; (2) Enolate attacks another acetone → tetrahedral intermediate → protonation → 4-hydroxy-4-methylpentan-2-one (DIACETONE ALCOHOL); NO condensation (-H2O) occurs at low temperature; DISTINCTION: Aldol RXN = β-hydroxy carbonyl product (diacetone alcohol = aldol product); Aldol CONDENSATION = α,β-unsaturated product (mesityl oxide); condition for aldol rxn only (no condensation): dilute NaOH, low temp; condition for condensation: heating/stronger base; REQUIREMENT: at least 1 α-H for aldol rxn; at least 2 α-H for aldol condensation (one for enolate, one for E1CB elimination) | Carbonyl + NH2-G reaction: >C=O + NH2-G (pH 5-7, H+) → >C=N-G + H2O; propan-2-one (acetone) + NH2OH (hydroxylamine) → propan-2-one oxime ((CH3)2C=N-OH); MECHANISM: (1) N: of NH2-G attacks C=O → tetrahedral aminol intermediate (C with OH and NH-G); (2) protonation of OH → loss of H2O; (3) >C=N-G product; pH 5-7 is OPTIMAL: too acidic (pH<3) = amine protonated (RNH3+) → no nucleophile; too basic (pH>9) = C=O not activated → slow reaction; ALSO: G table: G=-H → NH3 → imine (>C=NH); G=-R → RNH2 (primary amine) → Schiff base (>C=NR); G=-OH → NH2OH → oxime; G=-NH2 → N2H4 → hydrazone; G=-NHPh → PhNHNH2 → phenylhydrazone; G=-NH-2,4-DNP → 2,4-DNP-hydrazone (orange precipitate, used for identification); G=-NHCONH2 → semicarbazide → semicarbazone | Hydrazone formation: C=O + N2H4 (hydrazine, NH2-NH2) + H+/pH 5-7 → hydrazone (>C=N-NH2); propan-2-one (CH3COCH3) + N2H4 → propan-2-one hydrazone ((CH3)2C=N-NH2); KEY USE: hydrazone is the INTERMEDIATE in Wolff-Kishner reduction (forms at step 1); ALSO: o-phthalaldehyde + N2H4 → cyclic di-imine product (both CHO react with both NH2 of N2H4 → bicyclic ring containing N); BASICITY/REACTIVITY order for NH2-G reagents toward C=O: N2H4 (hydrazine) > PhNHNH2 (phenylhydrazine) > NH2NHCONH2 (semicarbazide) [order 1>2>3]; semicarbazide is least reactive because the CONH2 group withdraws electrons from the NH2 making it less nucleophilic; phenylhydrazone: Ph ring withdraws electrons → less nucleophilic than hydrazine; 2,4-DNP reagent (2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine) gives bright orange/red precipitate (characteristic test for C=O compounds = 2,4-DNP TEST)

genuine_levers: Acidic conditions (NaOAc buffer) stop at mono-halogenation; basic conditions (NaOH) lead to polyhalogenation and haloform reaction
Reagent choice: X2 (Cl2, Br2, I2) determines the halogen introduced; NaOAc provides acidic buffer to prevent base catalysis
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the α-carbon of the starting ketone is prochiral, halogenation creates a stereocenter; for symmetrical ketones (e.g., acetone), no stereocenter is created
common_traps: Mistaking this for base-catalysed halogenation (which gives trihalomethyl ketone and haloform)
Assuming the reaction requires a strong acid (it uses a weak acid buffer)
Forgetting that the α-carbon must have at least one α-H for enolisation to occur
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acid vs base catalysis distinction for α-halogenation); Structural if the product is chiral

edges.exception_description: Aldol condensation requires base → Acid-catalysed aldol is also possible; intramolecular aldol (Dieckmann equivalent) can cyclise to form rings under both acid and base conditions | aldol_condensation_requires_only_1_alpha_H → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation requires only 1 alpha h. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_E2_mechanism → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation e2 mechanism. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Robinson_annulation_aldol_first_then_Michael → Exception to the standard rule: Robinson annulation aldol first then michael. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Wolff_Kishner_reduces_both_C=O_and_C=C → Exception to the standard rule: Wolff kishner reduces both c=o and c=c. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Mechanism: CH₂N₂ adds to C=O as nucleophile → diazonium zwitterion intermediate (R₁-C(-O⁻)(-CH₂N₂⁺)-R₂). Loss of N₂ → 1,2-shift of group anti-periplanar to N₂ → chain-elongated ketone. Migration aptitude H > Ph > R. Examples: Ph-CO-CH₃ + CH₂N₂ → CH₃-CO-CH₂-Ph (Ph migrates); RCHO (aldehyde, H on C=O) + CH₂N₂ → R-CO-CH₃ (H migrates → methyl ketone). For cyclic ketones: ring expansion by one CH₂.
edges.notes: In the diazonium intermediate, the group ANTI-PERIPLANAR to N₂ migrates (like Beckmann rearrangement where anti group migrates). Priority: H > Ph > R — H migrates fastest because C-H bond is most easily donated (hyperconjugation-like). Ph > R because Ph can delocalize the developing positive charge. Contrast with Baeyer-Villiger where H also migrates fastest, but phenyl migrates LESS than 2° alkyl in BV (different mechanisms). CH₂N₂ + ketone is NOT the same as Arndt-Eistert (which uses acyl halide + CH₂N₂ → ketene → homologated acid).

genuine_levers: Cyclic ketone substrate leads to ring expansion by one CH2, forming a lactone; acyclic ketone gives chain-elongated ketone
Migration aptitude (H > Ph > R) determines which group migrates, affecting product structure
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — stereochemistry of the migrating group is inverted if it is chiral, due to anti-periplanar migration requirement
common_traps: Confusing this with Baeyer-Villiger oxidation (peracid oxidizes ketone to ester/lactone) — reagents and mechanism are different
Assuming the most substituted alkyl group migrates fastest (correct for carbocation rearrangements, but here H migrates fastest)
Thinking aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N2+) are stable intermediates (they are extremely unstable and lose N2 instantly)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (migration aptitude order is counterintuitive and contrasts with Baeyer-Villiger); Structural (ring expansion vs chain elongation)

edges.exception_description: Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | The rate of intramolecular SN2 cyclization increases as ring size increases (larger rings form more slowly due to entropy loss, so smaller rings form faster), giving order: 3 > 4 > 5 > 6 > 7 → Rate order is: 6 > 5 > 3 > 7 > 4. The 4-membered ring is the SLOWEST (not the largest ring). The 6-membered ring is the FASTEST (not the 3-membered). The 3-membered ring (aziridine) forms FASTER than the 4-membered ring (azetidine) despite being more strained. Teacher's Fun Fact (page 3, yellow data): n=4 (6-membered ring) = 100, n=1 (3-membered) = 7, n=2 (4-membered) = 1, n=5 (7-membered) = 2. The 4-membered ring is exceptionally slow because the cyclobutane-like TS has extreme angle strain (Baeyer strain ~ 90° bond angles). The 6-membered ring is fastest because the chair-like TS has no ring strain AND near-ideal SN2 geometry (N···C-Br angle ~170°). The teacher's teacher 'Rate of intramolecular cyclisation ↓' (decreasing arrow) refers to the rate going from 6 → lower as you go to other ring sizes, not a monotonic decrease with ring size. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions. | In 1,2-migration reactions, more substituted (tertiary) alkyl groups migrate FASTER than less substituted (hydrogen), following carbocation stability → In Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, HYDROGEN migrates FASTER than tertiary alkyl: the full order is H > 3° > 2° > cyclohexyl > phenyl > 1° > cyclopropyl > methyl. This is the ONLY major rearrangement where H has the highest migratory aptitude and where cyclopropyl (highly reactive in SN1) migrates SLOWER than primary alkyl. Reason: BV migration is NOT a carbocation process — it is a concerted migration toward the electrophilic O-O bond (Criegee mechanism). The C-H bond donates electrons directly into the O-O σ* orbital; C-H hyperconjugation is exceptionally effective in this geometry.
edges.exception_trap: Students apply SN2 mechanism to diazonium hydrolysis; mechanism is SN1 or DN+AN (phenyl cation).

edges.conditions: pH 4–5 (buffered slightly acidic); aqueous-alcoholic; NH₂OH·HCl (hydroxylamine hydrochloride) + base (pyridine or NaOAc). Condensation: C=O + NH₂OH → carbinolamine → –H₂O → oxime (C=N-OH). Syn/anti isomers possible (E/Z about C=N bond). Ketones give ketoximes; aldehydes give aldoximes. Beckmann rearrangement converts oxime → amide (H₂SO₄, PCl₅, or BF₃; group anti to OH migrates).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'II'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: pH control (buffered acidic) is critical: too acidic protonates NH2OH, too basic deprotonates it, both slow reaction
Reagent choice: NH2OH·HCl + base (pyridine/NaOAc) gives oxime; NH2NH2 (hydrazine) gives hydrazone; 2,4-DNP gives 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone — reagent identity is a genuine lever
Product geometry: oximes exist as E/Z isomers (syn/anti) about C=N bond; formation can be stereoselective
common_traps: Confusing oxime formation with Beckmann rearrangement — Beckmann is a separate reaction requiring strong acid/catalyst (H2SO4, PCl5)
Writing NH2OH alone without the acid/base pair — the actual reagent is hydroxylamine hydrochloride plus a mild base to generate free NH2OH in situ
Forgetting that oximes from unsymmetrical ketones can give two isomeric oximes (though not stereocenters, they are constitutional isomers)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: oxime vs other C=N derivatives); Structural (E/Z isomerism of product)

edges.conditions: Dienone-Phenol rearrangement: H⁺ (conc H₂SO₄ or HCl), heat (Δ). Mechanism: protonation of C=O → allylic cation at C-4 → 1,2-shift of better migrating group (COOEt > Ph > Me) → dienyl cation → loss of H⁺ → aromatic phenol. Driving force: aromatisation. Acid catalyst essential. Migration aptitude: COOEt > Ph > Me.

reagent_1.conditions: Mechanism: H⁺ protonates enone → allylic carbocation at C-4 → 1,2-migration of the better-migrating group (COOEt > Ph > Me) from C-4 to the adjacent C-3 → dienyl cation → loss of proton → phenol (aromatization). The group that MIGRATES is the one with better migratory aptitude; the remaining group s
reagent_1.note: Migration aptitude in Dienone-Phenol: COOEt (ethoxycarbonyl) > Ph (phenyl) > Me (methyl). The migrating group moves with its bonding electrons (nucleophilic migration). EWG-containing group (COOEt) migrates fastest because it stabilises the developing carbanion character at the migration terminus. Contrast with SN1 where EDG-Ar migrates fastest. Product is a phenol (thermodynamically stable aromatic compound drives rearrangement).
