At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

reagents: Lewis acid (BF₃, AlCl₃, FeBr₃, SnCl₄) + trace H₂O
edges.conditions: Step 1 Initiation: BF₃ + H₂O → H⁺[BF₃OH]⁻; H⁺ adds to CH₂ (Markovnikov) → tertiary (CH₃)₂C⁺-CH₃. Step 2 Propagation: (CH₃)₂C⁺ + n isobutylene → growing tertiary carbocation chain. Step 3 Termination: carbocation + counter-ion → neutral polymer chain.
edges.notes: Cationic polymerisation requires: (1) electron-rich alkene (stabilises carbocation intermediate); (2) Lewis acid catalyst. Works for: polystyrene (H₂C=CHPh → tertiary-benzylic), polyvinyl ethers (H₂C=CH-OR), polyisobutylene. Contrast with anionic (electron-poor monomers) and radical (any alkene). Cationic: rate of propagation >> termination → high MW but broad distribution.

genuine_levers: Monomer must be electron-rich alkene (e.g., isobutylene, styrene, vinyl ethers) to stabilise carbocation — electron-poor alkenes (e.g., acrylonitrile) do NOT undergo cationic polymerisation
Lewis acid + trace H₂O (proton source) is essential for cationic initiation — anionic polymerisation requires strong base; radical polymerisation requires radical initiator (e.g., AIBN, peroxides)
common_traps: Assuming any alkene can undergo cationic polymerisation — only electron-rich monomers work
Confusing cationic (Lewis acid + H₂O) with radical (peroxide/heat) or anionic (organolithium) polymerisation conditions
Forgetting that termination step yields a neutral polymer chain, not a charged species
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer selectivity: cationic vs anionic vs radical polymerisation)

edges.conditions: BUNA-N: oil-resistant rubber (nitrile rubber); BUNA-S: general purpose synthetic rubber; Neoprene (polychloroprene): oil and flame resistant, homo-polymer of 2-chloro-1,3-butadiene (chloroprene); all are addition polymers and elastomers
edges.notes: BUNA = butadiene + Na (sodium, early catalyst). -N = acrylonitrile; -S = styrene. Neoprene monomer: 2-chlorobuta-1,3-diene (IUPAC: 2-chloro-1,3-butadiene); polymer is polychloroprene. Neoprene is a HOMO-polymer (one monomer type). BUNA-N and BUNA-S are both copolymers. All are elastomers held by weak van der Waals forces.

genuine_levers: Monomer identity determines polymer properties: butadiene + acrylonitrile (BUNA-N) gives oil-resistant rubber; butadiene + styrene (BUNA-S) gives general-purpose rubber; chloroprene (Neoprene) gives oil/flame-resistant rubber
Polymerisation type: addition polymerisation (radical-initiated) vs. condensation polymerisation (e.g., nylon, polyester) — this is an addition polymer
common_traps: BUNA-N and BUNA-S are copolymers (two monomers), Neoprene is a homopolymer (one monomer) — students often misclassify them all as copolymers
Confusing 'BUNA' as a monomer — it is a brand name for butadiene-based polymers, not a chemical name
Incorrectly stating these are condensation polymers (they are addition polymers)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer identification and polymer classification: homopolymer vs. copolymer, addition vs. condensation)

edges.conditions: Each step: one -Cl replaced by -S₂-S⁻Na⁺; second -Cl of adjacent molecule closes chain. NaCl eliminated. Polysulphide rubber (Thiokol) — resistant to organic solvents, oils, jet fuels. Condensation + copolymer (two different monomers) + addition of sulphide links.

reagent_1.conditions: Sulphur forms cross-links (-Sx- bridges, x = 1–8) between polyisoprene chains at double bond C=C or adjacent allylic CH₂ groups; prevents chain slipping → improved mechanical properties. Temperature 373–415K. Higher %S → more cross-links → harder rubber.
reagent_1.note: Properties of vulcanised rubber vs raw: BETTER elasticity (optimal cross-links), BETTER tensile strength, BETTER chemical resistance, LESS absorption of water, LESS solubility in organic solvents, NOT brittle at low T or sticky at high T. Ebonite (40–50% S) = very hard, non-elastic (excessive cross-linking removes elasticity entirely). Vulcanisation also involves thiazole accelerators to speed process. Natural rubber = cis-1,4-polyisoprene; gutta percha = trans-1,4-polyisoprene (non-elastic, different geometry).

edges.notes: Thiokol uses SN2 on the primary C-Cl bonds. Product is a polysulphide elastomer (rubber-like) with alternating C₂H₄ and S₂ units. Used as fuel hose, gasket material. Not to be confused with natural rubber — Thiokol has sulphur in main chain (not as cross-link). Question: Is it addition or condensation? Condensation (because NaCl small molecule is eliminated). | Properties of vulcanised rubber vs raw: BETTER elasticity (optimal cross-links), BETTER tensile strength, BETTER chemical resistance, LESS absorption of water, LESS solubility in organic solvents, NOT brittle at low T or sticky at high T. Ebonite (40–50% S) = very hard, non-elastic (excessive cross-linking removes elasticity entirely). Vulcanisation also involves thiazole accelerators to speed process. Natural rubber = cis-1,4-polyisoprene; gutta percha = trans-1,4-polyisoprene (non-elastic, different geometry).

genuine_levers: Reaction is a step-growth polycondensation (eliminates NaCl), not a chain-growth addition polymerization
Requires primary alkyl halide (C-Cl) for efficient SN2 attack by disulphide nucleophile
Sodium disulphide (Na₂S₂) provides -S-S- linkages; changing to Na₂S would give monosulphide (-S-) linkages
common_traps: Mistaking it for vulcanization (sulphur cross-linking of natural rubber) — here, sulphur is in the polymer backbone
Confusing it with an addition polymerization — this is a condensation (eliminates NaCl)
Assuming the product is a thermoplastic like polyethylene — it's a specialized elastomer resistant to solvents
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (polymer classification: condensation vs addition; mechanism: SN2 polycondensation)

edges.conditions: addition polymerisation: no byproduct; monomer must have C=C (alkene); repeat unit = monomer minus π-bond. HDPE denser, higher MP, more crystalline. LDPE less dense, more flexible.
edges.notes: LDPE: high pressure (1000–2000 atm), 350–570K, radical mechanism → branched chains (branching prevents close packing → low density). HDPE: Ziegler-Natta, low pressure, 333–343K → linear chains → high crystallinity → high density. PVC thermoplastic, can be plasticised. Teflon = poly(tetrafluoroethene): most chemically inert polymer known; non-stick surface.

genuine_levers: Radical initiator (LDPE) vs. Ziegler-Natta (HDPE) determines polymer branching and properties (density, crystallinity)
Monomer structure (e.g., ethene vs. propene) determines polymer identity (polyethene vs. polypropene) and tacticity
Presence of peroxide/UV initiates radical mechanism, leading to branched LDPE; Ziegler-Natta gives stereoregular polymers
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — Ziegler-Natta polymerization of unsymmetrical alkenes (e.g., propene) can create stereocenters along the chain, leading to isotactic/syndiotactic polymers
common_traps: Assuming all alkene polymerizations give the same product — LDPE and HDPE have different structures/properties due to mechanism
Forgetting that radical polymerization (LDPE) requires high pressure/temperature, while Ziegler-Natta (HDPE) works under mild conditions
Mistaking polymer repeat unit — it is the alkene monomer minus the π-bond, not the full alkene structure
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and catalyst choice determines polymer structure and properties); Structural (monomer identity determines polymer identity)

edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Step 1 Initiation: Ph-CO-O-O-CO-Ph → Δ/hν → 2 Ph-CO-O• → 2 Ph• + 2 CO₂; Ph• + CH₂=CH₂ → Ph-CH₂-CH₂•. Step 2 Propagation: Ph-CH₂-CH₂• + n CH₂=CH₂ → growing radical chain. Step 3 Termination: two radical chains combine (coupling) or disproportionate (H-transfer → one saturated + one unsaturated end).
edges.notes: Free radical polymerisation used for LDPE (high pressure, radical mechanism → branched). Termination modes: (a) combination: R• + R• → R-R; (b) disproportionation: one chain gives H to another → one saturated + one with terminal alkene. Explains why NOT all ethylene converts to polymer (side reactions, termination). Initiator is NOT the same as catalyst — it is consumed. AIBN (azobisisobutyronitrile) is another common initiator.

genuine_levers: Initiator choice (benzoyl peroxide, acetyl peroxide, AIBN) determines radical generation rate and temperature needed (heat vs UV)
Termination mode (combination vs disproportionation) determines polymer end groups (saturated vs terminal alkene) and molecular weight distribution
High pressure conditions favor linear growth; low pressure favors chain transfer/branching (LDPE vs HDPE)
common_traps: Confusing initiator (consumed) with catalyst (regenerated)
Assuming all monomer converts to polymer (ignoring termination side reactions)
Forgetting that radical addition to unsymmetrical alkenes gives anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity (e.g., HBr/peroxides)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (radical chain mechanism: initiation/propagation/termination steps and their consequences)

edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Reimer-Tiemann reaction: CHCl₃ + NaOH; aqueous; 60–70°C. Mechanism: CHCl₃ + NaOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene); :CCl₂ attacks phenol ring (EAS-like) → intermediate → hydrolysis → CHO group. Regioselective ortho (with trace para). Product: 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde) + 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde. Also: Duff reaction (hexamethylenetetramine/AcOH → ortho-formylation).

reagent_1.conditions: Étard reaction: CrO₂Cl₂ (chromyl chloride); CS₂ or CCl₄ solvent; RT. CrO₂Cl₂ oxidises toluene (methylbenzene) selectively at benzylic position → chromium ester complex → hydrolysis → benzaldehyde. Alternatively: MnO₂/H₂SO₄, or electrolytic oxidation. Different from KMnO₄ (gives benzoic acid). CrO₂Cl
reagent_1.note: Etard reaction; methylbenzene + CrO2Cl2/CS2 → [Ph-CH(OCr(OH)Cl2)2] complex intermediate → H3O+ → benzaldehyde (Ph-CHO); SELECTIVE: stops at aldehyde (not COOH); contrast: KMnO4 goes to benzoic acid; Etard specific to benzylic CH3 → CHO

edges.notes: Reimer-Tiemann reaction; CHCl3+NaOH → :CCl2 (dichlorocarbene); :CCl2 attacks ortho carbanion of phenoxide → -Cl-/-H+ → salicylaldehyde (2-hydroxybenzaldehyde, MAJOR); ortho selectivity due to carbene attack on o-carbanion of phenoxide | Etard reaction; methylbenzene + CrO2Cl2/CS2 → [Ph-CH(OCr(OH)Cl2)2] complex intermediate → H3O+ → benzaldehyde (Ph-CHO); SELECTIVE: stops at aldehyde (not COOH); contrast: KMnO4 goes to benzoic acid; Etard specific to benzylic CH3 → CHO

genuine_levers: Reimer-Tiemann reaction requires a phenol (or highly activated benzene like aniline) — simple benzene or alkylbenzene does NOT react
Ortho selectivity is the major lever — if both ortho positions are blocked, reaction proceeds at para position
Dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) is the electrophile, not CHCl₃ itself — generation requires NaOH and CHCl₃
common_traps: Applying this reaction to non-phenolic substrates (e.g., toluene) is wrong — it only works for phenols and similarly activated arenes
Forgetting that the product is an ortho-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde), not a simple benzaldehyde
Assuming the reaction works on any substituted benzene — it requires a strong activating group (-OH, -NH₂)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: phenol vs other arenes; regioselectivity: ortho vs para)

edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | Oxidation of primary amine RCH2NH2 with KMnO4 gives RNO2 → RCH2NH2 + KMnO4 → RCHO → RCOOH (over-oxidation path); tertiary primary amine R3CNH2 gives R3C-NO2 | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Reimer_Tiemann_CHCl3_is_NOT_the_electrophile → In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, CHCl₃ is NOT the electrophile; the actual electrophile is dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), generated from CHCl₃ by base (NaOH removes HCl: CHCl₃ + OH⁻ → :CCl₂ + Cl⁻ + H₂O). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Reimer_Tiemann_gives_para_hydroxybenzaldehyde_as_major_product → Exception to the standard rule: Reimer tiemann gives para hydroxybenzaldehyde as major product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | KMnO4_H_plus_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_aldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: Kmno4 h plus oxidizes primary alcohol to aldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | tert_alkylbenzene_oxidized_by_KMnO4_to_benzoic_acid → Exception to the standard rule: Tert alkylbenzene oxidized by kmno4 to benzoic acid. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

edges.conditions: O₃; Zn (reductive workup); –78°C; CH₂Cl₂. 1,2-Dimethylbenzene (o-xylene) ozonolysis → ring opening → glyoxal-like fragments + butane-2,3-dione (2,3-butanedione = diacetyl = biacetyl, CH₃COCOCH₃). Complete ring opening of the ortho-substituted aromatic system? More precisely: per-ozonolysis of all C=C in ring of o-xylene → multiple carbonyl fragments including CH₃CO-COCH₃ (butane-2,3-dione). Vigorous conditions for aromatic ozonolysis.
edges.notes: Ozonolysis of o-xylene (1,2-dimethylbenzene); treating benzene ring as Kekulé structure with 3 C=C bonds: o-xylene + O3/Zn → butane-2,3-dione (diacetyl, CH3-CO-CO-CH3) + ethanedial (glyoxal, OHC-CHO); the two C=C bonds adjacent to the two CH3 groups give the CH3-CO-CO-CH3 fragment; the third C=C gives OHC-CHO; general rule for benzene ozonolysis: treat as Kekulé structure, cleave each C=C → sum of fragments; for plain benzene: 3x ethanedial (glyoxal) = 3x OHC-CHO

genuine_levers: Ozonolysis of aromatic rings requires treating the Kekulé structure as a set of three C=C bonds; each bond is cleaved to give carbonyl fragments
The substitution pattern on the benzene ring determines the specific carbonyl products (e.g., o-xylene gives butane-2,3-dione and glyoxal)
common_traps: Forgetting that aromatic ozonolysis requires reductive workup (Zn) to prevent over-oxidation to carboxylic acids
Incorrectly applying alkene ozonolysis rules directly to benzene without considering the Kekulé structure and multiple cleavages
Mistaking the product as a single ketone instead of a mixture of carbonyl fragments from ring opening
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (requires conceptual translation of aromatic ring into Kekulé structure for bond cleavage)

edges.conditions: -M power determined by: electrophilicity of carbonyl/sp carbon + stabilisation of negative charge in -M structure. Anhydride has two C=O competing for electrons. Carboxylate (COO⁻) weakest because negative charge already stabilised.
edges.notes: Practical ordering for ring electron density comparison: NO₂ strongest -M (both -M and -I); COO⁻ weakest -M (compensating negative charge). Amide (-CONH₂) weaker -M than ester (-COOR) because N lone pair donates back into C=O.

genuine_levers: Relative -M strength of substituents determines electrophilicity and reactivity order: NO₂ > COOR > CONH₂ > COO⁻
Anhydride formation requires a highly electrophilic carbonyl; substituents that strongly withdraw electrons (-M) promote reaction
common_traps: Mistaking COO⁻ (carboxylate) for a strong -M group; it is the weakest due to its pre-stabilized negative charge
Forgetting that two C=O groups in anhydride compete for electron density, affecting overall electrophilicity
Assuming amide (-CONH₂) is a stronger -M group than ester (-COOR); N lone pair donation makes it weaker
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ranking -M strength of substituents and predicting electrophilicity for anhydride formation)

edges.conditions: Kolbe-Schmitt reaction: Step 1: phenol + NaOH → sodium phenoxide. Step 2: CO₂ (4–7 atm); 125–150°C; dry sodium phenoxide → sodium salicylate. Step 3: H⁺ workup → salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid). Mechanism: CO₂ electrophile attacks phenoxide at ortho C (ortho selectivity for Na salt; para for K salt). Used industrially to make aspirin precursor.

reagent_1.conditions: Hot KMnO₄/OH⁻ (aq); reflux; then H₃O⁺ workup. Oxidation of methyl group to COOH. KMnO₄ (hot, conc, basic or acidic) oxidises alkylbenzene side chain → carboxylic acid (regardless of chain length — all Cα and beyond oxidised, only Cα bond to ring retained). Alternatively: K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄/heat. Product:
reagent_1.note: KMnO4 oxidation of alkylbenzene side chains: methylbenzene (toluene, PhCH3) + hot acidic KMnO4 → benzoic acid (Ph-COOH); GENERAL: any alkylbenzene (Ph-R where R = primary or secondary alkyl of ANY length) + hot KMnO4 → Ph-COOH (benzoic acid); the ENTIRE alkyl chain is oxidized away, leaving only ONE COOH carbon directly bonded to the ring; the length of the alkyl chain DOES NOT MATTER — whether R = CH3, C2H5, C10H21, etc., the product is always Ph-COOH; MECHANISM: KMnO4 oxidizes the benzylic C-H (alpha carbon to ring) → this benzylic carbon becomes the COOH; successive oxidations oxidize the chain away; MULTIPLE GROUPS: if the benzene ring has multiple alkyl groups, each becomes a -COOH (e.g., para-dimethylbenzene → terephthalic acid (para-benzenedicarboxylic acid)); SECOND EXAMPLE: Ph-CH(CH3)- (isopropyl: Ph-CH(CH3)2) → Ph-COOH + CH3COOH (both fragments oxidized to acids; however the phenyl C that was attached to the chain → benzoic acid; the detached fragment CH3 → ethanoic acid (actually any chain fragment not attached to ring becomes separate RCOOH)); EXCEPTION: tert-butyl benzene (Ph-C(CH3)3) has NO alpha-H → NOT oxidized by KMnO4

reagent_2.conditions: NaOH (aq, hot); then H⁺ workup. PhCCl₃ + 3 NaOH → Ph-C(OH)₃ (orthoformate-like, unstable) → Ph-COOH + 3 HCl/NaCl. Mechanism: SN2 hydrolysis of each C-Cl bond successively → PhC(OH)₃ → benzoic acid. Also: phenyltrichloromethane + H₂O/heat → benzoyl chloride (partial) → benzoic acid (full). Used in ol
reagent_2.note: Geminal trihalide hydrolysis: benzotrichloride (trichloromethylbenzene, Ph-CCl3) + NaOH → benzoic acid (Ph-COOH); GENERAL: R-CX3 (geminal trihalide = all 3 halogens on same carbon) + NaOH/H2O → R-COOH; MECHANISM: R-CX3 + OH- → first substitution: R-CX2(OH) (dihalide hemiorthoester) → R-CX(OH)2 (monohalide) → R-COX (acyl halide; loses HX) → acyl halide + H2O → R-COOH + HX; intermediate is acyl halide R-C(=O)-X; EQUIVALENT: R-CX3 = 3 halogens on same C; hydrolysis goes stepwise by NaOH, each step replaces X with OH; after loss of HX from R-C(OH)2X → R-COX (acyl halide) → further hydrolysis → RCOOH; EXAMPLE: CHCl3 (trichloromethane) = geminal trihalide with H (not R); CHCl3 + NaOH → (under basic conditions forms dichlorocarbene CCl2, NOT carboxylic acid); Ph-CCl3 (benzotrichloride) + NaOH → PhCOOH (benzoic acid); ALTERNATIVE PATH for ArCX3: heating with NaOH gives acyl intermediate → benzoic acid directly

edges.notes: Kolbe-Schmitt reaction; NaOH → Na+ chelates O- and CO2 via 6-membered ring at ORTHO position; major = salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid); minor = 4-hydroxybenzoic acid; salicylic acid has higher bp (intramolecular H-bond, less volatile) | KMnO4 oxidation of alkylbenzene side chains: methylbenzene (toluene, PhCH3) + hot acidic KMnO4 → benzoic acid (Ph-COOH); GENERAL: any alkylbenzene (Ph-R where R = primary or secondary alkyl of ANY length) + hot KMnO4 → Ph-COOH (benzoic acid); the ENTIRE alkyl chain is oxidized away, leaving only ONE COOH carbon directly bonded to the ring; the length of the alkyl chain DOES NOT MATTER — whether R = CH3, C2H5, C10H21, etc., the product is always Ph-COOH; MECHANISM: KMnO4 oxidizes the benzylic C-H (alpha carbon to ring) → this benzylic carbon becomes the COOH; successive oxidations oxidize the chain away; MULTIPLE GROUPS: if the benzene ring has multiple alkyl groups, each becomes a -COOH (e.g., para-dimethylbenzene → terephthalic acid (para-benzenedicarboxylic acid)); SECOND EXAMPLE: Ph-CH(CH3)- (isopropyl: Ph-CH(CH3)2) → Ph-COOH + CH3COOH (both fragments oxidized to acids; however the phenyl C that was attached to the chain → benzoic acid; the detached fragment CH3 → ethanoic acid (actually any chain fragment not attached to ring becomes separate RCOOH)); EXCEPTION: tert-butyl benzene (Ph-C(CH3)3) has NO alpha-H → NOT oxidized by KMnO4 | Geminal trihalide hydrolysis: benzotrichloride (trichloromethylbenzene, Ph-CCl3) + NaOH → benzoic acid (Ph-COOH); GENERAL: R-CX3 (geminal trihalide = all 3 halogens on same carbon) + NaOH/H2O → R-COOH; MECHANISM: R-CX3 + OH- → first substitution: R-CX2(OH) (dihalide hemiorthoester) → R-CX(OH)2 (monohalide) → R-COX (acyl halide; loses HX) → acyl halide + H2O → R-COOH + HX; intermediate is acyl halide R-C(=O)-X; EQUIVALENT: R-CX3 = 3 halogens on same C; hydrolysis goes stepwise by NaOH, each step replaces X with OH; after loss of HX from R-C(OH)2X → R-COX (acyl halide) → further hydrolysis → RCOOH; EXAMPLE: CHCl3 (trichloromethane) = geminal trihalide with H (not R); CHCl3 + NaOH → (under basic conditions forms dichlorocarbene CCl2, NOT carboxylic acid); Ph-CCl3 (benzotrichloride) + NaOH → PhCOOH (benzoic acid); ALTERNATIVE PATH for ArCX3: heating with NaOH gives acyl intermediate → benzoic acid directly

genuine_levers: Kolbe-Schmitt reaction requires phenol (not benzene or alkylbenzene) — NaOH converts phenol to phenoxide, which then reacts with CO2
Counterion matters: Na+ phenoxide gives ortho product (salicylic acid); K+ phenoxide gives para product (4-hydroxybenzoic acid)
Ortho position must be free for the major ortho product; if both ortho positions are blocked, reaction gives para product
common_traps: Confusing Kolbe-Schmitt (phenol + CO2) with KMnO4 oxidation of alkylbenzene side chains (toluene → benzoic acid)
Assuming any substituted benzene can undergo this reaction — only phenols (or highly activated arenes like aniline) react
Forgetting that the carboxylic acid group is introduced at the ortho (or para) position relative to the existing OH group, not at the meta position
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and substrate specificity: phenol vs other benzenes; ortho/para selectivity based on counterion)

edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | Oxidation of primary amine RCH2NH2 with KMnO4 gives RNO2 → RCH2NH2 + KMnO4 → RCHO → RCOOH (over-oxidation path); tertiary primary amine R3CNH2 gives R3C-NO2 | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Kolbe_Schmitt_KOH_gives_para_NOT_ortho → Kolbe-Schmitt with potassium phenoxide (K⁺ counterion) at ~200°C gives para-hydroxybenzoic acid (4-hydroxybenzoic acid), NOT salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_cathode_H2_not_Na_metal → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe cathode h2 not na metal. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_CH4_from_HCOONa_not_possible → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe ch4 from hcoona not possible. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_propanoate_ester_major_not_alkane → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe propanoate ester major not alkane. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gomberg_trityl_radical_stable_from_Kolbe_triphenylacetate → Exception to the standard rule: Gomberg trityl radical stable from kolbe triphenylacetate. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | KMnO4_H_plus_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_aldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: Kmno4 h plus oxidizes primary alcohol to aldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_Schmidt_gives_para_product_always → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe schmidt gives para product always. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | tert_alkylbenzene_oxidized_by_KMnO4_to_benzoic_acid → Exception to the standard rule: Tert alkylbenzene oxidized by kmno4 to benzoic acid. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

edges.conditions: Step 1: RT, air/O₂, radical initiator (or sunlight, or simply air at industrial scale) → cumene hydroperoxide (Ph-C(CH₃)₂-OOH); 80–120°C, autoxidation. Step 2: Dilute H₂SO₄ (dil, 5–10%), RT to 60°C; acid-catalysed cleavage of O-O bond → phenol + acetone. Industrial: cumene (isopropylbenzene) + O₂ → CUMHP → H₂SO₄ → phenol + acetone. Both products valuable.
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: The reaction is the Cumene Hydroperoxide Process for phenol/acetone synthesis — the genuine lever is that the starting benzene must be isopropylbenzene (cumene). Any other substituted benzene will not give this specific ketone (acetone) as a product.
common_traps: Students may think this is a standard electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reaction — it is not. The reagents O₂/H₂SO₄ do not install a ketone directly onto the ring via Friedel-Crafts acylation.
The 'notes' field mentions EAS regiochemistry, but this is a misdirection for this specific edge. The ketone (acetone) is a cleavage product, not a ring substituent.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the Cumene Process as a specific industrial synthesis, distinct from typical aromatic substitution reactions)

edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35).
