Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; Δ. Sulfonation of branched alkanes at tertiary C-H positions: (CH₃)₃CH + H₂SO₄ → (CH₃)₃C-SO₃H (tert-butylsulfonic acid). Not applicable to straight-chain alkanes. Reed reaction (SO₂Cl₂/hv) gives alkanesulfonyl chloride from straight-chain alkanes.
edges.notes: Corrected: to field was wrongly 'substituted_benzene'. Product of alkane + conc H2SO4 is a sulfonate (alkanesulfonic acid), not a substituted benzene. Reaction limited to branched alkanes at tertiary positions. Not a primary JEE transformation.

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: conc. H2SO4 + heat sulfonates tertiary C-H; SO2Cl2/hv (Reed reaction) gives alkanesulfonyl chloride from primary/secondary C-H
Substrate branching: reaction ONLY works for branched alkanes with tertiary C-H; straight-chain alkanes do not react
common_traps: Mistaking product for a substituted benzene (it is an alkanesulfonic acid)
Applying this reaction to straight-chain alkanes (it is not applicable)
Forgetting the heat (Δ) condition
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: tertiary vs primary/secondary C-H; reagent selectivity: H2SO4 vs SO2Cl2/hv)

edges.conditions: α-Elimination of carbanion intermediate → carbene. Example: CHCl3 + t-BuOK → CHCl2⁻ (carbanion) → :CCl2 (dichlorocarbene) + Cl⁻. Dichlorocarbene reacts with alkenes → gem-dichlorocyclopropane (Reimer-Tiemann also proceeds via carbene). Strong base required (t-BuOK preferred over NaOH for carbene generation).
edges.notes: This represents the α-elimination step: carbanion (CHCl2⁻) → carbene (:CCl2) + Cl⁻. Mechanistically correct. The overall sequence is CHCl3 + strong base → carbanion intermediate → carbene. Not a general carbanion reaction — specific to haloform substrates.

genuine_levers: Strong base choice (t-BuOK vs NaOH/KOH) influences carbene yield and reaction efficiency — t-BuOK preferred for steric hindrance and non-nucleophilicity
Substrate must be a haloform (CHX3) to undergo α-elimination — other carbanions do not form carbenes this way
common_traps: Assuming CHCl3 is the direct electrophile in Reimer-Tiemann — the actual electrophile is the generated dichlorocarbene
Predicting ortho substitution in Reimer-Tiemann when both ortho positions are blocked — para product forms instead
Overgeneralizing α-elimination to all carbanions — it is specific to trihalomethyl anions
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: α-elimination vs β-elimination; electrophile identity in Reimer-Tiemann); Structural (substrate requirement: haloform)

edges.exception_description: Reimer_Tiemann_CHCl3_is_NOT_the_electrophile → In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, CHCl₃ is NOT the electrophile; the actual electrophile is dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), generated from CHCl₃ by base (NaOH removes HCl: CHCl₃ + OH⁻ → :CCl₂ + Cl⁻ + H₂O). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | Reimer_Tiemann_gives_para_hydroxybenzaldehyde_as_major_product → Exception to the standard rule: Reimer tiemann gives para hydroxybenzaldehyde as major product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: H₂/Pt (1 atm, RT to 50°C, EtOH or AcOH) or LiAlH₄ (dry ether). Isocyanide is reduced to secondary methylamine: R-NC + 2[H₂] → R-NH-CH₃. The C of isocyanide (originally NC:) picks up 2H atoms → methyl group. Mechanism: H₂ adds to C≡N triple bond. Product is N-methyl secondary amine.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Reduction stops at secondary amine (R-NH-CH3); further reduction to primary amine (R-NH2) is not possible with these reagents
Reagent choice (H2/Pt vs LAH) is a lever for mechanism (catalytic hydrogenation vs hydride transfer) but yields the same product
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: false
common_traps: Misidentifying product as primary amine (R-NH2) instead of secondary methylamine (R-NH-CH3)
Assuming the C≡N triple bond is reduced to a primary amine like a nitrile (R-CN) would be
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the unique reduction outcome of the isocyanide functional group)

edges.conditions: Lossen rearrangement: Step 1: Ac₂O (acetic anhydride) + hydroxamic acid RCONHOH → O-acylated hydroxamic acid RCONHOCOCH₃; Step 2: base (OH⁻, heat) → loss of AcO⁻ → isocyanate R-N=C=O → H₂O → RNH₂ + CO₂. Similar to Hofmann and Curtius. Isocyanate intermediate common to all. Product: primary amine one C shorter.
edges.notes: Rearrangement step: product carbon skeleton differs from substrate. Always verify whether migration occurs before assigning product structure.

genuine_levers: Lossen rearrangement: product is primary amine with one fewer carbon atom than the hydroxamic acid — skeleton changes
Isocyanate intermediate (common to Hofmann, Curtius, Lossen) — hydrolysis yields amine + CO2
common_traps: Forgetting the carbon skeleton change (loss of one carbon) and drawing the amine with the same chain length as the starting acid
Confusing the Lossen with the Beckmann rearrangement (oxime to amide) — different substrate and product
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the rearrangement and the isocyanate intermediate common to multiple amine-forming reactions)

reagents: KOH, then RX, then OH-/H2O, then H+
edges.conditions: Step 1: KOH (or K₂CO₃)/EtOH; phthalimide + KOH → potassium phthalimide (N-K salt). Step 2: DMF or DMSO; R-X (primary only, SN2) + K-phthalimide → N-alkylphthalimide. Step 3: NaOH (aq., reflux) or NH₂NH₂ (hydrazinolysis, EtOH reflux) → primary amine RNH₂ + phthalate. Hydrazinolysis preferred (milder, no saponification of ester). Gives pure primary amine (no over-alkylation).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Primary alkyl halide (RX) required for SN2 step; secondary/tertiary halides give elimination or poor yield
Hydrazinolysis (NH2NH2) vs. basic hydrolysis (OH-/H2O) as final deprotection step — hydrazinolysis is milder and avoids saponification of sensitive esters
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide (RX) is chiral or introduces a chiral center; the SN2 step inverts configuration at the carbon of RX
common_traps: Using secondary/tertiary alkyl halide leads to elimination (alkene) or poor substitution, not the desired amine
Forgetting the final acidic workup (H+) after basic hydrolysis to protonate the amine salt to the free amine
Confusing this with Gabriel synthesis using phthalimide alone without the potassium salt formation step
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: primary RX only; mechanism: SN2 with inversion) and Structural (multi-step sequence awareness)

edges.conditions: Benzidine rearrangement: conc HCl (or dilute H₂SO₄); heat (60–80°C); acid-catalysed [3,3]-sigmatropic-like or radical cation mechanism. Protonation of both N atoms → dication → reorganisation → 4,4'-diaminobiphenyl (major, ~70%) + 2,4'-diaminobiphenyl (semidine, minor). Acid concentration controls product ratio. Benzidine is a carcinogen; IUPAC: 4,4'-diaminobiphenyl.
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: Acid concentration controls product ratio: conc HCl favors 4,4'-diaminobiphenyl (benzidine), dilute acid favors 2,4'-diaminobiphenyl (semidine) — a genuine lever
Existing substituent(s) on the hydrazone aryl ring determine the regiochemistry of the final substituted benzene via EAS directing effects in the rearrangement
common_traps: Assuming the product is always the symmetrical 4,4'-diaminobiphenyl (benzidine) — the semidine product is also possible
Forgetting that the rearrangement is intramolecular and does NOT involve intermolecular coupling of separate molecules
Mistaking this for a simple hydrolysis back to a carbonyl compound (like a reverse Wolff-Kishner)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and product distribution control via acid concentration); Structural (regiochemistry via EAS directing effects of existing substituents)

edges.conditions: Step 1: RT to 80°C; O₂ (air), radical initiator → diphenylmethyl hydroperoxide (Ph₂CH-OOH). Step 2: Dilute H₂SO₄; acid-catalysed O-O homolysis or heterolysis → Ph₂C=O (benzophenone, initially) or PhCHO + PhOH depending on mechanism. Oxidative cleavage at the benzylic C-H position (activated by 2 phenyl groups). Contrast with cumene which gives ketone + phenol cleanly.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

genuine_levers: Radical initiator + O₂ (air) → benzylic hydroperoxide formation; acid-catalysed cleavage of O-O bond determines final product (ketone vs aldehyde + phenol)
Substitution pattern on benzene: activated benzylic position (e.g., diphenylmethane) is required for this oxidative cleavage; simple alkylbenzene (e.g., cumene) gives ketone + phenol cleanly
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: false
common_traps: Confusing this with standard side-chain oxidation (KMnO₄/K₂Cr₂O₇) which gives carboxylic acid; this is a radical/acid-catalysed oxidative cleavage
Assuming the product is always benzaldehyde; the mechanism can yield benzophenone or a mixture depending on the substrate and cleavage pathway
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: radical autoxidation vs ionic oxidation; acid-catalysed O-O cleavage specificity)

edges.conditions: O₂ (air), radical initiator or autoxidation; 80–120°C; then acid workup (dil H₂SO₄). Unsymmetrical diarylmethane: electron-rich p-OMe aryl stabilises radical/cation intermediate → O₂ attack → hydroperoxide → cleavage → p-methoxyphenol + p-nitrobenzaldehyde. Migration: electron-rich Ar (OMe) stays as phenol; electron-poor Ar (NO₂) becomes aldehyde.
edges.notes: Archetype ['III', 'IV'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35).
