reagents: Delta (>=100 degrees C, Cope elimination via N-oxide)
edges.conditions:
COPE/CHUGAEV SYN ELIMINATION STEREOSPECIFICITY — E/Z RATIOS (RM lec 10 p2, pages 2 and 6):

STEREOSPECIFICITY RULE FOR SYN ELIMINATION:
  For syn Cope/Chugaev elimination:
  Erythro diastereomer → Z (cis) alkene = major
  Threo diastereomer → E (trans) alkene = major
  This is because:
  In the syn conformer (H syn to LG = 0° dihedral):
    Erythro: when H is syn to LG, the two Ph groups are cis in the product → Z alkene
    Threo: when H is syn to LG, the two Ph groups are trans in the product
edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.
edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.

genuine_levers: Cope/Chugaev elimination is syn-specific: erythro diastereomer → Z-alkene major; threo diastereomer → E-alkene major
Ester pyrolysis (Ei) gives Hofmann product (less substituted alkene) as major, except in cyclopentane rings where Saytzeff (endocyclic) dominates
Base size changes E2 product: small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff; bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann
Leaving group ability affects E2 selectivity: poor LG (NR₃⁺, F⁻) → Hofmann; good LG (Br⁻, I⁻) → Saytzeff
Resonance-stabilized alkene product overrides Saytzeff/Hofmann rules in E2/Ei
common_traps: Assuming all Ei eliminations follow Hofmann rule (cyclopentane rings give Saytzeff)
Confusing t-BuO⁻ (E2, Hofmann) with t-BuOH (E1, Saytzeff)
Thinking E1 gives a mixture of regioisomers (E1 gives only Saytzeff product)
Believing anti-periplanar geometry is irrelevant in cyclic E2 (in rigid systems, anti-periplanar > Saytzeff)
Mistaking fluoride as a good leaving group in E2 (F⁻ is a poor LG, gives Hofmann selectivity)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry hierarchy: anti-periplanar > resonance > Saytzeff/Hofmann; mechanism discrimination: E1 vs E2 vs Ei)

edges.conditions:
E2 BLOCKED AT EQUATORIAL LG IN CYCLOHEXANE → SN2 INSTEAD (RM lec 10 p2, page 4):

TEACHER Q3 (page 4): '**E2 not possible at eq**' (explicit label)
Substrate: Cyclohexyl NMe₃⁺ (quaternary ammonium) with NMe₃⁺ in EQUATORIAL position + OH⁻/Δ

WHY E2 FAILS:
  E2 mechanism requires: H-Cβ-Cα-LG in ANTI-PERIPLANAR (180°) arrangement
  In cyclohexane CHAIR:
    Anti-periplanar requires LG (axial) and β-H (axial) on adjacent C's
    If LG = EQUATORIAL: there is no anti-axial H (the axial-axial pair is n
edges.chapter: E2 BLOCKED AT EQUATORIAL LG IN
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: E2 blocked for equatorial leaving group in cyclohexane chair → SN2 substitution occurs instead
Reagent choice (aqueous OH⁻) favors SN2 over E2 for this substrate
Leaving group identity (quaternary ammonium) is a Hofmann elimination precursor, but geometry overrides typical regiochemistry
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the SN2 carbon is chiral and the nucleophile attack inverts configuration; substrate is cyclohexyl, so product alcohol is not chiral unless the ring has other substituents
common_traps: Assuming E2 always occurs with strong base/heat — ignoring anti-periplanar geometry requirement
Forgetting that equatorial LG in cyclohexane has no anti-axial β-H, so E2 is impossible
Mistaking aqueous OH⁻ for alcoholic OH⁻ (which would favor E2 if geometry allowed)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: E2 vs SN2 based on substrate stereochemistry)

reagents: Base (to deprotonate NH2 to NH- if needed)
edges.conditions:
INTRAMOLECULAR CYCLIZATION RATES — RING SIZE PREFERENCE (RM lec 11, page 3):

TEACHER 'FUN FACT' (yellow, page 3): H₂N-(CH₂)ₙ-CH₂-Br → intramolecular SN2 → cyclic amine

RATES (teacher's data, directly labeled on structures):
  n=1 → 3-membered ring (aziridine): rate = 7 (circled, yellow)
  n=2 → 4-membered ring (azetidine): rate = 1 (reference, SLOWEST, circled)
  n=3 → 5-membered ring (pyrrolidine): rate not labeled (between 7 and 100, ~50-60 from literature)
  n=4 → 6-membered ring (piperidin
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

genuine_levers: Ring size (n) determines rate and product: n=1 (3-membered) fastest, n=2 (4-membered) slowest, n=3 (5-membered) moderate, n=4 (6-membered) fast
Intramolecular SN2 is irreversible — product is the cyclic amine; intermolecular SN2 with external amine would give a linear diamine
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the alpha carbon to the leaving group is chiral, the SN2 inversion creates a new stereocenter; otherwise, no new stereocenters are formed
common_traps: Assuming 6-membered ring (n=4) is always fastest — for intramolecular SN2, 3-membered ring (n=1) is fastest due to angle strain relief
Forgetting that intramolecular reactions outcompete intermolecular ones at high dilution
Mistaking this for an E2 elimination — requires base to deprotonate NH2, but product is cyclic amine, not alkene
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (kinetic ring-size preference for intramolecular SN2 vs. typical thermodynamic stability order)

edges.conditions: Fe catalyst (or Ni, Cr₂O₃); Δ (trimerisation, ~300°C). Three molecules of C₂H₂ → benzene (Berthelot reaction, 1866). Fe wire or Fe₂O₃ at 300°C. Also: cyclic trimer formation. Not efficient industrially (side products). Low yield; mainly of historical/mechanistic interest.
edges.notes: 3 HC≡CH → benzene; Berthelot reaction; cyclotrimerization of ethyne with Fe catalyst

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice: Fe, Ni, or Cr₂O₃; all give benzene via cyclotrimerization
Requires high temperature (~300°C); lower temperatures do not drive the trimerization
common_traps: Assuming this is a high-yield or practical synthesis (it is low-yield, historical)
Expecting a different aromatic product (only benzene from ethyne under these conditions)
Confusing with other alkyne reactions (e.g., Lindlar's hydrogenation to alkene)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing a specific named reaction and its conditions)

edges.conditions: Dil H⁺ (dil H₂SO₄ or HCl); 150–250°C; aqueous; steam or pressure. Reverse of sulphonation: PhSO₃H + H₂O → Ph-H + H₂SO₄. Reversible reaction (sulphonation equilibrium). Used in synthesis to temporarily block a ring position (sulphonate → direct other EAS → desulphonate). Temperature: 150°C for activated aromatics; 250°C for deactivated.
edges.notes: PhSO3H + dil H+ → C6H6 + SO3; REVERSIBLE sulfonation; used to temporarily block ring positions

genuine_levers: Temperature is a genuine lever: 150°C for activated aromatics, 250°C for deactivated aromatics
Reaction is reversible (sulfonation equilibrium) — high temperature and dilute acid drive the reverse reaction
common_traps: Assuming concentrated acid is used (it's dilute acid)
Forgetting this is the reverse of sulfonation and writing a forward sulfonation mechanism
Using this step as a general deprotection without noting the specific high-temperature, aqueous acidic conditions
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reaction reversibility and temperature control as a synthetic lever)

edges.conditions: EAS (electrophilic aromatic substitution); electrophile E⁺ attacks C-1 position of 2-naphthol (β-naphthol). C-1 is peri position (alpha to OH). β-Naphthol: OH activates ring toward EAS; C-1 (in same ring as OH, alpha to OH) preferred. E⁺ = Br₂ (gives 1-bromo-2-naphthol), NO₂⁺ (gives 1-nitro-2-naphthol), ArN₂⁺ (azo coupling at C-1). Lewis acid may be needed depending on E.
edges.notes: beta-naphthol (2-OH): OH activates SAME ring; EAS at C-1 (ortho to OH on same ring, major); EDG directs to activated ring not the other ring
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

genuine_levers: Electrophile identity (Br₂ vs NO₂⁺ vs ArN₂⁺) determines substitution product (bromo, nitro, azo)
Solvent polarity (CS₂ vs H₂O) controls degree of substitution (mono vs tri)
Presence of Lewis acid catalyst depends on electrophile strength; strong activation by OH often makes it unnecessary
common_traps: Assuming all phenols react with NaHCO₃ (only those with pKa < 6.35 do)
Predicting halogenation requires a Lewis acid catalyst (phenol reacts with Br₂/H₂O without one)
Incorrectly applying ortho/para directing rules to fused ring systems (in β-naphthol, C-1 is major, not C-3)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (exception-based reasoning: acidity trends, catalyst requirements, solvent effects)

edges.conditions: Carbylamine (isocyanide) test: CHCl₃ + KOH (alc.); heat (Δ). Primary amine (PhNH₂) + CHCl₃ + base → isocyanide (PhNC, isocyanobenzene). Mechanism: CHCl₃/base → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene) → adds to NH₂ → N-CHCl₂ intermediate → base deprotonates → elimination of 2 HCl → isocyanide. Characteristic foul smell (carrion-like). Test for primary amines only (1°→positive; 2°, 3°→negative).
edges.notes: Carbylamine reaction (Abnormal Reimer-Tiemann); :CCl2 attacks N of primary amine instead of ring C; mechanism: ArNH2 + :CCl2 → ArNH-CHCl2 → -H+ → ArN-=CHCl2 → -Cl- → ArN=CCl → -Cl- → ArN≡C (isocyanobenzene); foul odor; ONLY primary amines give this test; secondary/tertiary amines do NOT
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | Reimer_Tiemann_CHCl3_is_NOT_the_electrophile → In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, CHCl₃ is NOT the electrophile; the actual electrophile is dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), generated from CHCl₃ by base (NaOH removes HCl: CHCl₃ + OH⁻ → :CCl₂ + Cl⁻ + H₂O). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | Reimer_Tiemann_gives_para_hydroxybenzaldehyde_as_major_product → Exception to the standard rule: Reimer tiemann gives para hydroxybenzaldehyde as major product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para).
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

genuine_levers: Reaction is ONLY for primary amines (1°); secondary/tertiary amines give no reaction — substrate scope is a genuine lever
Base (NaOH/KOH) is essential to generate dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) from CHCl₃; no base → no reaction
common_traps: Confusing this with Reimer-Tiemann reaction (phenol + CHCl₃/OH⁻ → salicylaldehyde) — here the electrophile attacks the N of aniline, not the ring
Thinking any amine (including 2° or 3°) gives a positive test — this is a specific test for 1° amines only
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: primary vs. secondary/tertiary amines; mechanism: carbene formation and attack on N vs. aromatic ring)

edges.conditions: HNO₃/H₂SO₄ (mixed acid) or conc HNO₃ alone; RT or 0–5°C. Anthracene is very reactive in EAS (central ring most reactive; C-9 and C-10 most nucleophilic positions). Nitration very easy — mild conditions sufficient. Regioselective C-9 (meso position). If too harsh → oxidation side reactions. Dilute conditions (cold) → mono-nitration at 9-position.
edges.notes: EAS on anthracene at meso position (C-9 or C-10); attack at C-9 gives Wheland intermediate where BOTH outer rings retain full aromaticity; maximum stabilization; C-9 >> C-1 (alpha) >> C-2 (beta); meso (9,10) positions are most reactive in anthracene EAS

genuine_levers: Regioselectivity: nitration occurs at the meso position (C-9 or C-10) due to maximum stabilization of the Wheland intermediate, not at alpha or beta positions
Condition severity: harsh conditions (high temp/concentration) can lead to oxidation side reactions or poly-nitration; mild, cold conditions favor clean mono-nitration
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: n/a
common_traps: Applying benzene-like ortho/para directing rules to naphthalene/anthracene — the meso position reactivity is unique to fused polycyclic aromatics
Assuming nitration occurs at the most substituted carbon — reactivity is governed by aromatic stabilization, not alkyl substitution
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regioselectivity in fused polycyclic aromatics; condition control to avoid over-reaction)

edges.conditions: Fischer esterification: methanol (excess) + conc H₂SO₄ or HCl (cat.); reflux. COOH → COOCH₃ (not phenolic OH → esterified under these conditions since phenol is poor acyl acceptor via acid catalysis). Product: methyl salicylate (oil of wintergreen). Both acid and phenol groups present but only COOH undergoes Fischer esterification.
edges.notes: Oil of wintergreen; methyl salicylate; main component of IODEX (topical analgesic); COOH esterified (not the phenol OH in this case); 2-hydroxybenzoic acid + MeOH/H+ → methyl 2-hydroxybenzoate (methyl salicylate)
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

genuine_levers: Fischer esterification targets only the carboxylic acid group; the phenolic -OH in salicylic acid does NOT react under these conditions — a different reagent (e.g., acetic anhydride) is needed to esterify the phenol
common_traps: Assuming both -OH groups (phenol and COOH) react with methanol/H+ — only COOH esterifies
Confusing this with aspirin synthesis where the phenol -OH is acetylated and the COOH remains free
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (functional group selectivity within a bifunctional molecule)

edges.conditions: Claisen rearrangement: Δ (heat alone, 200°C); no catalyst. Phenyl allyl ether (prop-2-en-1-yloxy)benzene → [3,3]-sigmatropic → 2-allylcyclohexadienone → tautomerism → 2-allylphenol (2-(prop-2-en-1-yl)phenol). Thermal, pericyclic (concerted). Stereospecific (chair-like TS). No solvent needed; high-boiling solvents possible (DEA, PhCl).
edges.notes: Claisen rearrangement; allyl phenyl ether + heat → [3,3]-sigmatropic concerted rearrangement → cyclohexadienone intermediate (ortho allylated) → 1,5-keto-enol tautomerism → 2-allylphenol; ORTHO product major; pericyclic (no catalyst needed); if BOTH ortho blocked → para product via double [3,3]; if all ortho+para blocked → No Rxn
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰.
