genuine_levers: Choice of solvent: dry THF is required for aryl halides (higher bp, better solvation for ArMgX); Et₂O often insufficient for unreactive ArBr
Halogen identity: Aryl-Br and aryl-I form Grignards; aryl-F does NOT react with Mg under these conditions
Activation: Trace I₂ activates Mg surface; without it, initiation can be slow
common_traps: Assuming aryl-F can form Grignard (it does not)
Using wet solvent leads to immediate hydrolysis: ArMgBr + H₂O → ArH
Forgetting that vinyl halides (CH₂=CH-X) also form Grignards by the same method
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (solvent/halogen selectivity; anhydrous conditions)

reagents: or any protic H+ source (ROH, RCOOH, PhOH, RNH2, R-C≡CH)
edges.conditions: H₂O (or any proton source: ROH, RCOOH, PhOH, R-C≡CH, RNH₂); RT; instantaneous. EtMgCl + H₂O → EtH + Mg(OH)Cl. Grignard is a strong base (pKa of EtH ~50). Any proton source with pKa < ~50 protonates it. Used to: (1) detect active H (measure H₂ evolved); (2) destroy Grignard after reaction; (3) Zerewitinoff method for -OH, -NH count.
edges.notes: Grignard acid-base reaction: ethylmagnesium chloride (EtMgCl) + H₂O → ethane (CH₃CH₃) + Mg(OH)Cl; GENERAL: RMgX + H-A → R-H + AMgX; the Grignard carbanion R⁻ acts as a STRONG BASE (pKa of RH ≈ 45-50 for alkyl; ≈ 43 for vinyl; ≈ 36 for aryl) → deprotonates ANY acid with pKa < 45; COMPREHENSIVE LIST of H-donors that destroy Grignard (all give R-H): (1) RSO₃H (sulfonic acids); (2) RCOOH (carboxylic acids); (3) PhOH (phenol); (4) RSH (thiols); (5) ROH (alcohols); (6) H₂O; (7) D₂O → gives R-D (DEUTERIUM LABELING); (8) T₂O → R-T; (9) R-C≡CH (terminal alkynes, pKa≈25) ✓; (10) NH₃; (11) RNH₂ (primary amines); (12) RNH-R (secondary amines); (13) RCONH₂ (primary amides); (14) RCONHCH₃ (secondary amides); (15) cyclopentadiene (pKa≈16!) ✓; (16) β-diketone (pKa≈9-11) ✓; (17) NC-CH₂-CN (malononitrile); (18) O₂N-CH₂-NO₂ (dinitromethane); (19) CHCl₃ (chloroform); (20) CH(NO₂)₃ (nitroform); (21-22) α-thioethers/dithianes; COUNTING: excess CH₃MgBr + molecule → n × CH₄↑ (n = number of N-H + O-H + S-H + terminal alkyne C-H in the molecule)
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

genuine_levers: Grignard reagent is a strong base (pKa of RH ~45-50) → reacts with ANY protic source (H-A) with pKa < ~50 to give alkane R-H; the specific proton source (H2O, ROH, RCOOH, R-C≡CH, RNH2) does NOT change the product; the lever is the presence of any acidic proton, which destroys the Grignard
common_traps: Mistaking this acid-base reaction for a nucleophilic addition (e.g., thinking Grignard adds to the carbonyl of a carboxylic acid — it does not, it deprotonates the acid first)
Forgetting that Grignard is destroyed by trace water or alcohols in solvents — a common experimental trap
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a strong base, not just a nucleophile, and identifying all potential acidic proton sources)

edges.conditions: D₂O (deuterium oxide, heavy water); RT; anhydrous ether. EtMgCl + D₂O → EtD + Mg(OD)Cl. Incorporation of deuterium label at the Grignard carbon. Used for isotope labelling in mechanistic studies. Quantitative deuteration (one D per Grignard reagent). Same as H₂O quench but with D₂O.
edges.firing_condition: with D2O or other deuterated acid
edges.notes: Grignard deuterium labeling: RMgX + D₂O → R-D + MgX(OD); ethylmagnesium chloride + D₂O → ethane-d₁ (CH₃CH₂D) + Mg(OD)Cl; UTILITY: deuterium labeling is used to: (1) confirm the position of C-Mg in complex molecules (the D appears at the site of the original C-X); (2) count the number of equivalent C-X groups (how many D atoms incorporated = how many Grignard groups); (3) provide NMR-silent labels (D is NMR silent at ¹H NMR, visible at ²H NMR); SELECTIVITY: D₂O inserts D at EVERY C-Mg site → gives one D per RMgX; COMPARISON: H₂O → R-H (protio; no label); D₂O → R-D (deuterium; labeled); T₂O → R-T (tritium; radioactive label); MECHANISM: D-OD (D₂O) acts as acid (pKa of D₂O ≈ 15.7, same as H₂O); the carbanion of RMgX (R⁻) is a strong base → deprotonates D₂O → R-D + OD⁻

The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. genuine_levers: D2O vs. H2O — D2O gives deuterated alkane (R-D) for labelling; H2O gives protonated alkane (R-H)
common_traps: Mistaking this for a nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl — this is a simple acid-base protonation (deuteration) of the carbanion
Forgetting that the Grignard carbon is nucleophilic and reacts with ANY acidic proton source (H2O, ROH, NH3, terminal alkyne) to give alkane
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a strong base for acid-base reactions, not just a nucleophile for carbonyl addition)

edges.conditions: EtMgBr (ethylmagnesium bromide, 1 equiv) + prop-1-yne (propyne, HC≡C-CH₃, terminal alkyne); RT; dry ether or THF. Terminal alkyne C-H (pKa ~25) is more acidic than ethane C-H (pKa ~50) → Grignard base (EtMgBr) deprotonates alkyne → ethane (weaker acid leaves) + propynyl Grignard (RC≡CMgBr). Alkynyl Grignard is nucleophile for carbonyl additions.
edges.notes: Terminal alkyne + Grignard = alkynyl Grignard formation: prop-1-yne (propyne, HC≡C-CH₃, terminal alkyne) + ethylmagnesium bromide (EtMgBr) → prop-1-yn-1-ylmagnesium bromide (propargyl Grignard, CH₃-C≡C-MgBr) + ethane (CH₃CH₃); MECHANISM: the terminal alkyne C-H (pKa ≈ 25) is MORE ACIDIC than ethane C-H (pKa ≈ 50) → the Grignard carbanion (EtMgBr, pKa of EtH ≈ 50) deprotonates the terminal alkyne C-H → alkynyl carbanion (R-C≡C⁻) → stabilized by sp hybridization (high s-character, more electronegative than sp³) → MgBr+ coordinates → alkynyl Grignard (R-C≡C-MgBr); ACID-BASE: pKa terminal alkyne (25) << pKa EtH (50) → reaction is thermodynamically favorable; WHY SP IS MORE ACIDIC: sp orbital has 50% s-character vs sp² (33%) vs sp³ (25%); more s-character = electrons held closer to nucleus = more stable carbanion = lower pKa = more acidic; UTILITY: alkynyl Grignard can then add to carbonyl compounds → propargyl alcohols

genuine_levers: Terminal alkyne C-H (pKa ~25) is acidic enough to be deprotonated by Grignard reagent (RMgX) to form alkynyl Grignard; internal alkynes (no terminal H) do NOT undergo this reaction — substrate acidity is the lever
common_traps: Confusing this acid-base reaction with the typical nucleophilic addition of Grignard to carbonyls — here, Grignard acts as a base, not a nucleophile
Thinking the product is an addition product (e.g., R-MgX adding across the triple bond) instead of a substitution (H replaced by MgBr)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard reagent can act as a base with sufficiently acidic substrates, not just as a nucleophile)

reagents: 1-bromoethane (primary alkyl halide, R'-X where R' = primary)
edges.conditions: Primary alkyl halide (1-bromoethane, EtBr); dry ether; RT. Coupling (Wurtz-type via cuprate or direct if conditions right) or: PrMgBr acts as base toward primary RX? More likely: cross-coupling via organocuprate. PrMgBr + EtBr → does NOT couple cleanly (Grignard + alkyl halide gives poor yield); use Gilman reagent (PrCuLi or (Pr)₂CuLi) + EtBr → butane. Or: PrMgBr + H₂O → PrH (propane, not butane). So butane requires coupling of Pr (3C) + Et (1C) fragments = 4C alkane. Gilman reagent route.
edges.notes: Grignard SN2 coupling: propylmagnesium bromide (PrMgBr) + 1-bromoethane (EtBr, 1° alkyl halide) → butane (PrEt = CH3CH2CH2CH2CH3) + MgBrX; GENERAL: RMgX + R'-X (SN2) → R-R' + MgX₂ (new C-C bond formed; this is the WURTZ-type coupling via Grignard); REQUIREMENT: R'-X must be PRIMARY (1°) alkyl halide for SN2 to work; 2° = sluggish, competing elimination; 3° = NO SN2 (elimination E2 dominates instead); REACTIVE R'-X TYPES: primary aliphatic ✓ (methyl, ethyl, propyl, etc.); DOES NOT WORK: 3° alkyl halides, vinyl halides (sp₂ cannot do SN2), aryl halides (cannot do SN2); USE GILMAN (R₂CuLi) for vinyl and aryl halides instead; MECHANISM: RMgX acts as R⁻ nucleophile; attacks the back of the 1° R'-X carbon in an SN2 displacement; Mg-X end becomes MgX₂; NOTE: the Grignard R group is the nucleophile; R'-X provides the electrophilic carbon

genuine_levers: Primary alkyl halide (1°) required for SN2 coupling; secondary/tertiary halides give elimination
Grignard reagent acts as a strong base toward acidic protons (e.g., H₂O) to give alkane via protonation, not coupling
For efficient cross-coupling to form a new C–C bond, a Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) is typically used instead of Grignard + alkyl halide
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: false
common_traps: Assuming Grignard + alkyl halide always gives clean SN2 coupling (yield is poor; Gilman route is better)
Confusing this with Wurtz reaction (which gives symmetrical alkane from two identical alkyl halides)
Mistaking the product as an alcohol (from carbonyl addition) instead of an alkane (from coupling or protonation)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing Grignard as nucleophile vs. base, and knowing Gilman reagent for cross-coupling)

edges.conditions: Br₂ (molecular bromine, 1 equiv); anhydrous ether; 0°C to RT. PhMgBr + Br₂ → PhBr + MgBr₂. Electrophilic halogenation of organometal: R-MgX + X₂ → R-X + MgX₂. Product: bromobenzene (re-synthesised from phenylmagnesium bromide). Used in isotope labelling (BrMgPh + Br₂→ Br⁸¹-Ph or C-D/C-¹³C labelled bromo compounds).
edges.notes: Grignard + halogen → alkyl/aryl halide: PhMgBr (phenylmagnesium bromide) + Br₂ → PhBr (bromobenzene) + MgBr₂; GENERAL: RMgX + X₂ → R-X + MgX₂ (where X₂ = Cl₂, Br₂, or I₂); the Grignard R group is halogenated; R-Mg bond is replaced by R-X bond; UTILITY: this is used to INTRODUCE A HALOGEN at the position where the Grignard was (useful for complex structures where direct halogenation would not be regioselective); EXAMPLE: cannot halogenate arene at a specific position by EAS when other positions are equally reactive → make Grignard at that C → + X₂ → halide at that specific C; LIMITING REAGENT: use RMgX as LR (limiting reagent) to avoid over-halogenation; with excess X₂, only monohalogenation at the Grignard carbon; HALOGEN SCOPE: Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ all work; F₂ not commonly used (too reactive); REACTION WITH I₂ IS PARTICULARLY USEFUL for making aryl iodides (ArMgBr + I₂ → ArI)
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

genuine_levers: Grignard reagent acts as nucleophile in SN2-like displacement on molecular halogen (Br2) — the R group from Grignard becomes halogenated
Reaction is general for Cl2, Br2, I2 — choice of halogen determines product R-X
This is a method to introduce a halogen at the position of the Grignard carbon, useful for labelled compounds or when direct halogenation is not feasible
common_traps: Mistaking this for electrophilic aromatic bromination (Br2/FeBr3) — here the aromatic ring is already part of the Grignard reagent
Confusing with Grignard addition to carbonyls — this is a halogenation, not an addition
Assuming the Grignard carbon retains stereochemistry if chiral — the SN2-like step at the Mg-bonded carbon likely inverts configuration, but the product aryl halide is planar (sp2)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a nucleophilic carbanion equivalent for halogenation, not just for carbonyl addition)

edges.conditions: Mg; dry ether; RT. 1,2-Dichloroethane + Mg → not Grignard (vicinal dihalide eliminates). Mg inserts → organometal → β-elimination of MgClBr → ethene (ClCH₂CH₂Cl + Mg → MgClBr + CH₂=CH₂). Dehalogenation: vicinal dihalides + Mg (or Zn) → alkene (elimination). Industrial: 1,2-dichloroethane → ethylene via Zn/EtOH or Mg/Et₂O elimination.
edges.notes: 1,2-dichloroethane (Cl-CH₂-CH₂-Cl, n=2 dihaloalkane) + Mg/ether → ethene (CH₂=CH₂) + MgCl₂; NOT a diGrignard; MECHANISM: Mg inserts into one C-Cl → Cl-CH₂-CH₂-MgCl forms transiently → but the β-MgCl group induces intramolecular E2 β-elimination (β-haloalkyl Grignard: the halogen at β position and the MgCl act together → synperiplanar elimination) → CH₂=CH₂ + MgCl₂; GENERAL RULE for X-(CH₂)n-X + Mg: n=1 → carbene (:CX₂ or :CH₂ after hydrolysis); n=2 → alkene (β-elimination); n=3 → cyclopropane (intramolecular SN2 ring closure); n≥4 (n≠1,2,3) → diGrignard XMg-(CH₂)n-MgX; KEY DISTINCTION: 1,2-dihaloalkane gives ALKENE (not diGrignard); 1,3-dihaloalkane gives CYCLOPROPANE (not diGrignard or alkene)

The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Mg/dry ether with vicinal dihalide gives alkene via dehalogenation, NOT a Grignard reagent (which would require a non-vicinal alkyl halide).
Substrate specificity: Only vicinal dihalides (X-CH2-CH2-X) undergo this elimination; geminal dihalides or isolated halides give different products.
common_traps: Mistaking this for Grignard formation — vicinal dihalides with Mg eliminate to alkene, they do not form di-Grignard reagents.
Assuming the mechanism is a simple E2 — it proceeds via Mg insertion into one C-X bond followed by intramolecular β-elimination of MgX2.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role: Mg as reducing/eliminating agent vs. Grignard-forming agent)

edges.conditions: Mg (or Zn/Cu couple) + dry ether; RT. CH₂I₂ + Mg → ICH₂MgI (iodomethylmagnesium iodide, Simmons-Smith reagent). Alternatively: CH₂I₂ + Zn(Cu) → IZnCH₂I (Simmons-Smith reagent directly). On reaction with alkene → cyclopropane (stereospecific syn addition, :CH₂ equivalent via carbenoid). No free carbene — concerted [1+2] cycloaddition from carbenoid.
edges.notes: Diiodomethane (CH₂I₂, geminal dihaloalkane, n=1) + Mg/ether → :CH₂ (methylene carbene) + MgI₂ or MgI(I); geminal dihaloalkane (both X on same carbon) + Mg → CARBENE (:CRR'); MECHANISM: Mg inserts into C-X → X-CH₂-MgX (monoGrignard transiently) → the MgX eliminates the second X from the same carbon (alpha-elimination) → :CH₂ + MgX₂; CARBENE :CH₂: is a highly reactive intermediate (neutral carbon with only 2 bonds, 2 non-bonding electrons: one in each orbital, singlet or triplet); REACTIONS OF :CH₂: (1) inserts into C-H bonds; (2) adds to alkenes (syn) → cyclopropane; SIMMONS-SMITH: CH₂I₂ + Zn(Cu) → IZnCH₂I (carbenoid) → adds to alkene → cyclopropane (stereospecific syn); GENERAL: gem-dihalo (n=1) + Mg → :CR₂ (carbene, where R = substituents on the gem-dihalo carbon)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Mg/dry ether generates a carbene (:CH₂) from geminal dihalide; Zn(Cu) (Simmons-Smith) generates a carbenoid for cyclopropanation with alkenes
Substrate: Must be a geminal dihalide (both halogens on the same carbon) for alpha-elimination to carbene; vicinal dihalides give alkynes via double elimination
common_traps: Mistaking the product as a free, stable carbene; it is a reactive intermediate or carbenoid that immediately reacts (e.g., with alkenes to form cyclopropanes)
Confusing this with standard Grignard formation (RMgX from alkyl halide); here, the Grignard-like intermediate undergoes alpha-elimination
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing geminal dihalide + Mg as a carbene source, not a standard Grignard reagent)

edges.conditions: 2 equiv Li metal; dry ether (or diethyl ether/hexane); –78°C to 0°C; strictly inert atmosphere (Ar). EtCl + 2 Li → EtLi + LiCl. Organolithiums more reactive than Grignards (stronger C-Li bond, harder Lewis acid). Use 2 Li (one for C-Li bond, one for LiCl). Organolithiums react with ether slowly → use quickly or at –78°C. EtLi used to make cuprates (EtLi + CuI → Et₂CuLi).
edges.notes: Organolithium prep: chloroethane (ethyl chloride, EtCl) + 2Li/dry ether → ethyllithium (EtLi) + LiCl; GENERAL: R-X + 2Li → RLi + LiX; NOTE: requires 2 equivalents of Li (unlike Grignard which requires only 1 Mg); MECHANISM: (1) Li → Li⁺ + e⁻ (first Li oxidized); (2) e⁻ attacks R-X → R• + X⁻; (3) R• + second Li → RLi (R⁻ Li⁺; the second Li provides the electron and coordinates); PRODUCT: [R:⁻ Li⁺] (boxed; organolithium is MORE IONIC than Grignard; Li is more electropositive than Mg → C-Li bond is more polar → RLi is a better carbanion source than RMgX); REACTIVITY ORDER: RNa > RK > RLi > RMgX > RZnX (left = most reactive/basic); ADVANTAGES over Grignard: RLi can deprotonate more acidic C-H bonds; can react with substrates that Grignard cannot; DISADVANTAGE: RLi is more pyrophoric (catches fire in air) → harder to handle; SCOPE: primary, secondary, aryl, vinyl halides all form RLi; fluorides generally do not form RLi under standard conditions

This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

genuine_levers: Requires 2 equivalents of Li metal (unlike Grignard requiring 1 Mg) — one for electron transfer, one for product formation
Strictly inert atmosphere (Ar) and dry ether are essential — organolithiums are pyrophoric and react with moisture/air
Organolithiums are more reactive than Grignards (stronger C-Li bond, harder Lewis acid) — useful for making cuprates (EtLi + CuI → Et₂CuLi)
common_traps: Using only 1 equivalent of Li (like Grignard) is incorrect — mechanism requires 2 Li atoms
Confusing this with Grignard formation (R-X + Mg → RMgX) — reagents and stoichiometry differ
Ignoring the inert atmosphere — leads to decomposition (RLi + H₂O → RH + LiOH)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stoichiometry and conditions: 2 Li vs 1 Mg, inert atmosphere, dry ether)

edges.conditions: Step 1: cyclopentyl iodide + 2 Li → cyclopentyllithium + LiI; dry ether; –78°C; inert atmosphere. Step 2: cyclopentyllithium + CuI (0.5 equiv) → (cyclopentyl)₂CuLi·LiI (Gilman reagent). Gilman reagent used for 1,4-conjugate addition (Michael acceptors) or coupling with primary alkyl halides/acid chlorides (no 1,2-addition to ketones). More selective than RMgX or RLi.
edges.notes: Gilman reagent prep: cyclopentyl iodide + (1)2Li → cyclopentyllithium (CpLi); CpLi + (2)CuI (0.5 eq) → dicyclopentylcuprate lithium ((Cp)₂CuLi) + LiI; GENERAL: RX + 2Li → RLi → RLi + 0.5 CuI → R₂CuLi + LiI; (OR: 2 RLi + CuI → R₂CuLi + LiI); GILMAN REAGENT (R₂CuLi) = ORGANOCUPRATE; PROPERTIES: (1) LESS REACTIVE than RLi or RMgX (C-Cu bond is less polar than C-Li or C-Mg → weaker carbanion); (2) Only ONE R group is transferred as nucleophile (the other R stays on Cu; only R⁻ attacks the electrophile); (3) the unused R is wasted (hence need R₂CuLi, not RCuLi, for 1 equivalent of R transfer); REACTS WITH: acyl halides (R'-C(=O)-Cl → R'-C(=O)-R = ketone ✓); primary alkyl halides (SN2 ✓); vinyl halides (stereospecific ✓); aryl halides ✓; DOES NOT REACT WITH: ketones ×, esters ×, nitriles ×, carboxylic acids × (too unreactive for C=O of these; only highly reactive acyl chlorides work)

genuine_levers: Step 1: Alkyl halide + 2 Li → organolithium (RLi) — lithium-halogen exchange (not Grignard formation)
Step 2: 2 RLi + CuI → Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) — stoichiometry (0.5 eq CuI) is critical; 1 eq CuI would give different copper species
Gilman reagent selectivity: 1,4-addition to α,β-unsaturated carbonyls (not 1,2-addition like RLi/RMgX); coupling with primary alkyl halides/acid chlorides (no reaction with tertiary alkyl halides)
difficulty_notes.ez_propagates: conditional — if the alkyl halide is derived from an alkene, the E/Z geometry is lost during organolithium formation; however, if the organolithium/Gilman reagent is used in a subsequent stereospecific coupling, the product stereochemistry may be controlled by the substrate geometry in that step, not by this preparation step
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the preparation step itself does not create stereocenters; however, if the starting alkyl halide is chiral, the organolithium/Gilman reagent may retain configuration (with possible inversion/retention depending on mechanism) or racemize
common_traps: Confusing Gilman reagent preparation with Grignard reagent preparation (Mg vs Li; ether solvent vs dry ether/inert atmosphere)
Assuming Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) reacts like RLi/RMgX (e.g., adding 1,2 to ketones — it does NOT)
Forgetting the stoichiometry: 2 RLi + 0.5 CuI (or 1 RLi + 0.25 CuI per equivalent) to form R₂CuLi; incorrect ratios yield different copper complexes
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: RLi vs RMgX vs R₂CuLi; stoichiometry precision; application selectivity — 1,4 vs 1,2 addition)

edges.conditions: Gilman reagent (diethylcuprate, Et₂CuLi); dry ether or THF; –78°C to 0°C. Et₂CuLi + (Z)-1-bromobut-1-ene → SN2 coupling with retention? Vinyl halides normally unreactive toward SN2, but organocuprates couple efficiently via oxidative addition/reductive elimination at Cu. Stereospecific (retention of alkene geometry from vinyl halide). Product: (Z)-hex-3-ene with retention.
edges.notes: Gilman reagent + vinyl halide → stereospecific alkene: (Z)-1-bromobut-1-ene (a cis vinyl bromide with stereochemistry around C=C) + diethylcuprate lithium (Et₂CuLi) → (Z)-hex-3-ene (cis product; configuration retained); GENERAL: R₂CuLi + vinyl-X → vinyl-R (new alkene with C=C CONFIGURATION RETAINED); STEREOCHEMICAL RULE: the coupling of R₂CuLi with vinyl halides is STEREOSPECIFIC with RETENTION of the C=C geometry; (Z)-vinyl-X → (Z)-product; (E)-vinyl-X → (E)-product; WHY RETENTION: the mechanism goes through a transition state that preserves the geometry of the C=C (likely syn addition/oxidative addition at Cu preserving stereochemistry); contrast: SN2 would require back-attack at sp₂ carbon which is geometrically impossible → cannot invert; Gilman goes with retention (front-face pathway); SCOPE: also works with aryl halides (Ar-X + R₂CuLi → Ar-R); CANNOT BE DONE WITH GRIGNARD: RMgX does NOT undergo SN2 with vinyl/aryl halides → use Gilman for vinyl and aryl coupling; REFERENCE: Paula Y. Bruice textbook topic

Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

genuine_levers: Gilman reagent (R2CuLi) couples with vinyl halides via oxidative addition/reductive elimination at Cu, giving stereospecific retention of alkene geometry — Grignard reagents (RMgX) do NOT react with vinyl halides under normal conditions
Reaction is stereospecific: (Z)-vinyl halide gives (Z)-alkene; (E)-vinyl halide gives (E)-alkene
common_traps: Mistaking Gilman reagent for Grignard reagent — Grignard reagents do NOT form from or react with vinyl halides under standard conditions
Assuming SN2 mechanism — vinyl halides are unreactive toward standard SN2; this is an organocuprate-specific oxidative addition/reductive elimination
Forgetting stereospecificity — the E/Z geometry of the vinyl halide is retained in the product alkene
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (organocuprate vs. Grignard selectivity; stereospecificity of vinyl coupling)

edges.conditions: MnO₂ (activated, freshly prepared); CH₂Cl₂; RT. MnO₂ selectively oxidises activated (allylic and benzylic) primary and secondary alcohols to aldehydes and ketones respectively, without affecting isolated C=C or other functional groups. Very mild and selective. Alternatively: PCC (but slightly less selective than MnO₂ for benzylic). Swern also works.
edges.notes: MnO₂ selective oxidation: benzyl alcohol (phenylmethanol, Ph-CH₂-OH, benzylic primary alcohol) + MnO₂ → benzaldehyde (PhCHO); SELECTIVITY RULE: MnO₂ ONLY oxidizes ALLYLIC and BENZYLIC alcohols (alcohols adjacent to C=C or aromatic ring); does NOT oxidize simple aliphatic alcohols (neither primary nor secondary); EXAMPLES: PhCH₂OH (benzyl alcohol, 1°) → PhCHO ✓; Ph-CH(OH)-CH₃ (1-phenylethanol, benzylic 2°) → Ph-CO-CH₃ ✓; allylic CH₂=CH-CH₂OH (prop-2-en-1-ol) → CH₂=CH-CHO ✓; Ph-C(OH)(CH₃)₂ (dimethylphenylcarbinol, tertiary benzylic) → NO REACTION ×; simple CH₃CH₂OH → NO REACTION ×; REASON FOR SELECTIVITY: MnO₂ reacts via a radical mechanism; the benzylic/allylic C-H is weakest (lowest BDE due to resonance stabilization of the radical intermediate) → MnO₂ can abstract this H; aliphatic C-H is too strong; SYNTHETIC USE: oxidize benzyl/allyl alcohol to aldehyde in presence of aliphatic alcohol groups without affecting aliphatic ones
edges.exception_description: Aldehydes oxidised by KMnO₄ → PCC oxidises 1° alcohol to aldehyde and stops there; KMnO₄ goes all the way to carboxylic acid | PCC_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_carboxylic_acid_like_Jones → Exception to the standard rule: Pcc oxidizes primary alcohol to carboxylic acid like jones. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | MnO2_oxidizes_all_alcohols_like_PCC → Exception to the standard rule: Mno2 oxidizes all alcohols like pcc. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Choosing wrong oxidant for target oxidation level

genuine_levers: MnO2 oxidizes ONLY allylic and benzylic alcohols (primary → aldehyde, secondary → ketone); simple aliphatic alcohols are inert
Reagent selectivity: MnO2 is milder than PCC and does NOT oxidize isolated alkenes or other sensitive groups
common_traps: Assuming MnO2 oxidizes all primary alcohols (it does NOT oxidize simple aliphatic alcohols like ethanol)
Confusing MnO2 with PCC (PCC oxidizes all primary alcohols to aldehydes, MnO2 is selective for allylic/benzylic)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: MnO2 vs PCC vs KMnO4 for benzylic/allylic oxidation)

reagents: (1) LiAlH4 (lithium aluminium hydride) in dry diethyl ether
edges.conditions: LiAlH₄; dry ether (Et₂O) or THF; 0°C to reflux; anhydrous; excess (2 equiv per ester). Ester → 2 alcohols (the acyl part → primary alcohol, the alkoxy part → ROH released). Here: ethyl propanoate → propan-1-ol (from acyl C) + ethanol (from ethoxy). The question asks for propan-1-ol: LiAlH₄ reduces ester completely → primary alcohol from the acyl C.
edges.notes: LAH reduction of ester → TWO alcohols: ethyl propanoate (CH₃CH₂COOC₂H₅, ester) + (1)LiAlH₄ → (2)H₂O → propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH, 1° alcohol from the acyl R group) + ethanol (C₂H₅OH, 1° alcohol from the OR' group); GENERAL: R-C(=O)-OR' + LiAlH₄ → R-CH₂-OH + R'-OH (TWO primary alcohols!); MECHANISM: H⁻ attacks the electrophilic C of the C=O of ester → tetrahedral intermediate → OR'⁻ (alkoxide) leaves (the OR' is a good enough leaving group in presence of Al Lewis acid) → R-CHO (aldehyde) released; excess LiAlH₄ immediately reduces R-CHO → R-CH₂-OH; the released R'O⁻ → H₂O → R'-OH; KEY: both the acyl portion (R-) and the alkoxy portion (-OR') become PRIMARY ALCOHOLS; the ester C=O becomes -CH₂OH (2 H atoms added at this C); with LiAlD₄: → R-CD₂-OH + R'-OH (D at the acyl carbon position); PRACTICAL: ester + LAH → always gives 2 alcohols; CONTRAST ESTER HYDROLYSIS: ester + NaOH → RCOO⁻ + R'-OH (only 1 alcohol + carboxylate); LAH gives BOTH as alcohols

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces ester to TWO primary alcohols (acyl part → R-CH2-OH, alkoxy part → R'-OH); NaBH4 does NOT reduce esters — reagent choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Forgetting the second alcohol product (the alkoxy part R'-OH) is a common omission
Mistaking LiAlH4 reduction for NaBH4 reduction (NaBH4 reduces aldehydes/ketones but NOT esters)
Writing carboxylic acid as product (LiAlH4 reduces ester beyond aldehyde stage directly to alcohol)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs NaBH4; product count)

edges.conditions: LiAlH₄; dry ether or THF; 0°C to reflux; anhydrous; excess. Amide reduction: RCONH₂ → RCH₂NH₂ (C=O → CH₂, same N). Mechanism: H⁻ delivery to amide C=O twice → amino alcohol intermediate → → primary amine. NaBH₄ does NOT reduce amides. LiAlH₄ reduces all carbonyls: amide, ester, acid (all → alcohol or amine products).
edges.notes: LAH reduction of amide → amine: propanamide (CH₃CH₂CONH₂, primary amide) + LiAlH₄/ether → propan-1-amine (CH₃CH₂CH₂NH₂, primary amine); GENERAL (all amide types): primary amide (R-CO-NH₂) → primary amine (R-CH₂-NH₂); secondary amide (R-CO-NH-R') → secondary amine (R-CH₂-NH-R'); tertiary amide (R-CO-N(R')(R'')) → tertiary amine (R-CH₂-N(R')(R'')); KEY: C=O becomes CH₂ (oxygen is replaced by 2H; oxygen leaves); the N retains all its substituents; the nitrogen substitution TYPE is preserved (1°→1°, 2°→2°, 3°→3° amine); MECHANISM: H⁻ attacks the C of C=O of amide → tetrahedral intermediate → O⁻ (alkoxide-like) → with Al coordinating O and N → cyclic intermediate collapses → iminium ion (R-CH=N) → another H⁻ attacks the iminium C → amine (R-CH₂-N); the oxygen ends up as OAlH₂ → H₂O workup → Al(OH)₃; EXAMPLE: N-benzoyl piperidine (Ph-CO-N(CH₂)₅ ring) + LAH → N-benzyl piperidine (Ph-CH₂-N(CH₂)₅ ring; the C=O becomes -CH₂-; N stays as tertiary amine in ring); DISTINCTION from oxime/imine reduction: amide reduction goes C(=O)-N → CH₂-N (N stays same type); imine reduction: C=N-H → CH₂-NH (N becomes NH₂ if H on N)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces amides to amines; NaBH4 does NOT reduce amides — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Amide type determines amine type: primary amide (RCONH2) → primary amine (RCH2NH2); secondary amide (RCONHR') → secondary amine (RCH2NHR'); tertiary amide (RCONR'R'') → tertiary amine (RCH2NR'R'')
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the resulting amine carbon (former carbonyl) becomes a stereocenter; requires that the R group and the new CH2NH2 group are different, and the other three substituents on nitrogen are distinct (rare for primary amines from primary amides)
common_traps: NaBH4 is incorrectly used for amide reduction — it only reduces aldehydes/ketones
Writing carboxylic acid as product — LiAlH4 reduces amide C=O to CH2, not OH
Mechanism involves tetrahedral intermediates and elimination of oxygen as oxide, not a simple nucleophilic addition like with aldehydes
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs NaBH4 for carbonyl reduction); Structural (amide substitution pattern determines amine type)

edges.conditions: SeO₂ (selenium dioxide); dioxane or glacial AcOH; reflux. Riley oxidation: Ph₂CH₂ (benzylic CH₂) → Ph₂CO (benzophenone, diphenylmethanone). Mechanism: SeO₂ ene reaction at α-C of the activated benzylic CH₂ → intermediate → → ketone. Also: CrO₂Cl₂ (Étard), MnO₂/H₂SO₄ for benzylic oxidation.
edges.notes: SeO₂ oxidation of benzylic CH₂ → ketone: diphenylmethane (Ph-CH₂-Ph) + SeO₂ → diphenylmethanone (benzophenone, Ph-CO-Ph); GENERAL: benzylic CH₂ (Ph-CH₂-R) + SeO₂ → Ph-CO-R (ketone); the CH₂ between two phenyl groups is particularly reactive (both phenyl groups are electron-withdrawing at the sp₃ carbon → the α-C-H is activated); also allylic CH₂ (C=C-CH₂-) + SeO₂ → C=C-C=O (conjugated enone) or C=C-CHOH (allylic alcohol); MECHANISM: ene reaction type; SeO₂ acts as an electrophile toward the allylic/benzylic C-H via a concerted 6-membered TS (ene mechanism) → seleno intermediate → [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement → selenious acid ester → O transferred to the former C-H position → C=O

genuine_levers: SeO2 oxidises benzylic CH2 to C=O (ketone); allylic CH2 to conjugated enone — substrate type (benzylic vs allylic) is a lever
SeO2 is selective for activated CH2 groups (benzylic, allylic); does NOT oxidise isolated aliphatic CH2 groups — substrate activation is a genuine lever
common_traps: SeO2 oxidation is NOT for aromatic ring cleavage (that's KMnO4/heat) — product is ketone, not carboxylic acid
SeO2 oxidises CH2 to C=O; if the benzylic carbon is CH3 (e.g., toluene), the product is aldehyde (not ketone) — oxidation stops at aldehyde for benzylic CH3 groups
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: SeO2 for activated benzylic/allylic positions vs other oxidants for aliphatic or over-oxidation)

edges.conditions: SeO₂; dioxane or EtOH-H₂O; reflux. Allylic oxidation (Riley): SeO₂ oxidises allylic C-H in cyclohexene → cyclohex-2-enol (intermediate) → cyclohex-2-en-1-one. Ene reaction mechanism: SeO₂ + alkene → [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement → allylic seleninic acid → → enone. Regioselective allylic oxidation adjacent to C=C.
edges.notes: SeO₂ oxidation of cycloalkene → enone: cyclohex-1-ene + SeO₂ → cyclohex-2-en-1-one (2-cyclohexen-1-one); RULE 3: In cycloalkenes, the ring -CH₂- at the α-position to the double bond is oxidized to a KETONIC GROUP (C=O); the product is an enone (conjugated α,β-unsaturated ketone); RULE 4: If the cycloalkene is substituted on the double bond, the -CH₂- at α-position to the MORE SUBSTITUTED end of the double bond is oxidized → gives allylic alcohol product (R-cyclohexene + SeO₂ → allylic-OH at C adjacent to the R-substituted vinyl carbon); RULE 2 (allylic oxidation): for acyclic alkenes, SeO₂ oxidizes the -CH₂- or -CH₃ α to the MOST SUBSTITUTED end of C=C; preference order: -CH₂- > -CH₃ > -CH= (methylene > methyl > vinyl C); EXAMPLE: 2-methylbut-2-ene (CH₃-CH=C(CH₃)₂) + SeO₂ → allylic alcohol at the more-substituted end (the C with 2 CH₃ groups); one of the CH₃ → CH₂OH (allylic hydroxylation); if both CH₂ and CH₃ are present at same end: CH₂ reacts preferentially

genuine_levers: Regioselectivity: SeO2 oxidises the allylic -CH2- adjacent to the double bond; for substituted cycloalkenes, oxidation occurs at the α-position to the MORE SUBSTITUTED end of the double bond
Product is an α,β-unsaturated ketone (enone), NOT an aldehyde or carboxylic acid — this is a specific allylic oxidation
common_traps: SeO2 is NOT a typical oxidant for converting alcohols to carbonyls (like PCC) — it specifically targets allylic C-H bonds
The intermediate allylic alcohol (e.g., cyclohex-2-enol) is not the final product; it is further oxidised to the enone
Mechanism is an ene reaction and [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, NOT a simple carbocation or radical pathway
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (specificity of SeO2 for allylic oxidation and enone formation; regiochemistry in substituted cases)

edges.conditions: Nitrogen inversion: thermal energy (kT); no reagent needed; unimolecular; RT (ultrafast, ~2×10¹¹ s⁻¹). Trialkylamine nitrogen undergoes pyramidal inversion (umbrella flipping) via planar sp² TS (lone pair in p orbital). Energy barrier ~6 kcal/mol (very low) → extremely rapid → enantiomers interconvert at RT → racemisation. Cannot isolate chiral trialkylamine enantiomers at RT. Contrast: phosphines (higher barrier ~35 kcal/mol → can be chiral, configurationally stable).
edges.notes: Nitrogen inversion (umbrella flip) of trialkylamine: N(sp3) pyramidal ground state → [planar sp2 N at TS] → inverted N(sp3); MECHANISM: N is sp3 (pyramidal), lone pair occupies one tetrahedral position; thermal energy drives N through a planar TS (sp2-like, lone pair in unhybridised p orbital); the three substituents pass through the same plane as N → the N is momentarily planar → then settles on the other side (lone pair now opposite) → configuration inverted; ENERGY BARRIER (ΔG#): for trimethylamine [N(CH3)3] = 7.9 kcal/mol at room temp; barriers typically 6-10 kcal/mol for simple acyclic amines → inversion is RAPID at 25°C (NMR time scale cannot resolve two conformers → apparent single species); RATE: simple amines invert ~1000 times/second at 25°C; STERIC ACCELERATION RULE: bulky substituents at N (e.g., iPr, iBu, tBu) create steric repulsion in the pyramidal GS; at the planar TS the substituents splay apart → steric strain RELIEVED → lower ΔG# → FASTER inversion; EXAMPLE: tri-isobutylamine [N(CH2CHMe2)3] inverts FASTER than trimethylamine [N(CH3)3] despite bulkier substituents; EXAMPLE: N-tBu-aziridine inverts faster than N-nBu-aziridine because t-Bu groups are compressed in pyramidal GS of the 3-membered ring

genuine_levers: Barrier height: Nitrogen inversion barrier is ~6 kcal/mol (very low) → enantiomers interconvert at RT → racemisation. Contrast phosphines (~35 kcal/mol) → configurationally stable at RT → can be chiral.
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting amine is a chiral trialkylamine (N with three different alkyl groups and a lone pair). The step itself does not create a stereocenter; it inverts an existing one.
common_traps: Assuming chiral trialkylamines can be resolved at RT — they cannot due to rapid inversion.
Confusing nitrogen inversion with pyramidal inversion of other atoms (e.g., phosphorus).
Thinking a reagent is required — this is a unimolecular thermal process.
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (contrasting nitrogen vs. phosphorus inversion barriers and configurational stability)

edges.conditions: Thermal (kT); slow at RT; ring strain INCREASES inversion barrier. Aziridine N inversion barrier ~18–26 kcal/mol (much higher than trialkylamine ~6 kcal/mol) because ring constrains bond angles → planar sp² N in ring is very strained (ring would have to be flat in TS, requiring bond angle distortion). Therefore: aziridines are configurationally stable on chemical timescales — N-methylaziridine can exist as distinct cis/trans stereoisomers.
edges.notes: Nitrogen inversion in aziridine (3-membered N ring): 1-methylaziridine (N-methylaziridine) has ΔG# = 20.5 kcal/mol for nitrogen inversion (cf. 7.9 kcal/mol for trimethylamine); REASON: in the 3-membered ring, the C-N-C angle is constrained to ~60° (ring angle); at the planar TS, the N would need to be sp2 with C-N-C approaching 120°; but the ring PREVENTS the angle from opening to 120° → RING CANNOT ACCOMMODATE planar N TS → extremely high ring strain at TS → very high ΔG#; CONSEQUENCE: aziridine N chirality is PERSISTENT on the NMR time scale (unlike acyclic amines); N-substituted aziridines can be isolated as separate R and S isomers at N if the two C-substituents are also different; COMPARISON: N-methylaziridine (ΔG# = 20.5) >> N-methylpyrrolidine (ΔG# = 7.0) >> trimethylamine (ΔG# = 7.9 kcal/mol); 5-membered ring (pyrrolidine): ring is flexible enough to accommodate planar N TS → barrier similar to acyclic; STERIC ACCELERATION IN AZIRIDINES: if the N-substituent is t-Bu (bulky), the steric repulsion between t-Bu and the ring CH2 groups in the pyramidal GS is large → relief at planar TS → LOWER ΔG# for N-tBu-aziridine vs N-nBu-aziridine

genuine_levers: Ring strain (3-membered) dramatically increases inversion barrier vs. acyclic amines — this is the key lever that makes aziridine stereochemistry stable
Substituent size on nitrogen — bulky groups can further increase the inversion barrier, but the ring strain is the primary factor
common_traps: Assuming nitrogen inversion is always fast (like in acyclic amines) and that aziridines cannot have stable stereoisomers
Confusing this with epoxide ring-opening stereochemistry — this step is about configurational stability of the nitrogen itself, not a reaction of the ring
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding how ring strain alters fundamental physical organic properties — barrier to inversion)

edges.conditions: Electronic orbital interaction; no reagent; inherent electronic structure. Trivinylamine: three vinyl (CH₂=CH-) groups on N → lone pair on N conjugates (backbonding) into the π* of all three C=C bonds → N becomes sp² (planar) rather than sp³ (pyramidal). The nitrogen lone pair is delocalised into conjugated vinyl groups → mesomeric effect → flat geometry. Compare: aniline (PhNH₂): partially sp² but not fully flat. Trivinylamine essentially planar N.
edges.notes: Electronic effect on nitrogen inversion: backbonding (N lone pair → adjacent pi system) → flattening of N; EXAMPLE: trivinylamine [N(CH=CH2)3]: the N lone pair is delocalized into the three vinyl C=C pi* orbital systems (allylic-type conjugation: N-p orbital conjugates with C=C pi) → the N acquires partial sp2 character → the pyramidal angle flattens toward planar; CONSEQUENCE: the 'inversion barrier' in the traditional sense decreases (N is already near planar) or the nature of the 'barrier' changes; GENERAL: any N directly bonded to a pi system (vinyl, aryl, C=O) will have partial sp2 character: amide N (R-CO-NR2): nearly planar (C-N bond has partial double bond character; restricted rotation, not inversion); aniline N (Ph-NH2): slightly pyramidal but much flatter than aliphatic amine; allylamine: intermediate; KEY RULE: the more electron-withdrawing and better pi acceptor the substituent, the more planar the N becomes; COMPARISON: N(CH=CH2)3 (ΔG# effectively decreases relative to Me3N due to flattening) vs N(CH3)3 (ΔG# = 7.9 kcal/mol); for cyclic systems with vinyl substituents at N, the combination of ring constraint + backbonding can give unusual inversion profiles
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para).
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

genuine_levers: Presence of adjacent conjugated π-system (e.g., vinyl, phenyl) for backbonding → determines degree of N inversion barrier lowering and planarity
Number of conjugated substituents on N (e.g., three vinyl groups in trivinylamine) → influences extent of sp² character and planarity
common_traps: Assuming all amines are sp³ pyramidal — conjugated amines (aniline, trivinylamine) have partial sp² character
Overlooking that backbonding (N lone pair → π*) flattens geometry without a formal reagent
Confusing this electronic effect with steric effects (e.g., SIP/SIR) that also influence basicity/geometry
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (electronic structure effects on geometry and basicity without a classical reagent)

edges.conditions: Constitutional isomers: same molecular formula C₅H₁₀, same carbon skeleton, different position of C=C double bond. Pent-1-ene (terminal) vs pent-2-ene (internal). Not interconvertible at RT (double bond rotation restricted; to interconvert must break/re-form π bond, ~60 kcal/mol). Acid-catalysed isomerisation (H⁺/heat): pent-1-ene → pent-2-ene (thermodynamic, more-substituted alkene more stable by ~1 kcal/mol per additional alkyl substituent at C=C).
edges.notes: Position isomers (positional isomers): same molecular formula + same carbon skeleton + same functional group type; differ in the LOCANT (position) of the functional group or multiple bond on the chain; DEFINITION: the FG or multiple bond is placed at different carbons of the SAME skeleton; EXAMPLES: (1) pent-1-ene (C=C between C1 and C2) vs pent-2-ene (C=C between C2 and C3) — same 5C chain, different double bond position; (2) but-1-yne vs but-2-yne (same 4C chain, triple bond position differs); (3) hexan-1-ol vs hexan-2-ol vs hexan-3-ol (same 6C chain, OH at different positions); (4) cyclohex-1,3-diene vs cyclohex-1,4-diene (same ring, double bonds at different positions); (5) 1,2-dimethylcyclohexane vs 1,3 vs 1,4 — positional isomers of dimethylcyclohexane; AROMATIC POSITIONAL ISOMERS: o- (1,2-), m- (1,3-), p- (1,4-) disubstituted benzene are positional isomers; DISTINCTION from chain isomers: position isomers have the SAME carbon skeleton; chain isomers have DIFFERENT skeletons

genuine_levers: Acid-catalysed isomerisation (H⁺/heat) drives terminal alkene (pent-1-ene) to internal alkene (pent-2-ene) for thermodynamic stability
common_traps: Assuming pent-1-ene and pent-2-ene are interconvertible at room temperature (requires breaking π bond)
Confusing constitutional isomerism with stereoisomerism (E/Z)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (thermodynamic control of alkene stability)

edges.conditions: Enumeration of constitutional isomers of C₃H₅Cl₃ (trichloropropane): systematic approach. Propane backbone: (1) 1,1,1-trichloropropane (CCl₃CH₂CH₃); (2) 1,1,2-trichloropropane (CCl₂ClCHClCH₃? i.e., CHCl₂-CHCl-CH₃); (3) 1,1,3-trichloropropane; (4) 1,2,2-trichloropropane; (5) 1,2,3-trichloropropane; (6) 1,3,3-? Actually: propane has C1, C2, C3. Place 3 Cl: (1,1,1); (1,1,2); (1,1,3); (1,2,2); (1,2,3); (2,2,2) — but 2,2,2 doesn't exist (only 2C at C2 of propane). So 5 structural isomers exist.
edges.notes: Counting structural isomers of C3H5Cl3 (trichloropropane): propane has 3 C atoms (C1, C2, C3); C1 and C3 are equivalent (both terminal); need to place 3 Cl atoms on 3 carbons (with repetition allowed); SYSTEMATIC ENUMERATION: (1) 1,1,1-trichloropropane: Cl3C-CH2-CH3 (all three Cl on C1); (2) 1,1,2-trichloropropane: Cl2CH-CHCl-CH3 (2 Cl on C1, 1 on C2); (3) 1,1,3-trichloropropane: Cl2CH-CH2-CH2Cl (2 on C1, 1 on C3); (4) 1,2,2-trichloropropane: ClCH2-CCl2-CH3 (1 on C1, 2 on C2); (5) 1,2,3-trichloropropane: ClCH2-CHCl-CH2Cl (1 on each C) → TOTAL = 5 structural isomers; GENERAL METHOD: list all unique ways to place n identical substituents on m non-equivalent carbons; avoid duplicates by using symmetry (C1 = C3 in propane by symmetry); EXTENSION for C3H5Cl2Br (2 Cl + 1 Br on propane): since Cl and Br are DIFFERENT, each position of Br on the backbone gives additional isomers when combined with Cl placements → total = 9 structural isomers (all positional combinations of 2 Cl + 1 Br on propane skeleton, considering Cl and Br are distinct)

genuine_levers: Systematic enumeration of constitutional isomers based on the propane backbone and distinct placement of three chlorine atoms
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the generated constitutional isomer has a carbon atom with four different substituents (e.g., 1,2,3-trichloropropane is achiral; stereocenters are not inherently created by the enumeration process itself)
common_traps: Miscounting isomers by failing to recognize symmetry (e.g., counting 1,1,3 and 3,3,1 as different, or incorrectly proposing 2,2,2-trichloropropane)
Confusing constitutional isomerism with stereoisomerism (e.g., considering enantiomers of a chiral structure as separate constitutional isomers)
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (systematic counting of constitutional isomers)

edges.conditions: Benzene substitution counting: C₆H₄XY (two different substituents X and Y). Three positional isomers: ortho (1,2-; X and Y adjacent, 60° apart), meta (1,3-; two carbons apart, 120°), para (1,4-; opposite, 180°). Each is a distinct compound with different physical properties (bp, mp, dipole moment). E.g., dichlorobenzene: o-DCB (bp 180°C), m-DCB (bp 173°C), p-DCB (bp 174°C, highest mp 53°C due to centrosymmetric packing).
edges.notes: Positional isomers of disubstituted benzene (two DIFFERENT substituents X and Y): EXACTLY 3 positional isomers: (1) 1,2-XY (ortho, o-): substituents on adjacent carbons; (2) 1,3-XY (meta, m-): substituents separated by one carbon; (3) 1,4-XY (para, p-): substituents on opposite carbons; EXAMPLE: chlorobromobenzene (Cl + Br on benzene): o-chlorobromobenzene / m-chlorobromobenzene / p-chlorobromobenzene = 3 position isomers; SAME SUBSTITUENTS (X = Y, e.g., dimethylbenzene = xylene): also 3 position isomers: o-xylene (1,2), m-xylene (1,3), p-xylene (1,4); SPECIAL CASE (Ex7): 2,6-dimethylbromobenzene vs 1-methyl-2,6-dibromobenzene — the 2,6-pattern on a symmetric ring: 1-Br-2,6-di(Me) has a plane of symmetry through the 1-position → the two Me groups at 2 and 6 are EQUIVALENT (mirror images through the C1 axis) → this compound is a SINGLE compound (not two isomers); labeled 'Identical or Homomer' on the board; TRISUBSTITUTED BENZENE: 3 groups on benzene → more positional isomers depending on whether groups are same or different

genuine_levers: The identity of the two substituents (X and Y) determines the physical properties (bp, mp, dipole) of the three positional isomers
The relative directing effects of X and Y (ortho/para vs meta directors) determine the major product in electrophilic substitution synthesis, but this edge is about isomerism, not synthesis
common_traps: Incorrectly stating there are 4 or more isomers for C6H4XY (e.g., counting 1,5- as distinct from 1,3-)
Confusing this with the synthesis question: 'How many isomers?' is distinct from 'How to make a specific isomer?'
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (isomer counting and property correlation)

reagents: require bond breaking (pi bond cleavage ~65 kcal/mol)
edges.conditions: NOT interconvertible at RT: C=C π-bond rotation barrier ~65 kcal/mol (requires breaking π bond). To interconvert: (a) acid catalyst (H⁺/Br⁻): add then eliminate — scrambles geometry; (b) radical conditions; (c) photoisomerisation (hν). Catalytic isomerisation: H₂SO₄ → protonation → free rotation of σ-bond → trans product (thermodynamic). Or NO₂/hν radical → cis→trans. Without catalyst: cis and trans are distinct isomers, stable at RT.
